Description: Adze made of copper, bone, hide.;
Find spot: Found/Acquired: Victoria Island (Canada);  Found/Acquired: Prince of Wales Straits
Curators Comments: Collected by Robert McClure, HMS Investigator, Prince of Wales Strait (according to mss registration annotation); Parker Snow 1858 .p 29: '289. Hatchets of the newly discovered Esquimaux. They are shaped like an adze; from Victoria Land; by Captain Collinson, C.B.' Register: '328. Adze. flat copper blade 7 in. long, 2 in. wide, fixed with thongs at the end of a curved bone handle 11 3/4 inches long.' The printed information is then corrected in manuscript: '328 & 329 are from Prince of Wales Strait, & were brought by Capt. (Sir Robt.) McClure H.M.S.Investigator'. Jenness 1946 pp. 102-204 discusses this adze type and illustrates four examples. Sometime the blade is lashed directly to the shaft, at other times it is wedged into a separate head. He thinks that Hearne's 1771 comments about adzes refer to chisels, and says that by the 20th.  century almost every adze was equipped with an iron blade.

Description: Small carving, an amulet (?), made from a piece of soft grey stone, possibly argillite, in the form a marine/water creature, probably a salmon, carved in a rough ovoid, with formline details. The hooked mouth and gills are incised as are the ventral fins, the dorsal fin being very slight; the tail, carved with a face, points downards, acting as a platform for a small bear-like creature situated under the belly of the fish with the head touching the ventral fins. The amulet is inlaid with haliotis, four rectangular pieces along the body of the fish, one of the pieces missing; the eyes and ventral fins of the fish are also inlaid, as were once the eyes of the face on the tail. The dorsal fin is pierced with a hole for suspension, a short length of skin thong remaining. The whole item shows great wear and age.;
Curators Comments: The registration slip provides no additional information except for a note by Erna Gunther: 'Northwest Coast Amulet EG 4-17-52'. The carving of the bear figure suggests that this might be Haida, but the argillaceous shale of which it is made may also be from some other part of the coast. New description by JCK 5/7/96.

Description: Amulet, made from a piece of antler from a young deer, curved, and with three small tines, elegantly carved in the form of a killer whale[?], one tine acting as as the dorsal fin, two further tines as the tail. The creature is not carved with a mouth as such, but is carved with a low relief head, set in a rectangle, at the tip, in classic Tlingit style, this is followed by two large eyes, over a partial mouth and flipper on the underside with, two flippers just in front of the dorsal fin. The tip of the nose is pierced for suspension, has a piece commercial[?] string through it, with also a fragment of cotton shirt [?]. The whole artefact is dark brown, well patinated and clearly of age when collected. Attached is a printed pre-acquisition label: '154'.;

Object type: amulet (object name); pendant (object name); dagger (object name)
Description: Carving of a flattened curved piece of bone or antler, in the form of a sea creature, perhaps a killer whale. All the details are executed in Northwest Coast style, particular with a series of ovoids and u-forms for the head, including eyes, snout, and lip corner, with two further large ovoids on the body, and fins between the head and body. On the back of the creature is the reclining figure of a human-like mammal, perhaps with a forehead masquette, the hands drawn up under the chin, and the feet placed on other side of what may be the blow hole, drilled through the artefact for a cord. The mouth is excavated on one side only, for an inlay now entirely missing, perhaps once of ivory?, suggesting that it may be an amulet or pendant designed to be worn with the inlay facing out from the wearer. Four ovoids on both sides are inlaid with oval pieces of California haliotis, some of these pierced for suspension, suggesting that they come from re-used earlier possibly California artefacts. The eyes of the humanoid figure may also once have been inlaid. The end of the artefact is however ambiguous, and seems to have been attached to or articulated with an additional part, broken off and missing since before accession. This is indicated by a copper pin which runs through the artefact attaching a further piece of wood, bone or antler. An alternative purpose may therefore have been a dagger handle/hilt.;
Materials: bone (?); antler (?); copper; wood (?); haliotis shell

Description: Amulet or pendant made of the tip of a bear tooth or walrus tusk, carved in the form of a four legged creature, with a mammalian head, carefully carved in Northwest Coast style with a large snout, long mouth, and circular eyes raised above a deeply carved area. The four legs are carved with feet indicated, the forelegs with incised lines indicating digits, and the body decorated with long striations. The mouth, nostrils, feet and striations are heavily filled with red pigment. The tip of the tooth forms the long pointed tail of the creature. Two vertical holes are carved through the torso of the creature for suspension cords.;

Description: Girl's navel amulet or charm; sewn of buckskin; in the form of diamond-shaped animal? with two tassels coming out each side, loop at head end, two tassels at opposite end; covered with pink glass beads and white, green and red stepped square pattern.;
Dimensions: 36.50 centimetres including loop and tassels ;  25.00 centimetres including tassels ;  3.00 centimetres;
Curators Comments: Among the historical traditions of Plains People (Lakota, Blackfoot, or Ojibwe), a family member would make a special bag for a newborn baby often depicting a turtle or lizard – animals with protective qualities. When the baby’s umbilical cord fell off, parents place it in the bag and sew it shut. This object stayed with the child through his or her life, taking on many roles. It might have been the baby’s first toy or an ornament for his or her bed. Years later, it might have been worn as a necklace or charm. In these various roles, umbilical cord bags offered protection. They symbolised the connection to one’s mother and represented the transition from before life to birth.; on display in Many Tumors: Standing Alone
Bib references: Paolozzi 1985 / Lost Magic Kingdoms and Six Paper Moons from Nahuatl

Description: Spondylus bivalve shell, unworked.;
Curators Comments:
Found in the guano deposits on Macabi Island.
Vila Llonch 2013
Seashells and land snail shells were highly valued throughout South and Central America. Traders carried a wide range from the Caribbean and Pacific coasts to highland communities. Conch shells were used for body ornaments and musical instruments, while some shells were crushed and ingested as part of ritual practices, and others were offered to the gods. The fact that shells were also cast in gold shows their importance and value to the ancient Colombian people.

Description: Feathered arm bustles. The largest disc has fuzzy and stiff white feathers.   Part of an ensemble of powwow regalia.;

Description: Decorated arrow made of bamboo, wood, reed, cotton, feather.;
Materials: bamboo; wood; reed; cotton; feather (curassow or guan)
Curators Comments:
After the rubber boom in the early 1900s, the Nawa people were thought to be extinct. Once used as weapons, arrows such as this are now wielded as symbols of territorial auto-demarcation. The designs on the arrowhead represent serpents: the boa constrictor shapes the course of the smaller streams, whereas the anaconda delineates the large rivers, such as the Juruá, a main tributary of the Amazon and the Nawa’s ancestral home.
Bamboo arrow with a carved and curved bamboo tip, tied with black and red-dyed cotton threads and golden glass threads, and decorated with black paint and red and black graphisms. The black paint appears to be a mixture of chokanary with charcoal (grated and sifted) and/or beeswax, which makes it very resistant, according to the traditional Nawa production technique. As chief Railson Carneiro Nawa explains, chokanary plant is used to make black, red and purple dyes and is also used to decorate pottery and envira bark skirts. The arrowhead is richly carved and presents red graphisms over red and black paints composed of triangles and a sequence of concentric diamonds, which is the most common type of design in the region, representing the boa constrictor for the Nawa people. According to Nawa cosmology, the boa constrictor inhabits and traces the course of the smaller streams (igarapés), whereas the anaconda traces the course of the rivers. The fletching shows black-painted decoration and has one feather cut in half attached to each side of the arrow's end by a thin thread forming a bundle at the arrow's nock.
Indigenous anthropologist Ykaruni Nawa developed the documentation of the Juruá River collection in consultation with leaders Railson Carneiro Nawa and Lucila Nawa as part of the project 'Relating to the territory: Remapping Amazonian collections through co-curation and participatory documentation' in collaboration with the Santo Domingo Centre of Excellence for Latin American Research (SDCELAR) at the British Museum.
Read an interview where he shares the importance of this project: https://www.sdcelarbritishmuseum.org/news/from-amazonia-to-the-world-retelling-the-nawa-story/; Register addition "Jurua River".

Simpson, who was present for some time during the Modoc War as artist/correspondent for the London Illustrated News writes:  '...Before I left the Modoc Country I managed to get one of their Arrows.  The head is of obsidian which is plentiful in the locality. At Yreka a gentleman presented me with a second Arrow with an iron head. I have sent them both with the bow [Am1959,+.114].  The Arrows are made from a reed, but a piece of wood is joined in a wonderful neat way to the end.  Obsidian was the usual material for heads till the diggers came, and then the Indians were much given to stealing shovels to make iron heads.  The Gentleman who gave me the second Arrow says it belonged to Scaur-faced Charlie, one of the [heroes?] at present in the Lava Beds.  I was also told at Yreka that the Glue or Gum used for putting the sinew on the back of the Bow is made from some part of a Salmon.  The two Arrows are said to be poisoned. The poison is made by keeping a Coyote's liver till it becomes putrid, and then a rattlesnake is made to bite it and impregnate the mass with his venom, and in this they are dipped...'

Description: Decorated arrow made of bamboo, wood, reed, cotton, feather.;
Materials: bamboo; wood; reed; cotton; feather (curassow or guan)
Curators Comments:
After the rubber boom in the early 1900s, the Nawa people were thought to be extinct. Once used as weapons, arrows such as this are now wielded as symbols of territorial auto-demarcation. The designs on the arrowhead represent serpents: the boa constrictor shapes the course of the smaller streams, whereas the anaconda delineates the large rivers, such as the Juruá, a main tributary of the Amazon and the Nawa’s ancestral home.
Bamboo arrow with a carved and curved bamboo tip, tied with black and red-dyed cotton threads and golden glass threads, and decorated with black paint and red and black graphisms. The black paint appears to be a mixture of chokanary with charcoal (grated and sifted) and/or beeswax, which makes it very resistant, according to the traditional Nawa production technique. As chief Railson Carneiro Nawa explains, chokanary plant is used to make black, red and purple dyes and is also used to decorate pottery and envira bark skirts. The arrowhead is richly carved and presents red graphisms over red and black paints composed of triangles and a sequence of concentric diamonds, which is the most common type of design in the region, representing the boa constrictor for the Nawa people. According to Nawa cosmology, the boa constrictor inhabits and traces the course of the smaller streams (igarapés), whereas the anaconda traces the course of the rivers. The fletching shows black-painted decoration and has one feather cut in half attached to each side of the arrow's end by a thin thread forming a bundle at the arrow's nock.
Indigenous anthropologist Ykaruni Nawa developed the documentation of the Juruá River collection in consultation with leaders Railson Carneiro Nawa and Lucila Nawa as part of the project 'Relating to the territory: Remapping Amazonian collections through co-curation and participatory documentation' in collaboration with the Santo Domingo Centre of Excellence for Latin American Research (SDCELAR) at the British Museum.
Read an interview where he shares the importance of this project: https://www.sdcelarbritishmuseum.org/news/from-amazonia-to-the-world-retelling-the-nawa-story/; Register addition "Jurua River".
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 4, Relating to animals)
Acq name (acq): Purchased from: J H A Meech (price for the collection £35)

Description: Arrow straightener made of ivory;
Curators Comments: see JCH King `First Peoples, First Contacts' British Museum Press 1999; McEwan 2009, p.74 Walrus Ivory Arrow Straightener. North Alaskan eskimo, USA, AD 1850 Durable walrus ivory had been used to fashion this arrow-straightener in the form of foetal caribou. It is used by wedging the end of the arrow shaft inside the diamond-shaped cavity to gain leverage and apply pressure. Around the aperture, walruses cavort in the water, one of which is being harpooned. An array of other detailed hunting scenes is inscribed into the surface of every facet of the tool. In one, a herd of swimming caribou distinguished by their huge antlers is pursued by a hunter with upraised spear in a canoe towards a figure standing on the shore. Another scene shows a row of tents marking the site of a seasonal camp beside which a band of eskimo with linked hands are engaged in a collective dance. A further encampment features game hanging from drying racks with a hunter left loosing off an arrow at a skein of migrating geese flying overhead.

Description: Atlatl or spear-thrower made of wood, with a loop of white shell lashed on with cotton thread.;
Curators Comments: Christy Additions,1868-1877,p.118,no.1 "Presented by AW Franks,January 1,1870; from the Klemm Collection".; McEwan 2009, p.59 Ceremonial wooden 'atlatl' (spear-thrower). Aztec, Mexico, AD 1300-1521 Among the arsenal of Aztec weapons were obsidian-bladed swords and an array of clubs, bows and arrows and spears. Perhaps the most prestigious was the spear thrower known by its Nahuatl name atlatl and used to hurl lethal darts. This fine example still preserves traces of paper-thin hammered gold foil that was applied over both the front and back, and the beautifully worked supine body of a warrior carved along its length. It was probably intended for display and may have been part of a suite of ceremonial regalia proclaiming the role and status of the 'warrior king'. The warrior thrusts a spear with a pointed tip in front of him; just above the spear head, the head and two-forked tongue of a rattlesnake can be seen. The snake imagery refers to the ability of the owner to strike at a distance and was likened to the deadly lunge of the rattlesnake.
Bib references: McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail; McEwan 2009a / Moctezuma. Aztec Ruler

Description: Wedge-shaped celt or axe blade made of copper.;
Culture: Hopewell; Middle Woodland Period; Late Woodland Period
Curators Comments:
Found with S.551 three feet (approx. 1 metre) below the upper surface of the mound. As deposition followed construction of the mound dating can not be precise.  Also extracted from the mound were a large number of roughly cut, overlapping mica laminae. These were found covering a  prepared, crescent shaped ground level clay surface.  

Squier and Davis describe mound 7 as follows:
"Fig. 41 (reproduced in Fig. 20) is a section of mound number 7 in 'Mound City.' This mound is much the largest within the enclosure, measuring seventeen and a half feet in height by ninety feet base. From its top a full view of the entire group is commanded. A shaft nine feet square was sunk from the apex. The outer layer of gravel, which in this case
was twenty inches thick, was found to be broken up, and at the depth of three feet (at a point indicated by a in the section) were found two copper axes, weighing respectively two, and two and one-fourth pounds. At the depth of seven feet occurred the first sand stratum, below which, at intervals of little more than a foot, were three more, -four in all. At the depth of nineteen feet was found a smooth level floor of clay, slightly burned, which was covered with a thin layer of sand an inch in thickness. This sand had a marked ferruginous appearance, and
seemed to be cemented together, breaking up into large fragments a foot or two square. At one side of the shaft, and resting on the sand, was noticed a layer of silvery mica, as shown in the plan of the excavation, Fig. 42 (reproduced as Fig.21). It was formed of round sheets, ten inches or a foot in diameter, overlapping each other like the scales of a fish.Lateral  excavations  were made to determine its extent, with the result indicated in the plan. The portion un-covered exhibited something over one-half of a large and regular crescent, the outer edge of which rested on an elevation or ridge of sand six inches in height, as shown in the supplementary section o. The entire length of the crescent from horn to horn couldnot have been less than twenty feet, and its greatest width five. The clay floor of this mound was but a few inches in thickness; a small shaft, c. was sunk three feet below it, but it disclosed only a mass of coarse ferruginous sand. The earth composing the mound was incredibly compact, rendering excavation exceedingly slow and laborious. Two active men were employed more than a week in making the excavation here indicated.  It is not absolutely certain that the mound was raised over the simple deposit above mentioned, and it may yet be subjected to a more rigid investigation.
"Although this mound is classed as a mound of sacrifice, it presents some features peculiar to itself. Were we to yield to the temptation to speculation which the presence of the mica crescent holds out, we might conclude that the mound-builders worshiped the moon, and that this mound was dedicated, with unknown rites and ceremonies, to that luminary. It may be remarked that some of the mica sheets were of that peculiar variety known as 'hieroglyphic' or 'graphic mica.' "

Object type: regalia (object name); back-ornament (object name); medallion (object name)
Description: Lower back bustle and bustle medallion.  The bustle is constructed of sticks wrapped in tape and coloured string, with feathers and orange strips of synthetic material streaming at the ends.  The sticks are threaded together with cord.  A wooden block is used to attach the bustle to the body, with holes to receive the cord (shoelace) which will be used to tie the bustle to the body.  The wooden block also gets concealed by a bustle medallion, which is constructed of two layers of cardboard discs, which are decorated and layered, the smaller on the larger; and attached with shoelaces threaded through the two disc layers.  The smallest top layer disc is constructed beaded hide with a rhinestone in the centre; the beads are white, blue, yellow, orange and red.  The larger disc is constructed of a three-layer cardboard backing with maroon feathers mounted on the front. Part of an ensemble of powwow regalia.;

Object type: back-ornament (object name); mosaic (object name); human remains (object name)
Title: The Turquoise Mosaics
Description: Human skull covered with turquoise and lignite mosaic held in place with pine resin adhesive. The nasal cavity is lined with red thorny oyster shell (Spondylus princeps). The eyes are made of polished pyrite surrounded by white conch (Strombus) shell. There is a lining made of deerskin and maguey (Agave) fibre on the interior and long deerskin straps which were originally painted red (traces of the iron rich ochre pigment remain). The skull is believed to represent Tezcatlipoca, or Smoking Mirror, and was probably worn as a back ornament.;
Materials: human bone; turquoise; conch shell; pyrite; deer skin; lignite; pine; agave
Curators Comments:
Tezcatlipoca is often depicted with obsidian mirrors at the head and is conventionally cast as an adversary to Quetzalcoatl.

Acq notes (acq):
Lot 1836, Catalogue of B.Hertz Collection.
For details on Christie's purchase of Am,St.399-401, see King et al. 2012. pp.  183-188
This item is part of group of three turquoises (sacrificial Knife Am St. 399), (Mask Am St.400), (Human skull Am St.401) acquired by the dealer and collector Bram Hertz (b. c. 1794) and subsequently bequeathed to the Museum by Henry Christy (1810-65). An account of the provenance of these objects is given in a letter from Hertz, who settled in London in the 1830s in a letter dated  ‘5 February 1858’ but (Dept of Asia, WWII archive) more probably written in 1859 at a time when Christy was contemplating their purchase at a forthcoming sale.  The letter is reproduced in E. Carmichael, Turquoise Mosaics from Mexico (London, British Museum, 1970) p. 37.
Hertz published a catalogue of his collection (Hertz, 1851) – possibly the first appearance of the mosaics in print since the 17th century – and in this (Hertz, 1851: iii) reference is made to the collection having been acquired “within the last twenty years”.  
The three turquoise items (mask, knife and skull) also appear in the catalogue of Hertz’s first Sotheby’s sale (Sotheby & Wilkinson, 1854) which took place in May 1854.  In an annotated sale catalogue in the British Library the group of three mosaics is shown as having been acquired by what appears to be a Mr Roussey or Roussell for 14 guineas (the handwriting is difficult to read).   It may be that some of the objects were bought in or were acquired by nominees buying on behalf of Joseph Mayer (1803-86) and a consortium of Liverpool businessmen (Gibson, 1988: 11).  The turquoises were still in Hertz’s possession in July 1856 when he wrote to Mayer offering his collection for sale, including “extremely rare” “specimens of Mexican, Chinese and Indian workmanship.”  The deal was concluded by 15 January 1857 when removal arrangements were discussed (Liverpool Record Office). 
It has been suggested that Mayer may have over-reached himself financially, at all events  , the three mosaics appeared in the Hertz/Mayer 1859 sale (Sotheby & Wilkinson, 1859: 125-6), were bought by Henry Christy 
Since it was not displayed at the Royal Asiatic Society on 28 January 1843, it can be inferred that Hertz acquired the human skull after the beginning of 1843.  He states in his 1859 letter to Christy:
The skull was in a Collection at Bruges which was sold about 12 years ago, as also a wig which was described as a scalp.  On the scull [sic] there is a material which is called obsidian, and which is found in great abundance in Mexico...   (Carmichael, 1970: 37).
It is possible that Hertz could be referring to a private sale, but there is only one public auction listed in Art Sales Catalogues Online which took place in Bruges in the 1840s and which contained antiquities and miscellaneous objects.    This was the immense collection of the rich landowner and aristocrat Joseph van Heurne (1752-1844), built up over half a century, auctioned at Bruges in October 1844.  Van Heurne had exhibited his collection in his residence at Bruges.   The sale catalogue, which was distributed locally and in Berlin, Cologne, Paris and London, lists almost 2,000 items: 288 paintings, 198 drawings, 535 prints, 69 sculptures, 54 porcelains, 179 natural history objects, and 603 ivories, enamels, antiquities and miscellaneous objects
Listed in the catalogue, amongst 84 “Objets divers” is:
483. Un Vanitas d’une composition vraiment extraordinaire, consistant en une caisse sur la porte de laquelle on a représenté la portrait d’une jeune femme; à l’intérieur se trouve un crane sur la face duquel on a appliqué des pierreries en mosaïque, les yeux sont en marcassite.  A l’intérieur de la porte il y ‘a 8 vers latins relatifs au sujet.  On croit que cette pièce date du milieu du l6eme siècle (Vandervin, 1844: 106).
It seems very likely that this is the BM’s human skull.  The date and place are right, given that Hertz’s letter is rather vague.   If this is indeed the BM’s skull one puzzle might be solved.   In Hertz’s 1858 letter to Christy and in both the 1854 and 1859 sales catalogues the skull was, for no obvious reason, said to be accompanied by a wig or scalp.  It would make sense if this were part of a Vanitas ensemble   Vanitas painting, with its focal point of a human skull, was particularly popular in Northern Europe (Flanders and the Netherlands) in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.  An assemblage of actual objects such as this is, however, unusual.
We do not know whether Hertz was present at the van Heurne sale.  His reference to Bruges (Carmichael, 1970: 37) is ambiguous so it is possible that he could have bought the skull later from a dealer.
See M Caygill, ‘Henry Christy, A W Franks and the British Museum’s turquoise mosaics’ in King et al (eds) Turquoise in Mexico and North America: Science, Conservation, Culture and Collections (2012)
Vandervin, H. (1844). Catalogue des Collections de Tableaux, Dessins, Gravures, Antiquités, Curiosités, et Objets d’Histoire Naturelle, formant le Cabinet Van Heurne, Dont la Vente se fera à Bruges ... le 21 Octobre, 1844 et jours suivants ... sous la direction de A. Bollaert, Directeur des ventes, et ministère competent, assisté d’Henri Vandervin. Gand [Ghent]: De Busscher Freres.

Description: Flat rectangular pouch, parfleche, constructed from a piece of rawhide, folded in a rectangular outline, first at the sides, and then at the ends, the end flaps tappering slightly, and painted. The designs are outlined in black, and each consists of an organge rectangle enclosing a green ractangle, with orange rectangles at each corner including white triangles, the centre filled with a green hour glass design including triangles decorated with small black triangles. Both the side and end flaps have holes for hide thongs for securing the parfleche.;
Curators Comments:
The Warwick label indicates that this is Blackfoot, from Mackenzie River, 1860, but it is perhaps more likely to have been collected with the other Blackfoot items by Seton; for biographical reference see 1982 Am 28 17. see JCH King `First Peoples, First Contacts' British Museum Press 1999
See also Ethdoc 1982 - papers relating to Major George Seton and the purchase from the Warwick Museum.  A letter from Seton, 1 July 1878, refers to a Blackfoot "Parflesh" i.e. a painted parchment case for carrying articles;  and a 'Blackfoot bag made of a "Parflesh" brought from Rupert'sland' .

Description: Bag, parfleche-like, of roughly rectangular shape, of painted skin. The back and front consist of two pieces of what is presumably bison rawhide, painted with geometric designs over what may be lightly incised [brown-filled] lines. The designs consist, on both sides, of star shapes at the centre of winged features, divided horizontally with points, and further triangles in the middle of the sides, tops and bottoms. The colour of the broad lines is on one side a dirty yellow and dull ochre; on the other side the designs are lighter, either a function of dirt or different pigments. The designs are enclosed in painted rectangles and the stars and side triangles edged with brown dashes, extensions of the brown design outlines. The sides are of a semi-hard skin, and the bottom of a smoked soft skin: the sewing is on the inside in sinew. Around the top edge are a series of holes, and skin strap so that the opening can be pulled closed. A faded ink inscription in G.Seton's [collector's] hand reads in part '...parfleche... Blackfoot... Indians of the far West of North America July 1858'.;
Curators Comments:
See General Notes 1982 Am 28 17 for biographical reference. see JCH King `First Peoples, First Contacts' British Museum Press 1999

Description: Basket, bowl made of grass, shells, sedge root, redbud, creeper (?), pearl-shell, feathers.;
Curators Comments: From acquisition "bowl". From acquisition "Santa Barbara". [Southern Pomo or Coast or Lake Miwok (Bruce Bernstein, Smithsonian, 2/03/2007)].; McEwan 2009, p.76 Reed basket with shell, glass and feather dangles. Northern California, USA, AD 1770 The basketry of northern Californian cultures can be traced back thousands of years and in historic times the Chumash and Pomo gained renown for their technical mastery. Weaving skills were passed from generation to generation, gradually building a shared repertoire of patterns and designs that identify the traditions of each group and distinguish them from those of its neighbours and rivals. A combination of weaving technique and the use of dyes was used to achieve bold contrasts and characteristic geometric motifs such as the zigzag design running round the circumference of this piece. Additional external embellishments include pendant strings of glass beads and abalone shell interspersed with groups of small disks and red woodpecker feathers.
Bib references: McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail; Dalton 1897 / Notes on an Ethnographical Collection from the West Coast of North America (more especially California), Hawaii & Tahiti, formed during the Voyage of Captain Vancouver 1790-1795; King 1994 / Vancouver's Ethnography: A preliminary description of five inventories from the voyage of 1791-95

Description: Basket made of grass (?), roots (?), bark (cedar), using twined technique. Design: flint or obsidian blade (?).;
Curators Comments:
Freer label: "183. Klamath Indians S. E. Oregon (Purchased of them by RWS. June 1876)".  /// Ethnography Department Christy Collection registration Slip: "('Flexible' crossed out) basket closely woven of straw coloured grass or roots, with vandyked bands of geometrical ormanent in dark brown on the sides. The bottom is of dark brown cedar bark. Klamath Indians S. E. Oregon. Purchased of them by R. W. Summers. 1876.".  [in ink II.110]; Sally McLendon:
Twined; warps willow or hazel. The weft is probably pine or conifer root, overlay of bear grass.  Three design elements consisting of opposed triangles forming large Vs.  Worked in bear grass and woodwardia fern overlay, with a row of three strand braiding at the rim.  Also four bands of three strand braiding at the bottom along the start.  Twined to the right.
Typical north eastern California style of weaving.  It had a handle made of twisted strip of fabric. Handle suggests that it is a gathering basket.; Design: this oblique parallelogram is often referred to as 'flint' (particularly when it is bisected by alternating chequered pattern) or 'obsidian blade'.
Bib references: Cawley 1994 / Indian journal of Rev. R. W. Summers: first episcopal priest of Seattle (1871-73) and McMinnville; Werth 1993 / "Ahnkuttie": ahnkuttie kleska wawa = Long ago they said: the Indian Journal of R.W. Summers

Description: Basket made of bark.;
Find spot: Found/Acquired: Colombia (Igara Paraná);  Found/Acquired: Ecuador (?);  Found/Acquired: Peru (?)
Curators Comments:
Information provided by Murui Muina elders and researcher Juan Alvaro Echeverri. Probably Bora. Baskets were woven by women. A loosely woven (muugai) basket represents a woman who is not willing to learn. Weaving style: armadillo ñenɨgaɨ. Made from “bejuco”. Murui Muina name: kɨrɨgaɨ muugaɨ
According to Murui-Muina elders Oscar Román Jitdutjaaño and  Alicia Sánchez, baskets symbolise the universe containing and connecting everything within it.
Baskets not only demonstrate the weaving ability of the women who make them, they also represent the role of women in preserving ancestral culture. Oscar Román Jitdutjaaño and Alicia Sánchez see tight and intricate weaving as evidence of wisdom, while looser and simpler work implies the loss of traditional knowledge. The ability to produce some of the patterns in these baskets has been lost as a result of the destruction caused by the rubber boom.
On display Room 24 (WTG) Relating to Animals (Amazonia) from July 2021
Román Jitdutjaaño, O. et al. "Genocide Collections at the British Museum: House of Shadows, Basket of Seeds." in Osorio Sunnucks, L and Cooper J Mapping a New Museum: Politics and Practice of Latin American Research with the British Museum. London: Routledge pp 247-257
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 4, Relating to animals)
Acq name (excavator): Field Collection by: Eugene Robuchon

Description: Basket (with sling) made of bark.;
Find spot: Found/Acquired: Colombia (Igara Paraná /Putumayo);  Found/Acquired: Ecuador (?);  Found/Acquired: Peru (?)
Dimensions: 56.00 centimetres including strap ;  25.00 centimetres;  22.50 centimetres;  22.00 centimetres;
Curators Comments:
According to Murui-Muina elders Oscar Román Jitdutjaaño and Alicia Sánchez, baskets symbolise the universe containing and connecting everything within it. Baskets not only reflect the weaving ability of the women who make them, they also represent the role of women in preserving ancestral culture. Tight and intricate weaving is evidence of wisdom, while looser simpler work implies the loss of traditional knowledge. The knowledge associated with basket weaving has been diminshed as a result of the destruction caused by the rubber boom. 
Baskets of this size are used to collect coca leaves and carry them to malocas, where they are dried out and prepared for consumption. Larger baskets are used to transport cassava roots and other large tubers, as well as hunted animals.

According to Murui-Muina elders Oscar Román Jitdutjaaño and  Alicia Sánchez, baskets symbolise the universe containing and connecting everything within it.
Baskets not only demonstrate the weaving ability of the women who make them, they also represent the role of women in preserving ancestral culture. Oscar Román Jitdutjaaño and Alicia Sánchez see tight and intricate weaving as evidence of wisdom, while looser and simpler work implies the loss of traditional knowledge. The ability to produce some of the patterns in these baskets has been lost as a result of the destruction caused by the rubber boom.
On display Room 24 (WTG) Relating to Animals (Amazonia) from July 2021
Román Jitdutjaaño, O. et al. "Genocide Collections at the British Museum: House of Shadows, Basket of Seeds." in Osorio Sunnucks, L and Cooper J Mapping a New Museum: Politics and Practice of Latin American Research with the British Museum. London: Routledge pp 247-257

Description: Coilwork basket for marriage dowry ornamented with shells, beads, formerly covered with feathers.;
Curators Comments:
Acquisitions register entry for 1949 records:
Pomo, California, USA.
"Marriage dowry basket (Chi-mo-pi-ko)"
Oldman Collection, Oldman No. 859.
See ETH DOC 1307.; Linda Yamane, an Ohlone basket weaver, comments that this basket is most likely Pomo in origin. This attribution is based on the use of Pismo clam shell beads, which can be identified by their straight edges, thick walls and coarse outer shell surface. These beads, and the patterns of glass beads are all attached to the completed basket with thread, rather than incorporated into the original basket structure as is normal with Ohlone examples. The red feathers are identified as those of the acorn woodpecker and the black as from a mallard, both of which are common to most weaving traditions of Central California.
The suggestion from Oldman that this is the base and Am1949,22.224.b is the lid of the same vessel is very unlikely to be true. The two sides do not fit cleanly, the decoration does not match and there is no tradition of making lidded baskets in this region of California. Instead these are likely to be two separate baskets that were collected at the same time. Likewise his attribution of this object as a "dowry basket" is unlikely to be accurate as marriage traditions in this part of California do not include dowry gifts. It is much more likely that this was a presentation dish used at ceremonial and sacred events, and perhaps given to the leaders of neighbouring groups as a gesture of esteem. (12/04/2010)

Description: Plaited fancy basket with lid made from fucia-coloured dyed black ash splints and sweet grass. Splints are looped, called 'curls', on outside of basket and lid for decoration. Weft of the bottom of basketry and top of lid is sweet grass.Lid has strip of braided sweet grass around rim and  lid handle is green-coloured dyed splint.;
Dimensions: 11.00 centimetres basket and lid ;  14.00 centimetres;  14.00 centimetres;
Curators Comments: Sweet grass is a native and wild plant to North America that grows along river banks in the northeast. It isn’t planted and grown like agricultural crops. Each plant has a rhizome from which buds sprout. The Haudenosaunee, the Iroquois Confederacy, have many traditions involving sweetgrass. Smudging sweetgrass, for instance, is a practice that often marks and ends ceremonies and celebrations. Many people insist that the sweet smell invokes kindness and compassion. In the 19th century, weaving sweetgrass into baskets became an essential way that women could earn money to support their families.  Grandmothers and mothers teach their daughters how to make the baskets but also nurture the plant stands, which are threatened today. Development and industrialised agriculture have taken over important sweetgrass habitat and threatened the species existence. The most vigorous stands, however, are the ones tended to by basket makers. Their knowledge of the plant and the craft (weaving) have enabled them to support their families economically and culturally.
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 6, Life's ordinary dangers)

Object type: wampum (object name); belt (object name); bead (object name)
Description: Wampum belt, woven on a bow-loom with skin thong warps and wool or vegetal fibre wefts, of shell beads. The white ground is decorated with a zig-zag line, composed 7 'V's touching at the base/apex, formed of purple wampum, two in each line. There are nine warps: it is 8 rows wide, and 145 beads long, to produce a total number of 1160 beads. The apex of each 'V' if flat, and four or five purple beads long. At one end this flat edge ends in two purple beads, and at the other in three purple beads; so it is possible that complete lines of bead are missing from one or both ends. This is further suggested by regular marks on the thong warps, such as might have been left by bound wefts, now no longer present. The beads are of varied diameters and lengths.;
Dimensions: 6.00 centimetres;  87.00 centimetres including warp ends ;  0.50 centimetres;

Title: The Fenton Vase; The Nebaj Vase
Description: Slip-painted polychrome vase made of pottery, with a scene representing the delivery of tribute to a seated lord. Above the basket presented to him are a series of six hieroglyphs which indicate his name and titles, while the other glyph panels correspond to those of the four figures in the scene. The figures wear jewellery, elaborate clothing and spangled turbans adorned with flowers.;
Curators Comments:
Am,F.1 to 569 is the collection of Mayan pottery formed in Guatemala by C L Fenton (qv) and purchased from him in 1930. The first three items were paid for by the NACF. The initial entry in Ethnography Dept. was under 1930,1212.1 to 3; the series was later re-registered as 1930,F.1 (as the Fenton Collection). See vol.2 of the Large Collections Register.
For the two vases and the sculpture given by the NACF, see the British Museum Quarterly, vol.V, 1930-1931, no.83, p.112.; McEwan 2009
Maya polychrome painted vases bear scenes rich in observed detail, which are combined with glyphic texts that communicate  information about the names of the main protagonists, the dates when key events took place and the nature and purpose of the exchange. This renowned vase records an interaction that is probably taking place in a palace setting. 
From the left in the rollout scene a kneeling visitor presents a devlivery of tribute consisting of a basket piled high with "tamales" (maize cakes). In the centre of the scene a ruler seated on a raised platform leans forward to receive the gift. Behind him a seated scribe is busy noting details of the exchange.
Bib references: McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail; Miller & Martin 2004 / Courtly Art of the Ancient Maya
Location: On display (Room 1 - Enlightenment, Scripts, Floor Case 17)

Description: Pair of skin moccasin-boots with beadwork (glass beads) and buttons (brass); traces of green dye or paint; cotton lining with floral pattern.;
Curators Comments:
Letter attached reads:
"Carlisle PA
Dec. 21, 1880
My dear Miss Booth:
I thought I would write to you this morning. I hope you write to me I think some time and tell me about all you home.
I like to hear how you home. I am ver sorry yesterday morning I 'd never come in school room. I am glad today my father letter from today and moccasins my father tell me about all my home I am glad today.
that is all to tell you.
I am close writing now.
from your friend
Emily Peatone [sp]; Kiowa suggested by Emma Hansen and Dennis Zotingh, 24.2.03; Almost all southern plains peoples (Southern Cheyenne, Arapaho, Kiowa, Apache etc.) used green pigment to colour hides for moccasins and shirts. This characteristic places this item among southern plains tribe. Blue diamond shape beaded area is however diagnostic of Kiowa craftswomanship. This motif can be found on a number of Kiowa itmes especially cradleboards. In Kiowa design, geometric motifs such as this can be juxtaposed to floral motifs that show woodland peoples' influences, most notably Delaware (Lenape) who became Kiowa's neighbours after resettlement to Oklahoma.
Location: On display (Room 26 - North America, Display Case 21)
Acq notes (acq): Acquisition details unknown.

Description: A polished wooden bow decorated at the apex with two bands of blue glass beads each formed from a bead-string wrapped around the bowshaft approxiamtely thirty times. Between the bands of beads is wrapped a piece of calico dyed with red and black stripes and circles on a white background.The string is made of plant fibre, wrapped thirteen times around the base of the bow and hooked over a peg at the top.;
Curators Comments: The bowshaft is studded with a number of knots and flaws, which would have made the bow impractical for its primary use: any attempt to draw the bow fully would have resulted in the shaft snapping and likely causing injury to the person drawing it. This coupled with the expensive bead and calico decoration suggest that this bow was a status symbol rather than a weapon. It is also likely that the plant fibre string is a later addition as both the fibre itself and the method with which it is attached are atypical of Plains bows and impractical for use. (Martin Schultz, 2/9/2010)
Bib references: Klann 1999 / Die Sammlung indianischer Ethnographica aus Nordamerika des Herzog Friedrich Paul Wilhelm von Württemberg; Carocci 2012 / Warriors of the Plains: The Arts of Plains Indian Warfare

Description: Bow made of reddish brown wood tapering to the end; with a stout string from which hang a tuft of feathers.;
Production place: Made in: Brazil (country);  Made in: Araguaia, River
Curators Comments:
Traditionally used for hunting and war, this bow and arrow (Am.5584) are among the oldest Iny-Karajá objects known to exist, following the 2018 fire at Brazil’s National Museum, which inspired this collaboration. These weapons reflect not only the violence of colonisation in Central Brazil but also the male lens of Western explorers and collectors.
The Iny-Karajá collection has been documented by traditional leaders from the Santa Isabel do Morro Hawaló community, Sokrowé and Ixysé Karajá, in collaboration with researchers Rafael Andrade (Brazil's National Museum), Manuel Lima Filho and 	Lucas Veloso Yabagata (Anthropological Museum of the Federal University of Goiás) as part of the SDCELAR project 'On the banks of the Araguaia:
Refounding Iny-Karajá collections in Brazilian Amazonia'.
Read more about the project here:  https://www.sdcelarbritishmuseum.org/projects/on-the-banks-of-the-araguaia/; Slip has added 'Carafa, Araguay River'. 
Note by A W Franks: "Band serves to tighten it? Similar bows at Maidstone Museum brought home by the Hon. Mr Marcham [?]".
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 4, Relating to animals)
Acq notes (acq): Christy 5354 to 5393 were all given by the master cutler and collector, William Bragge of Sheffield, on 21 April 1869.

Description: Bowl; carved of argillite; in the form of crouching animal (bear?) with bowl carved in its back; haliotis shell attached for eyes; bone pieces for teeth; pieces of bone inlaid around rim of bowl.;
Curators Comments: Superbly carved wood bowls, usually in the form of a single animal or creature, are among the finest of all Haida artefacts. They were used at feasts and potlatches to serve oolachen grease and other food. During the 1870s and 1880s argillite carvers began to carve bowls that were very similar in form and size to the traditional wood prototype. Almost all other argillite artefacts differ in scale or details from the traditional forms. Argillite plates, on the other hand, were derived from European prototypes and were usually decorated with American or European motifs. Argillite platters were an entirely new Haida idea, and oftern incorporate a myth carved in traditional style sometimes with one important character carved in high relief. (This entry is based on Introductory text for the 1980 ‘Art made for Strangers’ exhibition, and reflects scholarship at that time).; See Gessler, Trisha. The Art of Nunstints. Description: Dish or bowl, for grease, made of alder, in the form of a seal. The body of the bowl is roughly oval, or perhaps ogee, in shape, decorated with incised and low relief designs indicating for instance the flippers, the head, tail and tail flippers are carved 3-dimensionally. Both the underside of the head and tail have been carved away to lighten the feel of the bowl, and the interior has been carefully cut away to leave an eye-form shape [no doubt of abstract or practical rather than symbolic significance] in the interior. The grease is caked so thick that the interior wood has disappeared, leaving the bowl with a matt black surface that emphasises the superb plasticity of the design.;

Description:
Register 1979:
Pottery bowl in the form of a reclining female; painted red/brown and dark geometric designs.;
Curators Comments: McEwan 2009, pp.30-31 Ceramic bowl in the form of a reposing woman. Casas Grandes culture, Paquime, Mexico 1280-1450 AD The great trading centre of Casas Grandes in the desert reaches of northern Mexico was a key point of contact between the Pueblo cultures of what is today the southwestern United States and Mexican civilization to the south. The lively interaction between these two spheres of influence helped spawn a distinctive aesthetic tradition that finds expression in a combination of sensous modelled forms with bold geometric, polychrome designs. The accomplished execution and intriguing subject of this vessel beg close attention. The supine pose and closed eyes suggest that she has fallen asleep with legs drwn up, head nestling in a crooked left arm, and with her right arm resting across her chest. She wears a black armband and red wristbands, and the chevron painted on her cheeks may be an identifying clan emblem. Her carefully groomed and tightly tonsured hair seems to be held in place at the back by a segmented headband. The rectangular design on her front probably represents the woven pattern displayed on her skirt. It is based on the principle of rotational symmetry, contraposing identical motifs around a central, diagonal axis composed of two parallel rows of zigzags. The overall effect suggests the channelling and controlling of charged energies to productive ends.; Register 1979: North America, Casa Grandes. See ETH DOC 1396. Design incorporates Macaw head motif.
Bib references: McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail

Description: Kempo bowl with kené for serving fermented drinks;
Materials: pottery (fabric: clay, grog (keng kesh) and ashes from tree bark (apacherama)); resin (kaolin (maosh), ochre (mashinti), tree bark and copal resin were used in paints, slips and glazes)
Curators Comments:
The Shipibo-Konibo people have lived along the Ucayali River (modern-day Peru) for more than a millennium. Until the early 20th century, they were divided in two: the Shipibo occupied the middle Ucayali, and the Konibo its upper course. Increasing external pressures since the rubber boom led them to unite alongside the Xetebo people. Their recently created Indigenous Guard has been a turning point in the protection of their territory.
The geometric designs known as kené are an important expression of Shipibo-Konibo identity, representing elements of nature, their territory and worldview in pottery and textiles. Through mastering kené, women artists such as Sara Flores and Celia Vázquez Yui advocate for the protection of their territory and knowledge transmission. Made today by fewer and fewer Shipibo-Konibo women, traditional pottery is threatened by climate change and illegal logging, which limit access to essential forest materials. Yet this cultural practice endures, still relying on ancestral techniques: from the mixing of clay with grog (keng kesh) and ashes from tree bark (apacherama), to the seasonal procurement of kaolin (maosh), ochre (mashinti), tree bark and copal resin for use in paints, slips and glazes.
This object has been documented in collaboration with Shipibo-Konibo women potters* from the Ucayali Valley (Peruvian Amazonia) at the workshop ‘Ancestral Connections through Shipibo-Konibo pottery’ organised in November 2025 by the Shipibo Conibo Center and the Santo Domingo Centre of Excellence for Latin American Research at the Bakish Mai Multiversity (Pucallpa, Peru). *Shipibo-Konibo master ceramists and knowledge holders: Celia Vázquez Yui and daughter hija Diana Ruiz Vázquez, Judith Pereyra and daughter Rina Pereyra, Eusebia Picota Coribary and granddaughter Kimberly Pinedo, Elena González Baneo and daughter Janina Peso González, Lucía Vázquez and daughter Levania Puedo Romena Vázquez, and Lucilda Amacifuen Valera.; Register addition "Ucayali River".
The design on this pot, known as kené, represents and embodies a primordial anaconda. The anaconda is the life-force behind the waters, trees and animals that make up the Ucayali river system, the ancestral home of the Shipibo-Konibo People. If this power were to die, all life in the forest would die with it. Only women produce kené patterns. They dream about the designs and then bring them to life in artworks.
On display in Room 24 (WTG) Relating to Animals (Amazonia) from July 2021
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 4, Relating to animals)
Acq notes (acq): Am1936,0504.1 to 4 were given by Captain F McDermott and recorded as "Four pottery vases from the Upper Amazon". All were acquired on the Ucayali River; nos.1 and 2 are from Iquitos; nos.3 and 4 from Manacapuru, west of Manaos.

Title: Late Night News
Description: Earthenware bowl decorated in native red clay slip and gold lustre. The cartoon style imagery depicted on the interior of the bowl is centred on the self representation of the artist as a fictional character, Chongo, hence the bowl is signed ' Chongo Made and Painted Me'. Chongo is shown drinking beer or soda and channel surfing.;
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription quoted: Chongo Made & Painted Me;
Curators Comments:
J.C.H. King writes:
"Romero's traditional ceramic techniques and aesthetics are those of the San Juan valley pueblos. They utilise cartoon imagery featuring the self representation of the artist as a fictional character Chongo, here as signed on the bowl 'Chongo Made & Painted Me'. In this piece Chongo is shown drinking beer and channel surfing, hoping perhaps for news of his son, serving in Iraq. Chongo was a 1960s character from the television cartoon series 'Banana Splits'; later the term became a slang expression used by Hispanics of Native Americans. Romero was raised and educated in California and New Mexico:his work is celebrated in museums in U.S., Asia and Europe.
This contemporary graphic is placed in a geometric cartouche, of ancient Puebloan form. The image focuses on Native America's ambiguous relationship with the US armed forces - first as an enemy, of course, now as part of a fiercely held American identity, associated particularly with the US flag, for Indian people. This is expressed most forcibly in the celebration of the Navajo Code Talkers, who communicated in Navajo, in World War II, as an alternative to code, in the use US flag at powwows such as the annual Pawnee Veterans Homecoming, and in the celebration of the Native American marine Ira Hayes at the Marine Corps National Memorial, representing raising the flag on Iwo Jima in 1945. That this intense pride continues is seen most poignantly in the nationwide commemoration of the life of Lori Piestewa, a  single Hopi mother, who died early in the Iraq conflict, during the Spring of 2003".
Location: On display (Room 26 - North America, Display Case 20)
Exhibition history:
Exhibited:
2006 Jun-Present, BM Room 26; Gallery of North America, Case: "Pueblo Pottery"

Description: Bowl; carved of wood; in the form of a woman, on her hands and knees, wearing a bird maskette on her forehead, the bowl/body of shallow oval form, the bowl covered in black matt paint, the exterior carved with lines just under the rim.;
Ethnic name (made by): Northwest Coast Peoples; Coast Salish; Nuu-chah-nulth
Curators Comments: The Oldman collection was purchased for 50,000 sterling from the widow, on condition that the New Zealand Government bought the Oceanic sections, which they did for 35,000, and also sell off duplicates from the remaining African and American sections. 3 or more Oldman NWC items were set aside and never catalogued, I would guess because it was planned to sell them off. They include this bowl, and one of the very finest goat and sheep horn spoons, Q85 Am 368. One of the items that was sold off was the oldest and finest example of a Coast Salish horn rattle, collected by Cook, in the Leverian Museum, London, 1780-1806, illustrated in the Stone album in the Bishop Museum, and now in Rotterdam. This bowl, not duplicated anywhere, is a peculiar and difficult piece, and so may have been put in this category for disposal; nevertheless it has the Oldman number '1949 Am 22' and what may be a probate label attached.  //  Attached label (Oldman probate list number?): '839'. Acquired with 1949Am22 Oldman Collection but not registered at the time. Initially entered in Q-Register ("Found unnumbered" collections) as Q1972Am.118; subsequently Q-Registered again as Q1985Am.377, which number has precedence. The poor design and misproportion of the human figure in relationship to the size of the bowl suggests that this may not be Northwest Coast. However carving styles from Vancouver Island, the Inside Passage and northern Washington are neither well known nor documented. The rim decoration, and the rigid geometric decoration of the face both suggest a Coast Salish origin. This would need to be confirmed by trying to check that the wood was alder, Alnus rubra, or something else appropriate. (JCK,4/1995; plus additional details/description).  //  Ron Hamilton (12/9/1995) suggests that it may be Nuu-chah-nulth, representing a crawler, for instance using wood blocks to protect his hands; he suggests it may be a male despite the breasts; that the maskette may be a whale [and so strengthen the Nuu-chah-nulth designation] or possibly a porpoise. The rear feet, in being differentially stretched suggested a crawling motion. (Noted by JCK 12/9/1995).

Object type: figure (object name); lamp (object name); bowl (object name)
Title: The Cowichan Bay Bowl
Description: Human figure, lamp (?) made of stone (steatite).;
Curators Comments: This bowl is one of approximately fifty known examples of human-figure bowls from British Columbia, most found along the Fraser River. The purpose and origin of these bowls is unknown, but it is likely, based on their distribution, that they had ritual significance to the Salish peoples of the region, perhaps associated with burial rites and/or water rituals. There is little clear information on the ages of these bowls but they appear to be from a wide age distribution: those with an archaeological context suggest dates of around 1000 CE, but there is anecdotal evidence that they were in use by certain native shamans into the nineteenth century (see Duff 1956). This is one of two such bowls in the British Museum collection, the other being Am1976,03.22 (Duff no.48).; McEwan 2009, p.41 Seated Figure. Soapstone (steatite), Northwest Coast, Strait of Georgia tradition, USA 1st-9th century As in many other figurine traditions in the Americas, this pieces seems intended to replicate in miniature some aspect of social and spiritual life that was especially significant for the community. It is carved in soft soapstone with additional incised detailing of the head and hands. The encircling arms form a receptacle and the object may represent, or perhaps have been used in, shamanic or puberty rites.
Bib references: Inverarity 1950 / Art of the Northwest Coast Indians; Duff 1956 / Prehistoric Stone Sculpture of the Fraser River and Gulf of Georgia; King 1999 / First Peoples, First Contacts: Native Peoples of North America; McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail; Mitchell 1990 / Prehistory of the Coasts of Southern British Columbia and Northern Washington

Description: Chest or box made of cedar wood; carved and painted, and with lid.;
Inscription:
Inscription type: owner's mark Inscription position: carved along the bottom, front edge of the box Inscription language: English Inscription quoted: HAYDAH BOX, BRITISH COLUMBIA [italics] E.H.V. [italics end] 1864 Inscription note: Capitals. 
E.H.V. is Capt. Edmund H. Verney RN;
Curators Comments:
See McLennan and Duffek 'The transforming image' p 236 for images of four similar chests including this one described as of yellow cedar and having been carved by Captain Carpenter. The number is given slightly  eroneously as 'Am 1944.2197' as opposed to 'Am 1944.2.197'
Karen Duffek has clarified that in the above reference the authors make clear that the attribution of this chest to the Heiltsuk artist, Captain Carpenter is not certain: they state that this chest and some others "hint at a possible Carpenter attribution yet display fewer consistent features of his style. Among these are six containers whose compositions conform closely to the conventualized structures of northern chest box motifs (including this chest). In many ways they appear indistinguishable from similar Heiltsuk, Haida and Tsimsian examples. A closer look, however, reveals several qualities shared by Carpenter's known works"
Bib references: McLennan & Duffek 2000 / The Transforming Image: Painted Arts of Northwest Coast First Nations; Green 1999 / The British Museum Encyclopaedia of Native North America; King 1999 / First Peoples, First Contacts: Native Peoples of North America
Location: On display (Room 26 - North America, Display Case 8)
Exhibition history:
Exhibited:
2003 15 Dec-2009 Jun, BM Room 24: Wellcome Trust Gallery, dc4, Living and Dying
2011 2 Mar-Present, BM Room 26; Gallery of North America, Case: "The Northwest Coast of America"
Acq name (excavator): Field Collection by: Captain E H Verney
Acq notes (acq):
This object formed part of a collection obtained on the Northwest Coast by Royal Navy officer Lieutenant Edmund Hope Verney, commander of the gunboat HMS Grappler. The collection was shipped from the Northwest Coast to his family home at Claydon House in Buckinghamshire in three installments: February 1863, March 1864 and December 1864. It is possible that this chest was the one listed as No. 2 "large carved Indian chest" in his list of March 1864. Some uncertainty exists however as a photograph taken at the Claydon House private museum in 1902 illustrates this object on a shelf (right of picture) alongside a second, very similar chest: only one chest appears in the lists. The Verney collection was broken up and sold at auction in 1931. Collector Harry G. Beasley acquired a number of items from the Verney auction for his own Cranmore Museum in Essex, including this object. Following Beasley's death in 1939 his collection was dispersed with much of it, including this object, being donated to the British Museum in 1944. 
For more information see: 
-'Vancouver Island Letters of Edmund Hope Verney 1862-1865', ed. Allan Pritchard. Vancouver 1996
-'Provenance, twelve collectors of ethnographic art  in England 1760-1990', 	Hermione Waterfield and J.C.H.King, Paris 2006

Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription quoted: KEYSTONE BASE METAL 670499 [inner-cover]; Inscription type: inscription Inscription quoted: HAMILTON LANCASTER. PA., U.S.A. [dial]; Inscription type: inscription Inscription quoted: HAMILTON WATCH CO. MODEL 22-21 JEWELS. ADJ. TO TEMP. & 6 POS. MADE IN U.S.A. U.S. NAVY - BU.SHIPS - 1943 [barrel bridge]; Inscription type: mark Inscription quoted: 2F27246 [front-plate];
Curators Comments:
Comment from Richard Good, Catalogue of Watches in the British Museum. Vol. V (Unpublished manuscript)
Made by Hamilton Watch Co.
America, 1943
Boxed and aluminium cased deck watch with a club tooth lever escapement.
Signature: On the barrel bridge "HAMILTON WATCH CO MODEL 22-21 JEWELS ADJ. TO TEMP & 6 POS. MADE IN USA. US NAVY - BU SHIPS - 1943". On the dial "HAMILTON LANCASTER, PA, USA"
Box: of mahogany, not lockable but with a catch on the front. A glazed circular aperture in the hinged top reveals the watch. The piano type hinge is chromium plated and retained by Phillips screws, as is the catch. On the recess in the top is screwed an engraved aluminium label which reads as follows:-
CAUTION ALWAYS KEEP WATCH IN HORIZONTAL 
(DIAL UP) POSITION TO ASSURE 
ACCURATE PERFORMANCE
TO SET WATCH     (HOURS AND MINUTES ONLY)
1.  Depress shielded safety pin on edge of case
2.  Keeping pin depressed, pull out crown
3.  Release pressure on pin and set hands
4.  Push crown back to winding position
(Safety mechanism relocks automatically)
HAMILTON                            LANCASTER
WATCH CO                             PENNA
The interior of the box is lined with cushioned green baize.
Case: Aluminium open face case. In the screw on case back is "KEYSTONE and Co" in a keystone surround, "BASE METAL" and the number 570499. A map on inside back cover. Screw on bezel. 
Dial and Hands: Metal dial, silvered, with Arabic numerals. A subsidiary Up-and-Down and seconds dial. The up-and-down dial numbered 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48 and inscribed "UP" and "DOWN". Blued-steel spade hands.  Blued-steel up and down and seconds hands.
Movement:  
Ebauche Marks: The front plate numbered 2F 27246
Frame: In appearance a bar layout but the train has in fact a single bridge machined to resemble 3 bars.  The watch is designed to run for 56 hours.
Barrel and Mainspring: Going barrel I diameter 23.25 mm., height 4.2 mm.
Mainspring: height 4.1 mm., thickness 0.2 mm., this marked HW Co (see note 1)
Arbor: diameter 6.3 mm., snailed
Hooking: T piece and brace
Train: Brass wheels gilded with five crossings except for the steel escape wheel which has 4. The pinion leaves are exceptionally well finished.
Jewelled: From the centre onwards, unusually both the top and bottom centre holes jewelled. All the jewels are friction set in chatons except for the centre in the front plate. Ruby endstones to the escapement.
Escapement: Club tooth lever, straight line layout, short lever, double toller, the impulse roller brass, the safety roller steel, impulse pin D shaped. Pallet stones exposed. Escape wheel steel, both the impulse and locking planes burnished.
An equal impulse escapement.  
Number of teeth embraced 3½ 

Balance and Spring: Bimetallic uncut ovalising balance, the bar of invar the rim stainless steel, gold compensation screws, quarter timing nuts of platinum. Balance diameter 20.5 mm., height 1.75 mm. Ni-Span C flat spiral spring with 12½ turns and a terminal curve. The brass collet is poised.
Means of Regulation: Micrometer controlled. An index on the balance cock is biased by a spring against a cam which is mounted on a divided disc with "F S" engraved on the cock.
Train Counts and Beat Rate:
Great wheel  96
Centre wheel  75 pinion  12
Third wheel  80 pinion  10
Fourth wheel  80 pinion  10
Escape wheel  15 pinion  8
Beat rate 18,000
Motion work: cannon pinion 12
minute wheel 36, minute pinion 10
hour wheel 40
Keyless work: Positive set keyless work with a device patented by Ernest W. Wescher. This has a safety catch which needs to be pressed before the stem is put into the hand-set position, only then can the hands be set. This prevents accidental handsetting.
Up and Down Work: Planar differential work.
Dimensions: Box: Width 125.4mm., length 151.80mm., height 54.1mm
Case: diam 71.0 mm h. 22.0 mm.
Movement: diam 59.0 mm h. 13.0 mm over glass.
Provenance: Presented by the company to the Managing Director of Hamilton (UK), Mr Raymond Mellor, in 1962. Then purchased in 1988 
Notes:
(1) The barrel and mainspring design is unique to this watch. At first sight the spring seems to be incorrect since it fills so much of the barrel, far more than theory allows, however following theoretical considerations simply gives the maximum torque output possible for the available volume of the barrel which has nothing to do with the uniformity of torque output. The classical rules were in fact formulated for the fusee/barrel combination where maximum available torque is the aim, since the fusee is then used to equalise the torque output. With the going barrel the only way to equalise torque output is to utilise the intercoil friction, which is at a maximum when the spring is fully wound and diminishes as the spring unwinds. The ratio of intercoil friction to torque output depends upon the thickness of the spring. The thinner it is made the great the frictional losses. This then is the reason for the thickness of the spring being a mere 0.20 mm whereas one would expect it to be about 0.26 mm. for this size of barrel. Ultimately then maximum torque is sacrificed for uniformity of torque output.
One factor that cannot show in a pure torque/turns test is the effectiveness of the pull of the mainspring as a rector.  In the extreme case if the pull was radial no torque would be delivered at all, it would all be wasted. Thus the aspect of the spring in its barrel is important. In the case of the Hamilton barrel it is as favourable as it can be since, due to the amount of spring in the barrel, it never goes far from the barrel wall even when fully unwound. The same effect would be produced by making the barrel arbor bigger. The provision of a brace also helps in this direction.
(2) It is of interest to make a comparison with the Paul Ditisheim Deck Watch, registration no. 1972,0903.11. The relevent dimensions (in mm) are as follows:

Barrel Diameter	23	23.25
Spring Thickness	0.24	0.20
Spring height	5.0	4.1
Balance diameter:-
over rim	20.0	17.3
over screws	22.5	20.0
Balance height	1.825	1.75

Description: Marine chronometer; Earnshaw spring detent escapement; flat silvered dial; brass painted black hour and minute hands, blued steel seconds hand; movement in brass bowl gimballed in brass bound mahogany box; key, rate certificate,carrying box and two manuals.;
Inscription: Inscription type: signature Inscription position: front of box Inscription quoted: Hamilton Watch Co. Lancaster, Pa, U.S.A.; Inscription type: signature Inscription position: dial Inscription quoted: HAMILTON LANCASTER P.A., U.S.A.; Inscription type: signature Inscription position: back-plate Inscription quoted: HAMILTON WATCH CO LANCASTER, PENNA. Model 21. 14 jewels Made in U.S.A.; Inscription type: mark Inscription position: back-plate & dial Inscription quoted: 2 10228; Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: label on inside of lid Inscription quoted: NOTICE... Inscription note: series of rules; Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: label in bottom of box Inscription quoted: CAUTION... Inscription note: series of rules; Inscription type: mark Inscription position: front-plate Inscription quoted: ZE 10228;
Curators Comments:
Two manuals:
'Manual for overhaul, repair & handling of Hamilton Ship Chronometer with parts catalogue' Bureau of ship, Navy dept, October 1948
'Care and Handling of the Hamilton Marine Chronometer' Hamilton watch Company, Lancaster, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.; Comment from Anthony G. Randall and Richard Good, Catalogue of Watches in the British Museum.  Vol. VI (1990)
Made by Hamilton Watch Company, c. 1962  
Marine Chronometer
Signatures:  On the front of the box 'Hamilton Watch Co. Lancaster, Pa, U.S.A.'. On the dial 'HAMILTON LANCASTER P.A., U.S.A.' and the number '2 10228 '. On the back plate 'HAMILTON WATCH CO LANCASTER, PENNA. Model 21. 14 jewels Made in U.S.A.' and the number '2 10228'.
Case: The movement contained in a brass bowl. The winding hole is protected by a circular cover which can be moved aside to reveal it and which is returned by a spring. The heavy dial ring, also of brass, drops into a recess in the bowl top and is positioned by a peg. The glazed bezel clamps the movement into the bowl. The gimbal ring, its lock and the box furniture all of brass, the gimbal lock unusually fitted with two tensioning springs so as to make it a spring fit into the slot of the lock bracket that is on the bowl.
The brass bound mahogany box has a glazed panel in the top under the hinged lid. Inside the lid is fixed a panel:
NOTICE
WINDING: Wind chronometer regularly every day. 
SETTING: Set hour and minute hands in forward direction - never backward. Do not interfere with second hand at any time.
TRANSPORTING: In preparing chronometer for shipping, permit instrument to run down, wedge balance wheel as illustrated, remove chronometer and gimbal system from box, and pack in separate container with adequate padding. 
[Two illustrations showing the wedging of the balance.] 
To carry chronometer for short distances in operating condition, lock gimbal system and handle carefully.
CAUTION: Never impart a violent rotary motion to chronometer.
Do not transport chronometer when fully run down without first wedging balance wheel as illustrated above.
The hinges are continuous, that is of the piano hinge type. The lid catch is operated by a sideways movement of the small knob. The glazed middle portion carries a brass label with the signature as detailed above. There is no lock for the main box just a catch as for the lid. The carrying handles are cast and not recessed into the box. In the bottom of the box is another label with: 'CAUTION This instrument was designed for maritime navigation, and to be run only in the flat position. All factory rating tests have been made accordingly. To ensure accurate performance, always keep watch in horizontal dial up position, WHEN SHIPPING - always remove cased movement from gimbal ring and ship separately from mounting box'.
A tipsy key sits in a gusset in the box corner. Size of box 192 mm square x h. 190 mm.
Dial and hands: Flat silvered dial, the markings produced by etching and filling black. The dial secured by three screws with polished heads from the back, the screw ends visible at the dial edge but not polished, being hidden by the bezel. The reserve of going in the top half of the dial, the seconds in the lower. Signed as detailed above. Arabic numerals. The hour and minute hands of brass painted black, the seconds blued steel.
Movement:
Dial ring flange diam. 1066 mm reduced to 97.7 mm; dial ring th. 9.0 mm; front plate diam. 97.8 mm; back plate diam. 87.7 mm; frame h. 46.6 mm.
Frame: Of nickel-silver, the screws steel with polished heads. The nickel-silver parts finished with a wave decoration in straight lines. The pillars not riveted in but secured by screws at both ends. All the bearing holes bushed with gilded brass bushes or with jewels set in gilded brass chatons. The front plate numbered ZE 10228. The back plate unusual, being supported by three pillars only, the plate being cut away where it would normally be supported by the fourth pillar. Two large cut-outs allow for the removal of both the barrel and fusee once the barrel bridge has been removed. The polished steel setting up ratchet wheel is on the barrel bridge. The ratchet also of polished steel and controlled by a spring. The whole retained by a subsidiary bridge. The disc excluding pipe for the fusee arbor fitted with a rubber grommet. There is a separate bridge for the escape wheel lower pivot in the back plate which greatly facilitates assembly. The upper third and fourth jewels in a bridge secured to the front plate.
Fusee: Harrison's maintaining power with a brass ratchet wheel. The fusee gilded. The polished steel detent with an integral wire spring standing parallel to the arbor. The stop actuated by the chain which pushes a spring-loaded slider in the fusee so that it contacts a stud in the barrel bridge.
The barrel unusual, the lid being retained by five small screws. The barrel flanged at the cover end. The solid steel arbor snailed and 13 mm in diameter.
Barrel: Inside dim. 38 mm, internal available h. 15.3 mm, mainspring h. 15.0 mm, th. 0.44 mm. The mainspring fitted with a brace.
Train: Gilded throughout but wheels finished with a rather coarse straight grain. Pinions well polished all over, the centre alone with an undercut.
Jewelling: 14 jewels in all: lower third, upper and lower fourth, escape top and bottom, both with endstones. Two pierced balance jewels with endstones, that in the balance cock diamond, the other endstone ruby. Impulse, locking and discharge jewels.
Escapement: Spring detent escapement of the Earnshaw type but with some continental features. The brass escape wheel with only the teeth full thickness. The impulse roller set with a jewel, its acting face radial. The discharge roller unusual being made as a long split collet with two flats for adjusting and set with a jewel with a radius to the back of the acting part of the tip. The detent built up on nickel silver block which is steady pinned and screwed and made so as to provide an adjustable stop for the detent; this extended portion carries a gold stop against which the block that carries the locking jewel banks. The detent is wide - 3.00 mm - and the spring part is composed of two narrow strips each 0.5 mm wide and 3.5 mm long. The passing spring is supported by a bracket some 2.4 mm in height, the horn being bent and with a nib for the spring to bank against. The locking stone D-shaped and pinned into its hole in a square section portion of the detent. A slot in the end of the detent receives the head of a screw in the end of the detent block. With the detent securing screw slackened off, turning the screw in the block adjusts the depth of the detent with respect to the balance. The locking stone acts on the tooth after that which has just given impulse. The detent gilded.
Balance: Ovalising uncut balance with stainless steel rim and invar arm, the arm being brazed to the rim with signs of the brazing still visible. Ni-Span-C spring of 14 full turns with terminal curves. The spring clamped at both ends to a stud steady pinned to the cock and to a poised split stud mounted on the balance staff. The staff pushed into a flanged bush on which the balance sits, the balance sandwiched between the bush and a shaped washer with two screws.
The impulse roller of steel polished all over and with a ruby impulse pallet set with its impulse face radial. Three holes in the roller, the one opposite the stone being for poising purposes. The balance with eight compensation screws and four timing nuts; two for fine adjustment set to the side of the arm. The rim very thin - 0.6 mm. Rim diam. 35.5 mm; overall diam. 40.5 mm. h. 4.1 mm.
Going-train counts:
Great wheel (fusee) 90. Fusee with 8 turns
Centre pinion 14 leaves, wheel 90 teeth, 5 crossings
Third pinion 12 leaves, wheel 80 teeth, 5 crossings
Fourth pinion 10 leaves, wheel 75 teeth, 5 crossings
Escape pinion 10 leaves, wheel 16 teeth, 4 crossings
Beats per hour: 14,400
Motion work:
Cannon pinion 14 leaves, minute pinion 18 leaves (brass)
Hour wheel 54 teeth, minute wheel 56 teeth
Up-and-down pinion 12 leaves
Up-and-down wheel 120 teeth, 5 crossings
Provenance: Purchased in 1988.
Note:
According to the seller Mr Raymond Mellor, one-time Managing Director of Hamiltons U.K. Branch, the chronometer was finished especially for him from the last parts available at the factory in the U.S.A. This is therefore the last of this series of chronometers to be produced. It comes complete with rate sheet and service manual.
