Description: Lantern-shaped pendant reliquary; gold; middle formed of hexagonal tube of rock crystal enclosing minute wood-carving of the Ecce Homo and Crucifixion with Mary Magdalene and St John; column at each angle, lower part square, white enamel, upper baluster-shaped, ruby enamel; finial at top with double-faced head supported by two pairs of volute brackets; bottom plate engraved with strapwork panels and enamelled, projecting cherub in full relief. Openwork.;
Production place: Made in: Spain (?);  Made in: Mexico (?)
Dimensions: 4.00 centimetres;  23.00 grammes;  2.20 centimetres across base (max.) ;  1.70 centimetres;
Curators Comments:
Text from Tait 1986:-
Origin: Most probably Spanish or perhaps Spanish colonial (possibly in Mexico, 'New Spain'), late 16th or early 17th century.
Provenance: None is recorded.
Commentary: This jewel is probably one of the most carefully and skilfully executed examples in this small distinctive category. A number of these lantern-shaped rock-crystal devotional pendants containing minute religious wood-carvings have been recorded and published but with little agreement about their origin.
In the late nineteenth century opinion seems to have favoured a North European origin, and both Read (1902) and the authorities at the Louvre, where there are three examples (MV 386-8), attributed them to a German workshop in the late sixteenth century. In Dalton 1927 this attribution remained unchanged but a Mediterranean origin became an acceptable alternative after the Second World War, when, for example, Filippo Rossi (in ‘Italian Jewelled Arts’, London, 1957, pl. LXIIC) described one of them as “Italian, 16th century” and Parker Lesley (Parker Lesley, ‘Renaissance Jewels and Jewelled Objects from the Melvin Gutman Collection’, Baltimore Museum of Art, Baltimore, 1968, p. 63, no. 16) proposed a “Spanish workshop c.1590” for the rectangular version in the Melvin Gutman Collection. However, when that same piece was lent to a special exhibition at the Martin d'Arcy Gallery of Art, Loyola University of Chicago, Rowe (Donald F. Rowe, ‘The Art of Jewelry, 1450-1650’, exh. cat., Martin D’Arcy Gallery of Art, Loyola University of Chicago, 1975, no. 30) pointed out that “a Flemish attribution is possible for there was in that country a tradition of small-scale carvings, such as in the prayer beads. The political situation of the Spanish in the Lowlands could give rise to Spanish elements in the framing device and more of the Flemish tradition in the carvings”.
Nevertheless, there is ample evidence to show that European taste in this field during the sixteenth century had increasingly favoured enamelled goldsmiths' work and that the microscopic wood-carvings had become less fashionable before the middle of the century. Rock-crystal enclosing gold enamelled reliefs and figures became a great art especially in Spain; the beautiful IHS heart pendant from the Treasury of the Cathedral of the Virgin of the Pillar, Saragossa (now in the Victoria and Albert Museum, and published in Charles Oman, The Jewels of Our Lady of the Pillar at Saragossa, ‘Apollo’, June, 1967, pp. 400-6, fig. 4) is an excellently preserved and well-documented example. These minute wood-carvings are not particularly accomplished, and the suggestion that they may be the work of Mexicans, working under the supervision of the Spanish missionary fathers, is more convincing, especially as a background of blue featherwork occurs in the example in Baltimore (see Diana Scarisbrick, in ‘Jewellery, Ancient to Modern’, exh. cat., Walters Art Gallery, Baltimore, 1979, p. 183, no. 501.) The Mexican coloured feather-mosaic work was a much esteemed skill in Mexico that reached the highest levels shortly before the Spanish Conquest, but only a few fine examples with Christian subjects, like the Medici mitre in the Palazzo Pitti, have also survived in Europe.
Furthermore, the survival of a Mexican silver-gilt chalice set with a rock-crystal tempietto of this type enclosing minute wood-carvings of the Apostles against a background of feather-mosaic work does indicate that this proposed Spanish colonial origin may be correct. The chalice was formerly in the William Randolph Hearst Collection, and in 1948 was acquired by the Los Angeles County Museum. During the author's detailed study of the chalice in Los Angeles its authenticity and late sixteenth-century origin were confirmed. The chalice had been briefly mentioned and illustrated in the 'Addendum' in Charles Oman, ‘The Golden Age of Hispanic Silver 1400-1665’, London, 1968, p. 57, figs 278-9; this book, which was a catalogue of the Victoria and Albert Museum's collection, recorded the similarity of the Mexican mark stamped clearly on the foot of the chalice in Los Angeles (inv. no. 48.24.20) to the one stamped on a Victoria and Albert Museum piece (no. 104). In addition, Charles Oman stated that both pieces dated from the last quarter of the sixteenth century, along with a tiny, silver-cased carved boxwood triptych with a background of humming-bird feathers in each of the five scenes of the Passion (no. 104a, figs 275-6). Although the little triptych is unmarked, Charles Oman was confident that it could be attributed to a late sixteenth-century workshop in Mexico on the evidence of the Los Angeles chalice, the foot of which is not only richly embossed with figures, scenes from the Passion and ornamental motifs in the Renaissance manner but is also set with four minute wood-carvings under rock-crystal covers, one of which (the Entombment) has survived with its background of humming-bird feathers. Of the remaining three, one (the Agony in the Garden) still retains some traces of its humming-bird feathers adhering to the intricacies of the wood-carving; however, all three have now been mounted on a plain white background, which creates a very different effect. Although the four carvings are unpretentious, they have a direct expressiveness and a most convincing spontaneity - even the difficult crowded St Veronica scene is effectively handled - but there is one interesting error in the Scourging of Christ scene, where the Cock that crowed thrice in St Peter's presence has been placed on top of the Column.
The goldsmith, who was responsible for the very talented treatment of the shaped foot of the chalice, has skilfully incorporated these four carvings, flanking each with a standing figure of an Evangelist (on the one side) and a Latin Father of the Church (on the other). Using the full range of Late Renaissance ornamental motifs, the goldsmith has embossed and chased not only the three zones of the foot but also the stem and the lower part of the bowl. The quality of his work in these decorative friezes is no less fine than in his four relief scenes from the Passion of Christ on the foot, and it is particularly noticeable on the stem where immediately above and below the hexagonal rock-crystal tempietto this small-scale sculptural detail is well-preserved. The two smaller knops of rock-crystal that form part of the stem may be original, although neither seems entirely convincing and, indeed, the stem could have been assembled in this form at any time. Certainly, the plain bowl of the chalice that fits inside the embossed lower 'calyx' is not original, but when chalices are kept in use for liturgical purposes it is customary to have the bowl renewed at regular intervals.
Unlike this Waddesdon lantern-shaped pendant, the tempietto on the stem of the chalice is a true hexagon and, being much larger, it is designed with six individually carved reliefs, each composed of a pair of full-length Apostles, standing under a double shell niche, with architectural mouldings above and below. These little wood-carvings, although not of the highest quality, are fine examples of their genre, with features consistent with a late sixteenth-century Mexican origin, especially the background of humming-bird feathers to give colour and depth to the reliefs. The figures are treated with considerable variety, although the choice of a lance and a halberd for two of the Apostles to hold is exceptional, even in Mexico.
There seems no reason to doubt the evidence of this Mexican chalice, and certainly Charles Oman, after many years of study of Hispanic plate, was satisfied and included it in his major book on the subject. When taken in conjunction with evidence published by Theodor Müller in Das Altarchen der Herzogin Christine van Lothringen in der Schatzkammer der Münchner Residenz und Verwandte Kleinkunstwerke' (‘Zeitschrift für Bayerische Landesgeschichte’, bd 35, heft I, 1972), it would seem, therefore, that the Mexicans, under the tutelage of the Spanish missionary fathers, probably quickly learnt to carve these religious scenes.
The enamelled goldsmith's work on the Waddesdon jewel may also have been carried out in New Spain. However, as the quality seems as high as that found in Spain itself, the attribution can be made only tentatively, although it must be stressed that the goldsmith who made the Los Angeles chalice was a superior craftsman to many working in Europe at the end of the sixteenth century.
[A similar pendant is in the British Museum, inv. no. Af 2864.] 
Bibliography: Charles Hercules Read, ‘The Waddesdon Bequest: Catalogue of the Works of Art bequeathed to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild, M.P., 1898’, London, 1902, no. 183, pl. XL; O.M. Dalton, ‘The Waddesdon Bequest’, 2nd edn (rev), British Museum, London, 1927, no. 183; Hugh Tait, 'Catalogue of the Waddesdon Bequest in the British Museum. 1., The Jewels', British Museum, London, 1986, no.46, pl. XXVIII, figs. 197-199; Dora Thornton, 'A Rothschild Renaissance: Treasures from the Waddesdon Bequest', British Museum, London, 2015, pp.220-223.
Bib references: Read 1902 / The Waddesdon Bequest. Catalogue of the Works of Art Bequeathed to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild, M.P., 1898; Dalton 1927 / The Waddesdon Bequest : jewels, plate, and other works of art bequeathed by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild.; Tait 1986 / Catalogue of the Waddesdon Bequest in the British Museum; I The Jewels

Description: Pendant jewel; gold; standing ram, face and legs in white enamel; body covered in pearls, each held by wire passing through the gold; tail is twisted band covered with seed pearls; small bell fixed to neck; base enamelled with scrolls; upper part with flowers in full relief, rough ruby among them; whole pendant opens into two sections, hinges on edges of base; loop at bottom for pendant pearl.;
Production place: Made in: South America (?);  Made in: Spain
Dimensions: 4.65 centimetres incl. lower pendant loop ;  3.90 centimetres max ;  2.20 centimetres of base ;  25.00 grammes;  0.50 centimetres;
Curators Comments:
Text from Tait 1986:-
Origin: Perhaps South Italian or Spanish Colonial, late 16th or early 17th century.
Provenance: None is recorded.
Commentary: Amuletic jewellery has remained particularly popular in the Mediterranean lands until this century and, of course, seed-pearls are a common type of embellishment for jewellery from Southern Italy and the Eastern Mediterranean. However, there is no conclusive proof, and the attribution “German, late sixteenth century” published in Read 1902 and repeated in Dalton 1927 cannot be disproved, although there is little evidence to suggest that the techniques employed by this goldsmith were part of the Germanic tradition. The method of attaching the pearls and, more significantly, the flowers on the base is essentially a simple, almost primitive, solution, and although this pendant exhibits none of the characteristics of court jewellery, it nevertheless has the qualities of an early seventeenth-century provincial product.
It is valuable to contrast the pendant with that very pretentious large 'South German' ram pendant jewel from the Arturo Lopez-Willshaw Collection (sold at Sotheby's, London, on 13 October 1970, lot 12); not only was the 'jewel' of exceptional size and weight but both the body and the horns were set with diamonds, and it had one pendant loop under its belly from which a single pendant seed-pearl was suspended. The Lopez-Willshaw ram jewel appears to be a close variant of the enamelled gold Agnus Dei pendant jewel now in the Victoria and Albert Museum (M. 456-1936; Shirley Bury, ‘Jewellery Gallery Summary Catalogue’, Victoria and Albert Museum, London, 1982, case 26, no. 3, p. 153 with illus. on p. 154). This jewel came in 1936 with a number of other items of jewellery from a Hungarian noble family and was the gift of the Countess Teleki; catalogued as “Hungarian, late sixteenth century”, it has three gemstones (a diamond and two garnets) set in the front side of the Lamb's body and a loop under its belly from which hangs a single pendant pearl. Apart from the absence of horns, the two animals, and particularly the treatment of the fleece, are remarkably similar; of course, a rather different impression is conveyed by the Victoria and Albert Museum jewel because attached to it are a jewelled cross on a staff and a gold chalice into which flows a stream of gold blood from the breast of the Lamb. Significantly, a second example of this jewel was recorded in the Melvin Gutman Collection (see the full description in Parker Lesley, ‘Renaissance Jewels and Jewelled Objects from the Melvin Gutman Collection’, Baltimore Museum of Art, Baltimore, 1968, p. 62, no. 15), and it is clear that in all major respects the two versions are identical. The Melvin Gutman example had no known history beyond the previous owner, Millicent Rogers.
The wholly unconvincing characteristics of the Lopez-Willshaw ram jewel serve only to reinforce the impression that the Waddesdon amuletic pendant, with its unsophisticated construction, is a rare survival from that vanished mass of lesser jewellery, so popular in the Mediterranean lands. In such a context the pendant may have been intended as a votive jewel for a shrine rather than an amuletic jewel for wearing by the owner; however, such interpretations can rarely be documented. Certainly, the subject of a ram - not to be confused with the Lamb of God carrying a flag - is a most unexpected theme, as yet undocumented in European Renaissance jewellery designs and recorded in only one other example, the Lopez-Willshaw ram pendant.
The Cathedral Treasure of Santo Domingo (today called Ciudad Trujillo) contains one jewel that may be closely related, although it is a gold enamelled bird pendant. It was illustrated and briefly described in E.W. Palm, Renaissance Secular Jewellery in the Cathedral at Ciudad Truillo. ‘The Burlington Magazine’, XCIII, 1951, p. 319, fig. 16, where it is stated to represent “a dove with outstretched wings, 3.7 by 5 cm”). The entire front surface of the jewel - the tail, the body and the wings - is covered in small pearls, leaving only the head and neck free to be enamelled. It is not known if this Santo Domingo votive jewel was made in Spain or in a Spanish-American workshop, but its similarities to the Waddesdon ram pendant may help to establish the origin of this unusual 'reliquary'-type jewel.

Description: Large perforator made of jadeite incised with a pair of motifs on the head or handle; probably ceremonial or symbolic rather than practical.;
Curators Comments:
Register addition "Olmec,  SC".; McEwan 2009
Ritual bloodletting was an ancient and widespread practice in Mesoamerica. It was undertaken as a solemn sacrificial gesture to deified ancestors in order to maintain a mindful and respectful relationship and elicit their good will in return. This kind of reciprocity underlay much of Mesoamerican religious practice. Bloodletting implements were fashioned out of bone, flint, green stones, stingray spines and shark teeth and were used in self-sacrifice rites which involved drawing blood from various parts of the body. 
Carved in precious green jadeite, the size of this Olmec perforstor suggests that it was not actually used for bloodletting, but instead served as a highly visible element of ritual regalia to be carried and displayed on ceremonial occasions. The cleft gluph at the top recalls those on the Olmec adze and Teotihuacan stone mask and underlines the key symbolic purpose of these ritual instruments, which was to pierce the membrane marking the threshold between the visible and invisible worlds.
Bib references: McEwan 1994 / Ancient Mexico in the British Museum (Line drawings of detail); McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail

Description: Lemonade pitcher; spun aluminium; flared body opening out towards lip, with ring handle attached with spherical wood knop; maker's mark stamped on the base.;
Producer name: Made by: Russel Wright (and designed by)
Inscription: Inscription type: signature/monogram Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: RUSSEL WRIGHT Inscription note: Stamped.; Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base;
Curators Comments:
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950', revised ed. 1994, no. 434:
See also Rudoe 1991, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950', Cat. 318-25 
Unable to afford the machinery required to work pewter and chromium-plated steel, Wright turned to spun aluminium, which was cheaper and easier to work. Aluminium had up till then been used for kitchen wares rather than tablewares, but Wright designed a line of informal serving accessories, exploiting the spinning process to create novel shapes such as this striking, asymmetrical pitcher. The line was produced throughout the 1930s and included many newly-created items to suit changing styles of entertaining - bun warmers, cheese boards, tid-bit trays, sandwich humidor, etc (see W.J. Hennessey, 'Russel Wright: American Designer', Gallery Association of New York State, Cambridge, Mass. 1983, 20-24, A. Kerr, Russel Wright Dinnerware: Designs for the American table', Paducah, Kentucky 1985, 22-28 and a more recent publication, A.Kerr, The Collector's Encyclopedia of Russel Wright Designs, Paducah 1990, 30-40).
A comparative example of this pitcher is in Kerr 1990, p.37, top plate.
Bib references: Rudoe 1994 / Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A Catalogue of the British Museum Collection
Location: On display (Room 48 - Europe 1900 to the present, Display Case 6)
Acq notes (acq): Purchased by donor at Mood Indigo, New York, in September 1987 for $146.

Title: American Modern
Description: Pitcher; from group of 'American Modern' dinner-ware; earthenware, cast; in 'sea-foam' green glaze.;
Producer name: Designed by: Russel Wright;  Factory of: Steubenville Pottery
Inscription: Inscription type: signature/monogram Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: Russel Wright Inscription note: Impressed with facsimile signature.; Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: MFG. BY STEUBENVILLE Inscription note: Impressed.;
Curators Comments:
In set with 1988,0116.1
In set with 1988,0118.1-2
In set with 1989,0108.1-3
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no. 319. 
Russel Wright trained as a theatrical designer with Norman Bel Geddes (see Decorative Arts 1850-1950, Cat. 113) in the 1920s before setting up his own studio for the design of furniture and other household products for retail sale in aluminium, chrome, ceramics and wood.
     The designs for 'American Modern' were completed in 1937, but it took Wright two years to find a factory willing to undertake production. In order to overcome an initial lack of response, Wright mounted a huge advertising campaign, using his theatrical background to promote the service with radio interviews, appearances in stores, lectures and so on. Within two years the factory was unable to keep up with demand and over the next twenty years more than eighty million pieces of 'American Modern' dinnerware were produced, making it one of the most popular mass-produced patterns for informal dinnerware ever sold and the standard present for young brides in the early 1940s.
     Its success lay not only in its unprecedented asymmetrical organic shapes but also in its equally unconventional colours, subtle and original shades which customers were encouraged to mix and match as desired. Wright worked with ceramic engineers at Alfred University, New York, to develop the glazes, which were very different from the bright primary colours used in Frederick Hurten Rhead's 'Fiestaware', another informal service. In his use of soft shapes without rims or hard angles, Wright was among the first to make the shift from Bauhaus-inspired geometries to more complex, biomorphic forms and himself acknowledged his esteem for the Surrealists (W.J. Hennessey, 'Russel Wright: American designer', Gallery Association of New York State, Cambridge, Mass. 1983, 38). For similar concern with organic form, see Eva Zeisel's 'Town and Country' dinnerware (Cat. 333-5).
     For further discussion, see M. Greif, 'Depression Modern: The Thirties Style in America', New York 1975,172-3; Hennessey, 37-44; A. Kerr, 'Russel Wright Dinnerware: Designs for the American table', Paducah, Kentucky 1985, 34-44, Brooklyn 1986, The Brooklyn Museum, 'The Machine Age in America', 62.
     Wright found that his simple and austere style was no longer appreciated in the 1950s. He closed his design office in 1958 and became an environmental consultant for developing countries and then for the National Parks Service [Industrial Design 23, March/April 1976, 46-51).

Title: The Normandie Pitcher
Description: Pitcher; chromium-plated brass, of tear-drop section, the body formed of a single sheet of metal bent to shape, with a tear-shaped piece for the base, the join concealed beneath a strip which runs round the base, along the edge and round the rim; the handle is formed of a flat strip of metal expanding at the top to blend in with the line of the rim.;
Producer name: Designed by: Peter Müller-Munk;  Factory of: Revere Copper & Brass Incorporated
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: REVERE ROME NY Inscription note: Stamped.;
Curators Comments:
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed.1994, no.214:
Peter Müller-Munk studied at the Kunstgewerbeschule in Berlin under the silversmith Waldemar Ramisch and emigrated to America in 1926. He designed briefly for Tiffany & Co. before setting up his own studio for handmade silver. In his article in The Studio 98, London October 1929 (the same article appeared in the American magazine Creative Art 5, October 1929) Müller-Munk called for greater harmony of design and technique, criticising contemporary manufacturers in the silver and associated metal industries for striving to imitate handmade pieces with mass-production methods instead of adapting their merchandise to their machines; he despised the application of handmade ornament to a spun or stamped object and the 'artful practice' of cutting a hammered surface into the die. He claimed that the machine would not put the silversmith out of business: 'I still have the outmodish confidence that there will always remain a sufficient number of people who want the pleasure of owning a centre piece without being forced to share their joy of ownership with a few thousand other beings.' To illustrate his argument he included machine-made metalwork designed by Professor F. A. Breuhaus for WMF and his own handmade silver. He was soon to be proved wrong; the demand for silver was hit by the Depression and in the early 1930s he turned to industrial design. From 1935 to 1945 he taught at the Carnegie Institute of Technology, where he helped to organise the first college course in Industrial Design and Production Methods (Design 47, 9).
     This pitcher was known as the 'Normandie' pitcher because its shape was blatantly derived from the smokestacks of the celebrated French ocean liner launched in 1935. The Normandie was a noted example of French modernist design and the image of the ship became familiar through Cassandre's popular poster. The 'Normandie' pitcher has been described as 'streamlining at its most elegant and practical, a perfect harmony of efficiency, material and the machine process' (Brooklyn 1986, The Brooklyn Museum, 'The Machine Age in America', 307); the spout pours perfectly. Another recent discussion notes the use of the tear-drop form with reference to Norman Bel Geddes's view that a drop of water was the perfect streamlined form. Streamlining thus suggests the flowing surface of water, thereby blurring the distinction between mechanistic and organic design - the pitcher could be grouped with either (New York, 1985, Whitney Museum of American Art, 'High Styles: Twentieth Century American Design',  fig. 3.35, p. 120).
     For examples of Müller-Munk's silver, see Newhaven 1983, Yale University Art Gallery, 'At home in Manhattan. Modern Decorative Arts, 1925 to the Depression', K. Davies. cat. nos 9, 18, 68. Müller-Munk also participated in the Third International Exposition of Contemporary Industrial Art held at the Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York, in 1930-31, nos 396-7, with illustration. For an account of an exhibition of Müller-Munk's industrial design at the Philadelphia Art Alliance in 1946, see Design 47, May 1946, 8-9; the works exhibited ranged from electrical household goods and sewing machines to industrial canteens.
Information supplementary to Rudoe 1994:
See also J. Rudoe, 'An historical continuum: collecting 20th century applied art from Europe and America at the British Museum' from 'The International Art & Design Fair 1900-2002' pp. 15-28, fig. 12.
     For a geometric silver tea-set by Muller-Munk of 1928-9, see Sotheby's New York, Important Design, 13 December 2013, lot 71. 
     See also Rachel Delphia and Jewel Stern with Catherine Walworth. 'Silver to Steel: the Modern Designs of Peter Muller-Munk', exhibition catalogue, Carnegie Museum of Art, Pittburgh 2015.

Description: Plaque made of jadeite. Suspension holes drilled towards the top of the plaque indicate that it was worn as a personal ornament and possibly, due to its large size, as a separate pectoral pendant. The design represents a Maya ruler in full ceremonial regalia sitting cross-legged on a throne. His head is shown in profile, while his body faces to the front. He wears an elaborate plumed headdress incorporating part of the head of a monstrous animal. He is richly decorated with personal ornaments, many of which would have been made of jade. These include ear flares, a bar pectoral, wristlets, anklets and an impressive belt with a mask. On his left arm he wears a war shield bearing the image of the Jaguar God of the Underworld. The king inclines slightly to the right towards an attendant. A scroll issues from his mouth indicating either speech or song.;
Curators Comments:
Blurton, 1997
This exquisite plaque is one of the finest examples of Maya jade carving. Its irregular shape may be due to the craftsman's desire to take full advantage of the available fine quality stone to produce his work.; McEwan 2009
This Maya king is depicted cross-legged atop a raised dais with regal regalia including a plumed headdress, ear spools, bar pectoral and a royal belt. The war shield on his left arm bears the image of the jaguar god of the underworld. His speech scroll suggests the declaration of a formal edict. Below stands an attendant dwarf, part of the royal entourage. This plaque was found at Teotihuacan, apparently taken there from one of the Classic Maya cities.
Bib references: Blurton 1997 / The Enduring Image: Treasures from the British Museum; McEwan 1994 / Ancient Mexico in the British Museum; McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail; Joyce 1938 / The Gann Jades

Description: Plate; earthenware; overglaze transfer-printed design in pastel colours comprising the exhibition buildings round the rim and in the centre, a stylised design incorporating the two central structures - the Trylon and the Perisphere - with 'New York World's Fair, 1940'.;
Producer name: Designed by: Charles Murphy;  Factory of: Messrs Homer Laughlin (East Liverpool, Ohio)
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: Printed undergalze factory stamp: Homer Laughlin  MADE IN USA, with model numbers M 38 N 6; Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: Printed overglaze circular roundel with inscription in black: DECORATED BY CHARLES MURPHY/ 150TH ANNIVERSARY/ INAUGURATION OF GEORGE WASHINGTON/ AS/ FIRST PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES. This inscription partly obscures the factory stamp;
Curators Comments:
Officially, the New York World's Fair celebrated the 150th anniversary of the inauguration of George Washington as First President of the United States and this is recorded in the inscription. The Fair was built in the Bronx and its design was centred on two large white structures - the Trylon and the Perisphere - which became the symbol for the Fair and appear in the centre of the plate (see J. Allwood, 'The Great Exhibitions', London 1977, pp. 145-150).
         Charles Murphy was first introduced to Frederick Rhead at Homer Lauhglin by Guy Cowan, his tutor at the Cleveland School of Art in the late 1920s. They subsequently became good friends. In around 1936 he became assistant to Rhead at Homer Laughlin till 1938. He then worked for Declacomania/Decal Products and it was there that he designed the decal (design for transfer pirnting) used on the 1939-40 New York World's Fair plate, as well as the decal used on the San Francisco Exhibition plate of 1939 (see 2003,0104.1). At the same time he was commissioned to paint a large mural of 14 x 43 feet for the Fair by Arthur A. Wells of Homer Laughlin, who was in charge of the pottery section of the World's Fair Exhibit Committee in 1939 so the link with Homer Laughlin was maintained. The souvenir plates not sold in 1939 were carried over to the subsequent Fair in 1940. The factory was initially in Newell, W. Virginia, then moved to East Liverpool, Ohio (the above information comes from Jo Cunningham, 'Homer Laughlin, A Giant among Dishes 1873-1939', Schiffer 1998, and from biogrophical information on Murphy kindly provided by Homer Laughlin).
Location: On display (Room 48 - Europe 1900 to the present, Display Case 6)

Description: Earthenware side plate; high-fired earthenware with decal decoration of fish amongst spindly linear constructions, in black outline. Factory marks.;
Producer name: Designed by: William Craig McBirney;  Factory of: Shenango China Company
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: Shenango China Inscription note: printed;
Curators Comments: Part of a set of restaurant china designed for the Hotel Sherman (demolished 1980) in Chicago. McBirney had been chief designer for the factory since 1937. He had a distinguished career, working with designers such as Russel Wright. The pattern was called ‘Well of the Sea’ and its spindly linear constructions are a familiar feature of post-war art. The service was advertised in Shenango’s 1952 catalogue as ‘an honest expression of contemporary design’.  It emerged out of the collaboration between the Syracuse ceramic industry and the Syracuse Museum, to improve US ceramic design. This resulted in the first National Exhibition of Dinnerware Decoration, which aimed to draw the attention of American artists to the ceramic medium. Prizes were awarded to artists well known for their expression in other painting media (see Art News XLVIII.7, part 1, November 1949, p. 7).
Location: On display (Room 48 - Europe 1900 to the present, Display Case 6)

Description: Earthenware dinner plate; high-fired earthenware with decal decoration of fish amongst spindly linear constructions, in black outline with areas in colour. Factory marks.;
Producer name: Designed by: William Craig McBirney;  Factory of: Shenango China Company
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: Shenango China Inscription note: printed;
Curators Comments: Part of a set of restaurant china designed for the Hotel Sherman (demolished 1980) in Chicago. McBirney had been chief designer for the factory since 1937. He had a distinguished career, working with designers such as Russel Wright. The pattern was called ‘Well of the Sea’ and its spindly linear constructions are a familiar feature of post-war art. The service was advertised in Shenango’s 1952 catalogue as ‘an honest expression of contemporary design’.  It emerged out of the collaboration between the Syracuse ceramic industry and the Syracuse Museum, to improve US ceramic design. This resulted in the first National Exhibition of Dinnerware Decoration, which aimed to draw the attention of American artists to the ceramic medium. Prizes were awarded to artists well known for their expression in other painting media (see Art News XLVIII.7, part 1, November 1949, p. 7).
Location: On display (Room 48 - Europe 1900 to the present, Display Case 6)
Acq notes (acq): Purchased by J.Rudoe in New York, circa 1990

Description: Earthenware plate, transfer-printed in blue with text and imagery commemorating the American abolitionist, Elijah Lovejoy. In the centre, the text of the First Amendment of the American Constitution, and in four cartouches round the border, on the left, the words 'We hold that all men are created equal', on the right, 'Of one blood are all nations of men', at the top the words,' LOVEJOY, the first MARTYR to American LIBERTY, with the image of a printing press, the figure of Justice and a kneeling enslaved figure. At the bottom a set of scales, with American eagles in the spaces between.;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: front Inscription quoted: LOVEJOY, the first MARTYR to American LIBERTY;
Curators Comments:
Elijah Parish Lovejoy (1802-37), a prominent American abolitionist, Presbyterian minister and newspaper editor was killed while defending his right to print anti-slavery material. He was buried in an unmarked grave and commemorated much later in 1897 with a monument in Alton, Illinois, the city of his death. Several versions of this plate are known in American collections (eg. at Yale University Art Gallery, see https://artgallery.yale.edu/collections/objects/65569 and “Plate ,” Museums at Washington and Lee University: Online Exhibits, accessed June 30, 2020, https://omeka.wlu.edu/exhibits-ucah/items/show/21), and although none is marked, it is widely accepted that they were made in Staffordshire for export to America. Ellouise Larsen in ‘American Historical Views on Staffordshire China’, New York 1939, p. 242 discusses these plates with the information that they were produced ‘to be used in raising money for the purpose of freeing the slaves’, but gives no source for this information. 
    For further illustrations of the plates, see David and Linda Arman, ‘Historical Staffordshire’, Danville, VA, 1974, and ‘Anglo-American Ceramic Cup Plates’, Portsmouth RI, 2000; Sam Laidacker, ‘Anglo-American China’ 2 vols, Bristol, Pennsylvania, 1951.
     See also ‘Antiques Journal’ online, March 2020, https://antiquesjournal.com/index.php/2020/03/30/antislavery-ceramics/ (accessed 30.6.2020).
Location: On display (Old Ethno Library (BEP), Cupboard 58)
Assoc name: Named in inscription: Elijah Parish Lovejoy
Acq name (acq): Purchased from: Peter and Sue Rees
Acq name (previous): Purchased from: Peter and Sue Rees

Description: Toast-water pot and cover, earthenware, thrown, pale bluish glaze, the cylindrical body rising from the flared lower part, scroll handle, moulded spout terminating in an acanthus motif, moulded beaded decoration near the rim and on the rim of the domed cover which is ornamented with ten applied moulded daisy motifs and surmounted by a flat knop with a pointed button, decorated with a black print on each side showing a black man in fine clothes in a landscape with a verse and on other side a black woman in fine clothes in a landscape with a verse. Marked.;
Production place: Made in: England;  Retailed in: Kingston (Jamaica)
Dimensions: 17.00 centimetres with lid ;  14.00 centimetres without lid ;  18.00 centimetres max width - spout to handle ;  12.00 centimetres front to back ;
Inscription:
Inscription type: retailer's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: Manufactured/for/Mrs R Brandon/Kingston/Jamaica Inscription note: printed in black within a border of semicircles; Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: above and below print of black man Inscription language: Jamaican Creole Inscription quoted: Me take me road da cane piece, de nigga dem da run
An all come behind me, get de fum fum
Wid me centung da me back, chacolata da ma pan,
Me da mash putto putto, me da tink pon Quacco Sam,
Wid me ring ding &c; Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: above and below black woman Inscription language: Jamaican Creole Inscription quoted: Me hab me regan gown - me hab me gingham coat,
Hankecha me tie me head, tanky massa, the my treat,
And da **rra mo me wanty, me hab ma junka fan.
Fe de go da Berry Hill, fe go see Quaco Sam
Wid me ring ding &c;
Curators Comments:
Toast- water was drunk in the early 19th century for  medicinal purposes, as  a way of giving nutrients to  invalids. It was made by pouring boiling water onto  toast, letting it stand, then straining off the liquid. This  explains the strainer with large holes, made as part of  the pot, see 	http://dishynews.blogspot.com/2015/07/transferware- toast-water-jugs-and.html (for a more ornamental example of this form, made at Worcester's Flight, Barr and Barr factory and once owned by the 4th Duke of Gordon, see Bonham's, 27 November 2024, lot 261). 
      	‘Quaco’ is an African 'day' name, meaning  Wednesday's child, which became pejorative in Jamaica (Barbara Lalla, 'Quaco Sam: A relic of  archaic Jamaican speech', Jamaica Journal 45,  1981, 20-29, online at https://ufdc.ufl.edu/ UF00090030/00036/22j ). Lalla refers to a 1966  account of 'a series of crockery commissioned for  Mrs Rebecca Brandon just after her husband's death  in 1838’ among other evidence leading her to  conclude that the Quaco Sam verses date from  before emancipation. According to Paula Burnett,  'The Penguin Book of Caribbean Verse in English',  2005, the song was composed between 1812 and  1825. The verses were apparently sung to the tune of  ‘White Cockade’, initially an 18th century military  tune, which became a common dance tune by the  early 19th century. It is not known when the tune was  first heard in Jamaica, but it became a central part of  the colony's Creole musical identity in the 1830s  through the Quaco Sam verses. For the use of  Quaco, see also two satirical prints: 1877,0811.206  and 207.
	     Mrs Brandon sold other items, including a  transfer-printed pottery sugar-box, with the same  mark on the base, see https://picclick.co.uk/Antique- Pottery-Pearlware-Blue-Transfer-Sugar-Box- Jamaican-132714259802.html
	The Brandons were Sephardic Jews from Portugal  (the name was originally Brandâo), a number of  whom went to Jamaica and traded there, see http:// davidgibbins.com/journal/2016/3/24/the-portuguese- jewish-ancestry-of-rebecca-de-daniel-brandon-1783- 1820. This article deals with the generations before  the toast-water pot was ordered. A further source  lists the deaths of Jews in the  Sephardic  congregation in Kingston from 1809-1907 (see http:// www.jamaicanfamilysearch.com/Members/ jewds01.htm ), including a Rebecca Brandon, who  died on 16 May 1874, widow of Manasseh, whose  death is recorded on 30 April 1845. But these dates  do not correspond with the information given by Ray  Fremmer in an article on ’Jamaican Emancipation  earthenware’ in ‘The Daily Gleaner’, Kingston, of 15  July 1966, citing various newspaper advertisements.  According to Fremmer, Manasseh Brandon was a  Kingston merchant, whose father, Abraham, had  come to Jamaica from England before 1779.  Manasseh’s died in 1838, after which his wife  Rebecca continued the business and it was under  her direction that ceramics with the family name  were manufactured in England. Fremmer illustrates a  virtually identical pot, lacking its lid, as part of a  series of ceramics with transfer-printed scenes and  Jamaican creole rhymes commissioned by Mrs  Brandon. A plate printed in the centre with the same male figure as on this pot and a moulded alphabetical border is illustrated in B.  Lalla and J. D'Costa, "Language in Exile, three  hundred years of Jamaican Creole" University of  Alabama Press, Tuscaloosa and London, (1990),  p.144, pl.3, in National Library of Jamaica.
	For estate owners in Berry Hill, Jamaica see https:// www.ucl.ac.uk/lbs/estate/view/2379 (UCL Legacies  of British Slavery website). The principal crops were  sugar and rum.
Bib references: Pendergast 2012 / Strainer Jugs and their uses
Location: On display (Room 47 - Europe 1800-1900, Display Case 7)
Condition: glaze heavily crazed, knop damaged, strainer damaged, crack to spout, chip to rim of cover

Description: Globular or round wooden rattle carved in two more or less symetrical sections, each with a compressed face, deeply carved with great vigour: the mouth oval with a tongue peeping out from the teeth, full nose, deep cheek lines, deeply carved eyes, and broad swelling eyebrows. each face is compressed in ridged circle, the rest of the rattle carved with flat planes painted blue. The handle is bound with a skin thong, which ends in a knob finial to prevent slippage; the two halves are held together in four places with thong ties; the rattle inside is still present. The rattle was carved with great delicacy, and shows considerable use and patination. One face still has traces of red paint.;
Curators Comments:
Lady Franklin visited Sitka on her way to the Arctic in search of her husband. An account has been published, and is in the departmental library. (JCK,20/6/1995; plus additional description etc.).

Description: Rattle made of gourd, wood, metal.;
Curators Comments:
Sir Hans Sloane’s ‘Miscellanea’ catalogue is a bound volume in Central Archives containing seven separate catalogues: ‘Miscellanies’, ‘Antiquities’, ‘Seals’, ‘Pictures’, ‘Mathematical Instruments’, ‘Agate Handles’ and ‘Agate Cups, Bottles, Spoons’. Each contains numbered entries that list and describe objects collected by Sloane between the 1680s and 1750s. Each catalogue begins with object number one. 

Text from Sloane Miscellanea catalogue: Miscellanea Miscellanies 1237 "1237. A Maracca or rattle of a gourd made use of by the Indians of Carolina as a rattle in their triumphs Calumets & & c. from Coll. Nicholson."; Early blue object label reads: "Maracca or rattle. Indians of Carolina"

Description:
Rotary watch.
Front-plate fixed with a revolving steel plate with teeth cut around the periphery. A second revolving disc carries the train and escapement.
White enamel dial.
Blued-steel hands.
Nickel case with glazed bezel and back; inscription.;
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: movement Inscription quoted: AUBURNDALE ROTARY MASS. 346 PATENTS MAR 30 1875 JULY 20 1875 JUNE 20 1876 JAN 30 1877;
Curators Comments:
The Auburndale Watch Company began production of the Rotary watch in 1877 and sold them at a price of $10. The model was not successful and production ceased after about 1,000 had been made. [see American Watchmaking 1850 - 1930, by M.C. Harrold. NAWCC Buletin supp. 1984; Text from 'Watches', by David Thompson, London, 2008, p. 130-131.
Auburndale Watch Company
ROTARY WATCH
AUBURNDALE, MASSACHUSETTS, 1878-83
SIGNED: 'AUBURNDALE ROTARY MASS. 346 PATENTS MAR. 30 1875 JULY 20 1875 JUNE 20 1876 JAN 30 1877'
In the 1870s there was considerable interest in America in producing a cheap, reliable watch for the mass market, as Roskopf had done in Switzerland. At this time two patent solicitors, Edward Locke and George Merritt, came across Jason R. Hopkins's design for a 'rotary' watch. Hopkins had recently run the Washington Watch Company, and his design was for a watch with a revolving movement and a detent escapement, which could be manufactured for as little as fifty cents and consequently retailed at a very low price. After the construction of a model, Locke and Merritt rejected the idea as impractical. Unwilling to accept defeat, Hopkins entered into an agreement with William Wales, who had organized the United States Watch Company, and offered the design to William B. Fowle for $10,000. Wales set up a manufactory in Auburndale, Massachusetts, and the Auburndale rotary watch became a reality. Hopkins initially patented his design jointly with W.D. Colt on 30 March 1875 (no. 161,513). Further patents to cover the design were registered to William B. Fowle on 20 July 1875 (nos 165,830 and 165,831); 20 June 1876 (no. 179,019); and 30 January 1877 (no. 186,838). The history of the Auburndale Watch Company was extensively researched by Edwin A. Battison and published by the National Association of Watch and Clock Collectors Inc. in 1970. It is largely thanks to Battison that we know about these watches and their history today.
The Auburndale rotary watch was unusual in both design and concept. It had a large barrel containing a long mainspring, and the wheel train and escapement operated beneath cocks and bridges mounted on the top of the barrel. This meant that, as the watch ran, the whole assembly revolved around a fixed gear of teeth cut into the inside circle of a wide ring fixed into the case, which acted as the barrel cover. This in effect turned the watch into a form of karussel, in which the movement rotated once in 2½ hours. Although the initial patent was for a watch with a form of detent escapement, it seems that, by the production stage, the form of lever escapement found in this watch was adopted as standard. In 1877 production began in earnest and watches retailed at just $10 each. Unfortunately, the watch proved to be unreliable and in the end only about 1,000 examples were made, of which just 510 or so were completed. 
One aspect of this watch suggests that the case is a later addition: there is no provision in the case to put the watch into hand-set mode. In the original design this was done by operating a slide in the case band, which moved a lever in the movement to engage the winding button with the hand-set gear. As the watch stands now, access must be gained by removing the snap-on back, requiring the use of a case knife opener. A similar example, no. 447, formerly in the Time Museum, Rockford, Illinois, has the same problem. Perhaps both these pieces were left unfinished in the factory, and later cased cheaply, without consideration of hand-setting problems.
The watch was a commercial failure. Auburndale began to manufacture more conventional watches, timers and thermometers, but by 1883 the company was in financial difficulties. With no backers forthcoming, the firm closed down after only seven years in business. As a result, today Auburndale rotary watches are very rare indeed.; Comment from Richard Good, Catalogue of Watches in the British Museum. Vol. V (Unpublished manuscript)
Made by the Auburndale Watch Company 
Auburndale, Massachusetts, c. 1878
Nickel silver cased watch with a rotating movement and a pointed tooth lever escapement.
Signature: On the movement front plate 'AUBURNDALE ROTARY  MASS PATENTS MAR 30 1875 JULY 20 1875 JUNE 20 1876 JAN 30 1877'. There is also the number '346'. These markings are on the inside of the front plate and visible from the back of the watch. 
Case: A nickel silver open face case with a glazed back, the glass is missing. Both front and rear bezels are snapped on. Stamped on the case band under the back bezel is the number 14371. 
Dial and Hands: An enamel canister dial with Roman numerals. Blued-steel hour and minute hands.
Dial-plate: None intended. 
Dust-cover: None intended.
Movement:
Ebauche Marks: The number 346 is punched under the dial, on the movement plate and on the edge of the main movement support plate.
Frame: A complex frame with the main movement support plate of similar shape to a shallow mainspring barrel. The 'barrel cover' is the stationary internally cut gear which meshes with the first pinion of the going train and this gear is snapped in, in the same way as a cover would be. The barrel is centrally mounted and its cover is the top plate of the watch. On this plate the various parts are supported by cocks but with a bridge for the lever. The spring is wound from the arbor and as the barrel rotates so does the movement and the first pinion is caused to rotate as it runs around the fixed internally cut gear. The barrel bottom is numbered 346. The mainspring is hooked into a subsidiary ring which is then effectively hooked into the barrel wall via a loose piece of spring. The ring has an internal diameter of 29.1 mm and a wall thickness of 0.5 mm. 
Barrel and mainspring:
Barrel: going barrel, internal diameter 31.0 mm, height 1.45 mm.
Mainspring: height 1.30 mm, thickness 0.23 mm. 
Barrel Arbor: a loose ring-shaped piece 6.0 mm diameter, not snailed. 
Hooking: round 
Train: The internally toothed gear is of nickel silver. The two train wheels are of brass with five crossings.
Jewelling: Jewelled bearings for the balance only, the endstone in each case is rubbed into the same setting as the jewel hole. This means that they cannot be separated for cleaning purposes.
Escapement: A pointed pallet escapement of the Silvain Mairet type with all the lift on the wheel teeth. A single roller with a D-section impulse stone. The short lever is not jewelled but made from solid steel with a brass pin inserted for the safety action as in a normal ratchet tooth lever escapement. The brass escape wheel has four crossings. There is draw on both pallets. 
An equal impulse escapement. 
No of teeth embraced: 32.
Balance and spring: A plain nickel-silver three-armed balance with a D section rim. The blued-steel balance spring has 13 1/3 turns.
Means of Regulation: A blued-steel index on the balance cock registers against a divided scale with 'S' and 'F' stamped on the balance cock.
Train Counts and Beat Rate:
Internal gear 	120 
First wheel    	80  pinion 6 
Second wheel   	70  pinion 8
Escape wheel   	15  pinion 7
Beat rate: 18,000
Motion work: The pin with the pinion which meshes with the castle wheel passes through the barrel post and carries the cannon pinion of 12. This meshes with the minute wheel of 30 on which is mounted a minute pinion of 10. Below the minute wheel and mounted on it is another wheel of 30. This wheel, which can slip relative to the minute wheel, meshes with a wheel of 30 mounted on a square on the end of the barrel post. Thus in normal operation the drive to the hands is through the wheel on the barrel post but when the hands are set the two minute wheels slip relatively and the drive to the hands is through the cannon pinion. 
Winding system: Stem wound and set. There is a two-part stem in which the part mounted on the movement running across to the centre of the movement where a sliding castle wheel meshes either, for winding, with a contrate wheel mounted on an arbor which in turn connects with the barrel arbor. Or, for hand- set, meshes with a pinion mounted on the pin which carries the cannon pinion. The lever used to engage the hand setting is only revealed when the front bezel is removed. It is therefore surprisingly that this bezel is not hinged but snapped on.
Provenance: Formerly in the Ilbert collection. Ilbert purchased this from Chamberlain 25/12/1932 
Bibliography: Richard Good 'The Auburndale Rotary Watch' Horological Journal, Volume 139 (July 1997) pp.236-238.
Notes:
Apparently 1,000 of these movements were begun but only about 510 were completed. The Auburndale Watch Company existed from 1876 to 1883. See Edwin A. Battison The Auburndale Watch Company 'NAWCC Bulletin' 218: pp.49-68, Contributions from the Museum of History and Technology, Paper 4. 
The Auburndale Rotary is mentioned in G. Daniels 'English and American  Watches', item 40, p.114.
An example of one of these watches, No. 447, is in the collections of the Time Museum of Rockford Illinois. See Donald Robert Hoke, 'The Time Museum Historical Catalogue of American Pocket Watches', Rockford 1991, cat no. 218.
For other examples of this type see Battison, 'op. cit.' where the following are listed:
No. 46, now in the Science Museum, London. However, there is no record of an Auburndale Rotary watch in the Science Museum. Furthermore he recorded this watch as formerly belonging to C.A. Ilbert, and the watch here catalogued is no. 346. It seems likely therefore that the Science Museum reference and number are erroneous.
Nos. 124, 176, 224, 241 were all once in Edwin Battison's collection.
No. 161, owned by a Mr. Abbot. 
No. 250, in The Henry Ford Museum.
No. 361, owned by F. Earl Hackett.
No. 387, in Dr. Alfred G. Cossidente's collection.
No. 403, owned by Dr. W. B. Stephens.
No. 423, owned by Grossman. 
No. 447, now in the Time Museum, Rockford Illinois.
An unnumbered movement is illustrated in the 'American Jeweler', (December 1898) Vol. 17, No. 12, p. 371.

Curators Comments:
on display in All Short People: Little Ears
The following is the text used for the display mentioned above:
Little Ears 
Little Ears, or Small Eared Old Man, was born around 1854. He took over the All Short People band in 1892 when his brother Bull Back Fat passed away. In 1879 the Bloods and Crow camped together to hunt the last buffalo. Little Ears had a famous sorrel which he traded to the Crows for three good buffalo runners and a blue roan that had come third in a race to kill the first buffalo in a hunt. Photographed by Edmund Morris in late September, 1907. Manitoba Archives 149
When Little Ears was elected chief of his band, Agent James Wilson noted in his January report: "He has always been a good quiet fellow and has promised to help me in every way which the department may require.
He has also sent his girl to the 'Home' as an example to others & will use his influence in that direction." Sir Alexander Galt Archives
Little Ears is flanked by White Horses Woman, daughter of Stolen Person, and Young Pine. Little Ears' diminutive stature would suggest that the name of his All Short People band is at least partly based on the heights of its members. Glenbow Archives NA-3416-8 

Double saddlebags were used only by women. During the time this saddlebag was acquired from Little Ears, they primarily served a decorative function. The beadwork on the woolen panel forms an interesting contrast; traditional Blackfoot stepped triangles executed in the lane (lazy) stitch, alongside European influenced floral designs in overlay stitch. British Museum 1903.78 
Text courtesy of Arni Brownstone, Galt Museum, Lethbridge, Alberta, Canada.

Description: Sculpture of the Maya Maize God, a youth wearing a headdress in the form of a stylized ear of corn and hair in the form of the silk of the cob. The head is disproportionately large compared to the narrow shoulders and slender torso. The sculpture was probably carved from two different blocks of limestone, one for the head and another for the torso.;
Curators Comments:
Sculpture of the Maize God, one of eight that were once set on the cornice of Structure 22, commissioned by Waxaklajuun Ub'aah K'awiil (also known as '18-Rabbit'), the thirteenth ruler of Copan. It was built in AD715 to commemorate the twentieth anniversary of his accession to the throne.
The Maize God with his vibrant, youthful features represents the Maya ideal of beauty and features prominently in Maya art during the Classic period (200BC - AD900). He personifies the agricultural cycle and is associated with abundance and prosperity. In this sculpture his headdress is a stylized ear of corn and his hair the silk of the corn.
Bibliography:
S.G. Morley, G.W. Brainerd and R.J. Sharer 'The Ancient Maya', 5th ed. (Stanford University Press, 1994)
L. Schele and M.E. Miller, 'The Blood of Kings' (London, Thames & Hudson, 1986)
K. Taube, 'Aztec and Maya Myths' (London, The British Museum Press, 1993)
D. Tetlock, 'Popol Vu' (New York, Touchstone, 1996)
S. Martin and N. Grube, 'The Chronicle of the Maya Kings and Queens: Deciphering the Dynasties of the Ancient Maya' (Thames and Hudson, 2000); McEwan 2009, p.21 
Limestone bust of the Young Maize God. Maya, Copan, Mexico, AD 680-750
The Popol Vuh, the sacred book of the Maya, recounts a creation story that tells how the world came into being and how the gods created the first humans out of maize - a staple crop among the Maya. The Young Maize God is depicted here upon his emergence from the Underworld as the embodiment of vigorous fresh growth, marking the beginning of the annual agricultural cycle of renewal. 
Many similar busts were used as architectural embellishments on Temple 22 at Copan. Rather than being fashioned from a single block of limestone, the heads were sculpted separately from the torsos, and close inspection of this well known  version reveals differences in colour, graining and surface texture. The head is also disproportionately large compared with the narrow shoulders and slender torso. This disparity may have been intentional or might indicate that this particular head and torso were not originally meant to fit together but were "restored" in the late 19th century.
Bib references: McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail; MacGregor 2010 / A History of the World in 100 Objects

Acq name (previous): Transferred from: Victoria and Albert Museum
Acq notes (acq): Maudslay’s original sculptures, from Yaxchilán in Mexico and Copán in Honduras, were initially deposited at the Victoria and Albert Museum (known as the South Kensington Museum until 1899) in 1885. There appears to have been some lack of clarity over whether these were a gift, or a loan from Maudslay. With Maudslay’s permission, the original sculptures and a selection of casts were transferred to the British Museum, on the grounds their subject matter more properly fell within the British Museum’s purview. The deliveries took place on the 20th (originals) and 22nd (casts) of November 1893. The majority of the casts remained at the V&A, on the understanding that they too would be transferred to the British Museum as soon as space could be found for them. Continued confusion over the legal ownership of the collection (Maudslay, V&A or BM) – see correspondence V&A correspondence archive - probably explains why neither casts nor originals were registered at the time of their arrival. The issue was finally resolved with the transfer of the remaining casts to the British Museum for exhibition in 1923. The originals sculptures were therefore given a 1923 registration number, despite their having been present at the British Museum since 1893.

Curators Comments:
J. Brooking-Rowe (Ed.), VI. Ralegh Notes:The Seals of Sir Walter Ralegh, Twenty-Fourth Report of the Committee on Scientific Memoranda. Trans. Devon. Assoc., vol.  XXXVII, (1905), pp. 79-80 [source Steer, https://www.genuki.org.uk/big/eng/DEV/EastBudleigh/Brooking-Rowe1905]
"In 1904 there were put up for sale in London three silver seals, all made for Sir Walter Ralegh for use in his various offices. These afterwards passed into the hands of Messrs. Crichton Brothers, of Bond Street, who, finding they were wanted for the British Museum, devised a scheme whereby they should eventually be presented to that institution. They prepared a number of electrotype copies of the seals to be sold at a guinea the set, the purchasers of the electrotypes to be considered as the donors of the originals to the Museum. Messrs. Crichton's ingenious device met with only limited success, about two hundred sets only having been sold, but they decided, nevertheless, that the seals should go to the British Museum. The gift having been accepted by the trustees, they are now on exhibition in the Medieval Room. Apart from their personal and historical interest, they possess artistic qualities of no mean order, though the Elizabethan age was far from being the best period of English seal engraving. The largest, about three inches in diameter, is also the most interesting historically, being Ralegh's seal as Governor of Virginia, the first English colony, though its Governor never set foot in it. It bears the date 1584, and has the five fusils in bend with a martlet borne by Ralegh, with crest and supporters; around is the legend, in capitals, PROPRIA INSIGNIA WALTERI RALEGH MILITIS DOMINI ET GUBERNATORIS VIRGINIǢ. The second seal is for the offices of Warden of the Stannaries of Cornwall and Devon, Governor of the island of Jersey, and Captain of the Queen's Guard. It shows a mounted knight in plate armour with plumed helmet, sword, and shield riding to the left on a galloping horse caparisoned and plumed. On the shield and caparison are Ralegh's arms, five lozenges in bend. In two concentric circles are the legends: (1) SIGILL: DNI-WALTERI RALEGH: MILITIS: GARDIAN: STANNAR: CORNUB.: ET DEVON. (2) CAPITAN: GARD: REG: ET: GVBERNATOR INSULǢ: DE: JERSEY. A charter in the British Museum, dated 1594, has appended to it a second seal of Ralegh's, for the same offices, though without a legend. The third seal would appear to have been intended for unofficial use; it bears only the sixteen quarterings of Ralegh, with three crests, supporters, and the motto "Amore et virtute," and has no inscription. The first two seals have folding handles at the back, pierced and engraved with ornamental details."; The BM was given these seals by subscribers who bought copies of the seals made by Crichton's. Up to 600 of these, in silver but unmarked except with a serial number on the reverse, were apparently made. We have a list of subscribers. We occasionally receive letters (on object file) from owners of these copies. DFT

Curators Comments: Named after King Charles II, South Carolina was settled in the 1650s, becoming an official crown colony in 1719 (North Carolina split off and became a separate colony in 1729). Plantations of cotton, indigo and rice were worked by enslaved African labour, integral to the development of the colony. During the eighteenth century a large proportion of the enslaved Africans reaching British colonies in America entered the country at Charleston. South Carolina became one of the most prosperous of the British colonies in America, until the British were driven out in1780-81 during the American War of Independence.

Description: Circular silver seal-matrix for Grenada; one piece of a double die, with three loops. A sugar-mill pulled by oxen as bundles of raw cane are being fed in by enslaved Africans. Inscription in the exergue and legend around. With wax impression.;
Inscription: Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: exergue Inscription quoted: HÆ TIBI ERUNT ARTES; Inscription type: inscription Inscription quoted: SIGILLUM GRENADÆ CARRAIC : ET GRENADIN : AUSTRAL :;
Curators Comments:
The inscription ‘HÆ TIBI ERUNT ARTES’, is taken from Virgil’s ‘Aeneid’ 6. 847-853, describing Virgil’s vision of greatness, literally, ‘these arts to you’, used in this context to mean ‘these will be your arts’, in other words, as addressed to the island, the sugar mills will be your power’. The second inscription also refers to the island of Carriacou, an island north-east of Grenada, belonging to Grenada. ‘Austral’ in this context refers to the Southern Hemisphere.
     Grenada had been a French settlement since 1650. It was captured by British forces in 1762 and ceded to Britain with the Treaty of Paris in 1763 at the end of the Seven Years’ War. It became French again in1779, during the American War of Independence, until 1784 when the island was handed back to Britain with the Treaty of Versailles and was thereafter British until 1974. Sugar was initially introduced by the French in the early 1700s, using enslaved African labour to work the plantations. As the industry spread the cane fields expanded into the virgin forests of the interior, and even cultivated on the steep hillsides. The British vigorously expanded sugar production, but the rebellion of the French on the island during the French Revolution shattered the economy and after the abolition of slavery in 1833 the sugar industry declined quickly and by 1870 it had been replaced by cocoa. For further information see Beverley Steele, ‘Grenada, an island state, its history and its people’, Caribbean Quarterly , March 1974, Vol. 20, No. 1, pp. 5-43. For a later seal-die of Grenada for 1838, also depicting a sugar mill, see 1906,1117.21. 
      The image of the sugar mill may have been derived from Diderot's Encyclopaedia (Plates, vol.1, published 1762-72).

Curators Comments:
Text from 'Watches', by David Thompson, London, 2008, p. 152-153.
Jaeger-LeCoultre
AUTOMATIC WRISTWATCH
LE SENTIER, SWITZERLAND, 1953
SIGNED: 'LECOULTRE Futurematic'
The name LeCoultre is well known in the world of watches and has a history dating back to the nineteenth century. The origins of the company are quite complex, involving a number of changes of partnerships and financial arrangements with other firms that developed fine-quality steels for the watch industry and manufactured watch components and the machinery for making them. The partnership between LeCoultre and Jaeger began in 1915, when Jacques-David LeCoultre, in collaboration with the Parisian watchmaker Edmond Jaeger, designed a revolution counter for use in aircraft. Just two years later the alliance was cemented in a venture to sell high-quality watches. The company Jaeger-LeCoultre was formalized in 1926 and production began of new and innovatory watches such as the 'Duoplan', in which the movement was arranged on two separate levels, allowing a normal-sized man's wristwatch to be compressed to about one third of its size. Jaeger-LeCoultre is also renowned for producing very small watches. Its calibre 101 watch measured just 14 mm in diameter and 4.85 mm thick, and was described in 1940 as the smallest watch in the world.
Following the successful introduction by John Harwood of the first automatic winding wristwatch in 1929 (see registration no. 1958,1201.1179), it became clear that there were still problems to be overcome. One was to reduce the wear on the pivots of the winding weight, and another was to find a suitable method of stopping the winding mechanism when the mainspring was fully wound. The 'Futurematic' watch, first developed by Jaeger-LeCoultre in 1952, has some unique features. The movement used is LeCoultre's calibre 497, a 17-jewelled watch with lever escapement and automatic winding. An oscillating winding weight, centrally mounted on the back of the movement, provides automatic winding while the watch is worn. In rotation, the movement of the weight in both directions is transferred to the mainspring barrel to wind the spring. There is also a state-of-wind indicator to inform the wearer how much reserve is left in the mainspring. The system relies on a pinion that is turned as the mainspring is wound by the moving weight. As the mainspring is wound, a small lever is turned by the winding action. This lever carries a pin, which eventually comes into the path of the winding weight, on which is mounted a hook. The pin catches the hook and holds the weight in a locked position so that no more winding can take place. As the watch runs, the mainspring runs down. In doing so, the pin moves out of the hook's way and allows the winding weight to move freely again to wind up the mainspring. Very little motion is necessary to start the watch going as the mainspring is 'set up', meaning that it is partially wound even when the watch has run down.
A peculiar feature of this watch is the lack of a winding button and stem in the usual position at 3 o'clock in the edge of the case. Instead there is a sliding button on the back of the case for setting the time. When the button is moved across to the hand-set position, it causes a detent to intercept the oscillating balance while the hands are being set, so that precise setting can be achieved. As soon as the button is moved out of its hand-setting position, the watch begins to tick again at a rate of 18,000 beats per hour, a standard for modern wristwatches.; Comment from Richard Good, Catalogue of Watches in the British Museum. Vol. V (Unpublished manuscript)
Made by Le Coultre
Switzerland, c. 1950
Gold cased self winding wrist watch with power reserve indication and with a club tooth lever escapement.
Signature: On the dial "LECOULTRE Futurematic SWISS". On the winding weight 'LECOULTRE UNADJUSTED SWISS 497' in a rectangle. On the front plate 497/1 1069218 and on the cock an import 'VXN'.
Case: A 14ct gold case two piece. Stamped inside the snap on back is 'CASED AND TIMED IN THE USA BY LECOULTRE 14 K GOLD' and the Le Coultre trade mark. 
The button on the back of the case is stamped 'DO NOT LIFT SLIDE'. This button carries a hand set wheel which is within the case and is mounted on a circlip, which is on a boss on the inside of the case back. 
Dial & Hands: A silvered and grained dial with raised gilt numerals 6 and 12 and batons at the other hours. Two sunk subsidiary dials, one for the power reserve and the other for the seconds, this has just 4 lines for the 15, 30, 45 and 60 seconds. The up-and-down dial is marked 'POWER RESERVE' and has a red segment for the last eight hours of available running time, see section on the up-and-down work.
Gilt hands the hour and minute baton type with the other two hands of lozenge design.
The dial is held on by a small side clamp which also positions it. This clamp fits a notch in the dial edge.
On the dial side of the weight when it is as far in the clockwise direction as it will travel, a part seconds scale printed on it in the appropriate position may be used to time the watch to the second.
Movement: 
Ebauche Marks: On the front plate is the number 1069218 and the calibre number 497/1.
Frame: A nickel silver movement of complex construction. Under the barrel bridge is scratched very neatly 'VIII'.
Letting down the mainspring is carried out before working on the movement. The screw with the triple slotted head is held by the screwdriver as the ratchet which appears through a hole in the balance cock is released. The mainspring can then be let down in a controlled fashion by means of the screwdriver. 
Barrel and Mainspring: There is stopwork on the going barrel which is fundamentally of the differential screw type.(1) 
A disc is mounted on a thread on the barrel arbor. This disc is prevented from turning by two screws which are mounted on the barrel cover. These screws pass through holes in the disc and there are four holes so that the spring can be set up with an accuracy of half a turn. As winding takes place the chamfer on the edge of the disc moves aside a polished steel cone shaped pin C. This pin C is mounted on the lever P which in turn moves the L shaped sector that meshes with the pinion on which the up-and-down hand is mounted. A pin on the lever P as it moves eventually is placed in the path of the stop on the weight which then catches up and further winding is prevented. See below under up-and-down work.
The mainspring is set up by 1 ¼ to 1 ½ turns and the weight arranged to lock after a further four turns of winding. This leaves a safety margin of about ¾ of a turn before the mainspring would be fully wound. 
The mainspring has a brace which has tongues which locate in slots in the cover and barrel bottom. Printed on the barrel cover is 'UNBREAKABLE MAINSPRING'.  
Train: All the brass wheels are gilded, the centre, third and fourth wheels with five crossings, the steel escape wheel with four. A high quality train with undercuts on the pinion heads, even on the escape pinion.
Jewelling: Jewelled bearings from the third wheel onwards, all the jewels pressed in. Kif shock resistant jewelling to the balance. The pivots of the first wheel in the self winding train are also jewelled.
Escapement: A straight line layout club tooth lever escapement with short lever, double roller and D-shaped impulse pin. The exposed pallet stones have flat acting faces. A steel escape wheel with four crossings.
A mixed escapement.
No. of teeth embraced 3½.
Balance & Spring: a three arm monometallic balance without screws. Balance diameter 10.95 mm., thickness 0.63 mm. A 'self compensating' flat spiral spring with 12 turns.
Means of Regulation: An index on the balance cock registers against a divided scale which is engraved 'FS AR'.
Train Counts and Beat Rate:
Great wheel 96 (barrel)
Centre wheel 72 pinion 12
Third wheel 60 pinion 9
Fourth wheel 70 pinion 8
Escape wheel 15 pinion 7
Beat rate 18,000
Motion work: cannon pinion 12
minute wheel 32, minute pinion 8
hour wheel  36
The Up-and-Down Mechanism: This is part of the stopwork that prevents overwinding. It consists of an L shaped sector with 15 teeth on the inside of the lower limb. The pinion of 10 that meshes with this limb carries the up and down disc. As the watch is being wound the L shaped piece is moved by a lever P set below it. This lower lever carries a pin which after four turns of winding of the barrel moves into the path of a long flexible stop mounted on the weight. This prevents the weight from moving until such time as the watch has run down enough to clear the lever pin from the path of the stop lever.
The Self Winding Work: In one respect this watch is a reversion to the Harwood as like the Harwood its weight is pivoted on both sides of the movement. The weight swings through less than 90 degrees and there are no buffers mounted on the ends of the weight in the conventional way. Instead there are spring stops mounted on a plate on the inside of the weight on the dial side. These stops bank on parts of the front plate. The main part of the weight has a lead insert to increase the available torque. The weight winds in both directions and carries a wheel of 60 teeth which drives reversing pinions. These pinions of 30 and are the first stage in the geared reduction system between the weight and the barrel.  A ratchet wheel of 100 teeth is driven by the reversing pinions. A pinion of 12 leaves is mounted on this ratchet wheel and this a wheel of 62 which has another pinion of 12 mounted on it, this pinion drives the barrel ratchet wheel of 70.
Hand Set System: There is no way of winding this watch manually, such is the faith that the makers had in the efficiency of the self winding work. To set the hands the button on the case back is pushed towards the centre. In this position not only can the hands be set but a balance stop comes into play, stopping the balance.
Dimensions: Case diam. 35.0 mm., height 11.5 mm., over the glass. 
Movement: diameter 31.7 mm, height 7.8 mm. 
Provenance: Purchased in 1988 with the aid of funds from the Dingwall Bequest.
Note:
(1) A system invented by A. L. Breguet.
 
See an article by R. W. Pipe in 'The Watchmaker Jeweller and Silversmith' August 1952 pp.76-79.
The workmanship in this watch is of the highest standard.

Description: Sherbet glass, 'Knickerbocker'; clear glass, free-blown bowl with mould-blown and cut square base.;
Producer name: Designed by: Arthur Douglas Nash;  Factory of: Libbey Glass Company
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: Libbey Inscription note: Acid-etched in a circle.;
Curators Comments:
In set with 1988,0608.5-6
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no.220c.
Arthur Douglas Nash was the son of Arthur J. Nash, an Englishman who worked in Stourbridge before emigrating in 1892 to the USA, where he ran L.C. Tiffany's glass studios until 1919.
     From 1919 A.D. Nash ran the Tiffany Furnaces until their closure in 1928, when he formed his own firm, the A. Douglas Nash Corporation, but this too closed in 1931. He was then hired by Libbey to create a new line of luxury glassware. The series was formally introduced in 1933, with a catalogue and exhibition at the Waldorf Astoria Hotel in New York. Advertisements appear in Arts and Decoration, New York 40, November and December 1933. The Libbey-Nash series comprised some eighty new patterns, but much of it proved too expensive; it was hit by the Depression and admitted a failure after two years. Nash left Libbey in 1935 and made no further designs for glass.
    The complete 'Knickerbocker' service comprised a 12-oz tumbler, 10-oz tumbler, 6-oz sherbet, 4-oz cocktail and 1-oz cordial glass. It was a custom-built line - i.e. not regularly carried in stock - and so could be decorated at the factory with the purchaser's personal monogram, at an extra charge. The service was still in production in 1942, with the addition of a sugar bowl, cream pitcher and salt dips, not designed by Nash (C.U. Fauster, Libbey Glass since 1918', Toledo 1979. 398, reprint of Libbey 1942 Modern American Glassware Catalogue). As the sales blurb rightly says, 'the heavy crystal block gives each item perfect balance and a delightful "feel"'.
     Glasses with solid square bases were produced by other American firms in the early 1930s, notably the ranges designed by W.D. Teague for Steuben (Brooklyn 1986, The Brooklyn Museum, 'The Machine Age in America', 329, pl. 8.81) and by G. Sakier for Fostoria (see The Studio Yearbook, 1931, 149). Both these ranges were stemmed. The idea of placing the bowl directly on to the square base was already current in France, since the Paris Exhibition of 1925, when Baccarat exhibited a service with flared bowls on square bases designed by G. Chevalier. Later on, Jean Luce designed a straight-sided, square-based, clear-crystal service very similar to the Nash glasses. For illustrations of the Luce service, see Mobilier et Decoration, Sèvres 1934, 179 and 196, and Design for Today, 111, London June 1935, 212. For a square-based decanter and glasses designed by S. Gate for Orrefors, Sweden, see Design for Today 11,1934, 371.
