Description: Painted, smoked skin shirt, with skin tassles along the hems, decorated with large beaded rosettes to chest and back bearing designs possibly representing Thunderbirds. Further beadwork arranged in geometric forms, can be observed as thick bands running across each shoulder; a tassled fringe formed from skin and bound with porcupine quill has been applied along one edge on each of these bands. A strip of red woolen cloth forms a collar.;
Curators Comments:
Red Crow (about 1830-1900) was head chief of the Kainai, or Blood, tribe of the Blackfoot Confederacy from 1870. He was involved in thirty-three raids against neighbouring peoples, including the Crows, and Plains Crees. Later, in 1874, he welcomed the arrival of the North-West Mounted Police to restore order. Early on he recognized the awful reality of the disappearance of buffalo in 1879-80, and became a farmer growing grain and vegetables. In 1894 he accepted an offer from the government to exchange prized horses for fifteen cattle, part of a programme that led to the establishment of ranching among the Blood in southern Alberta.
Red Crow remained loyal to Canada during the North-West Rebellion in 1885. The Prime Minister, John A. MacDonald, rewarded him and other loyal chiefs with a tour of eastern Canada. This shirt was probably collected during this tour. It is decorated with great beaded rosettes on the chest and back, possibly representing Thunderbirds, and with beaded panels on the arms, and hair fringes.
He also visited the Mohawk Institute near Brantford, Ontario, and returned to the Blood Reserve a strong proponent of education for Indians. Although baptised a Roman Catholic, he retained his Native religion and spent the last years of his life fighting the attempted suppression of the Sun Dance.
The Kainai, or Bloods, gave the historic name of Red Crow to the Duke of Windsor in 1919 and to the present Prince of Wales in 1977.
The secondary education college is named after Red Crow, and a bronze statue of him wearing this shirt stands in the capital of the Kainai Reserve, Stand Off.; This shirt has appeared in a detailed study by Colin Taylor ('Saam, the symbolic content of early northern plains ceremonial regalia' Verlag fur Amerikanistik, 1999) who interpreted the circular beaded flap as being a stylised image of a thunderbird. (Max Carocci, 27/11/2008)

Description: Self-demarcation signboard from the Munduruku community of Sawré Muybu (middle Tapajós River) made of wood, painted with white and red paint. The inscription, which covers three quarters of the sign, is red on a white background. On the left of the inscription is a depiction of the mythical Munduruku warrior Karodaybi holding a trophy head on a pole, painted in red and white on an plain wooden background.;
Producer name: Made by: José Akay Munduruku (Sawre Muybu community)
Production place: Made in: Tapajós, River (Sawré Muybu village (Territory Daje Kapap Eypi))
Materials: wood (Wood from a fallen Cedrorana tree (false cedar))
Inscription:
Inscription type: inscription Inscription quoted: IPI WUYXI UBUYXIM 
IKUKAP DAJE KAPAP EYPI 
IECUG'AP KARODAYBI
Terra Mãe temos respeito
Território Daje Kapap Eypi
[in Munduruku and Portuguese languages] Inscription translation: The earth is our mother, we should look after her
This is [the sacred place] where the peccaries passed 
Under Karodaybi's government
Mother-Earth, we respect
Daje Kapap Eypi Territory;
Curators Comments:
Amazonian peoples have long claimed that the global fight against climate change is inseparable from the Indigenous struggle for territorial rights. Confronting land encroachment and violence, Indigenous communities have stood at the forefront of environmental conservation. In the absence of state action to secure their rights, many have undertaken the auto-demarcation of their territories to safeguard their lifeways and the sustainable use of natural resources.
For decades, the Munduruku have fought for the official recognition and protection of their ancestral territory along the Tapajós River (Brazil). This signboard was one of the first used to mark their territorial boundaries in response to the impact of illegal logging, mining, and hydroelectric dams. It shows ancestral warrior Karodaybi with a trophy head, invoking Munduruku’s long history of resistance.

Description:
Watch.
Duplex escapement; skeletonised plates and dial.
Metal-gilt dial.
Nickel plated case; glazed both sides.;
Producer name: Made by: New England Watch Company
Inscription: Inscription type: signature Inscription quoted: N.E.W.Co.;
Curators Comments:
Text from 'Watches', by David Thompson, London, 2008, p. 138-139.
New England Watch Company
NICKEL SILVER CASED DUPLEX WATCH 
WATERBURY, CONNECTICUT, 1912
SIGNED: 'N.E.W. CO.'
The origins of the mass-produced watch can be traced back to the end of the eighteenth century, when Frédéric Japy in Beaucourt, France, began to develop the idea of machine production of components. Improvements were made when the Swiss-born Pierre Frédéric Ingold brought his concepts of machine production to England in the 1840s. However, Ingold was effectively forced out of business and went to America, where the idea had already been tried with some success by Henry and James Pitken in East Hartford, Connecticut. Ultimately it was to be Aaron Lufkin Dennison, a clockmaker and jeweller, who refined the process of machine production in America, initially concentrating on making watch cases. Having achieved some success in this field, in 1849 he turned his attention to manufacturing watch movements, founding a company in Roxbury, Massachusetts, in partnership with Samuel Curtis, David Porter Davis and the clock manufacturer Edward Howard. In 1851, under the name of the American Horologe Company, their first machine-made watch movements came off the production line. While their initial attempts failed and the partnership broke up in 1857, the company was sold off to become Appleton Tracy & Company, which went on to successfully produce watches and in 1859 evolved into the American Waltham Watch Company. Aaron Dennison did not give up, and eventually found fame and fortune as a manufacturer of mass-produced watch cases, first in America and later in Birmingham, England, where his business supplied millions of watch cases to the American, English and Swiss trades. The New England Watch Company began with a firm of brass goods manufacturers founded in 1812 by Aaron Benedict, who saw a business opportunity in making brass buttons in Waterbury, Connecticut, for the US Army. As the company expanded it moved into the area of clockmaking, and in 1857 the Waterbury Clock Company was formed. Some thirty years later the company diversified and began producing watches. Their first model was the famous long-wind watch, designed by Daniel A. Buck, which was similar to the Auburndale rotary watch (registration no. 1958,1201.244), with a movement that rotated once per hour. From 1880 their watches sold in vast numbers for just $3.50 and were immediately successful. In 1892 the Waterbury Clock Company received an order from Robert H. Ingersoll & Bro. for 12,000 small clock movements destined to be cased as watches and sold by mail order. By 1898, however, the Waterbury Company was planning to re-organize and from that crisis emerged the New England Watch Company with a capital of $600,000. The new company specialized in making cheap mass-produced watches with duplex escapements similar to the one seen here, although, unlike this one, they were generally made with solid plates and a solid dial and case back. The company continued in business with declining success until, on 17 July 1912, it was declared bankrupt. Within two years the plant and stock had been purchased by Robert H. Ingersoll & Bro., who continued to manufacture millions of watches for a retail price as low as $1.
This open-face duplex watch is of a less common type as it has a skeletonized movement and dial, which allows the mechanism to be seen from both sides through glasses at the front and back. The components are machine-made, some of them simply pressed from brass sheet, and even the duplex escape wheel is produced in the flat with alternate teeth bent up to provide the duplex function. The case is made from nickel-plated base metal; turning the pendant button winds the watch and pushing it in and turning sets the hands. The Waterbury Duplex and its New England Watch Company successors are now highly collectable, despite being made in their millions.
Bib references: Thompson 2008 / Watches

Description: Sledge made of bone, ivory, sealskin, wood, sinew, with wooden dog whip;
Find spot: Found/Acquired: Baffin Bay;  Found/Acquired: North Greenland
Materials: bone (whale and walrus bone (Vitale and Grønnow, 2024)); ivory (caribou antler and walrus ivory (Vitale and Grønnow, 2024)); seal skin; sinew; wood
Curators Comments:
From Emma Vitale who studied the sled in 2024, paper published with co-author Bjarne Grønnow, Of Skin and Bones: Revisiting an Inughuit Dogsled Procured by John Ross in 1818:
The exhibited sled is not identical to the one shown in Ross’s account. Our investigation revealed that the sled consists of 89 different components made from whale and walrus bones, caribou antler, and a few pieces of wood. Furthermore, we discovered a previously undiscovered artefact; a dog whip mounted on the sled. The whip is made from local wood, features a finely shaped lash, and has a distinctive "knob" at the base end. These findings highlight the Inughuit people's technological ingenuity and strategic use of available materials during a period of isolation.; From 'The Times', 15 December 1818, p2 column 4:
"The dogs brought by the ships of the Northern Expedition, have for some days past been objects of much attraction at the British Museum. On Friday, one of the poor inoffensive animals was killed in the grounds belonging to that national establishment, for the purpose of its skin being stuffed and placed in the rooms; and the other has been sent to the Museum at Paris, probably for the same purpose. Of the four that remained at the Admiralty, two have been sent to the Duchess of YORK, and the other two to Sir JOSEPH BANKS. The fates of the four latter will be more congenial to the feelings of humanity than those of their companions.-Courier"; From 'The Times', 8 December 1818, p.2 column 4. 
"Yesterday morning, the curiosities, &c., brought from Baffins-bay, by Captain Ross, were landed at Whitehall-stairs, from the boats of the Isabella and Alexander discovery ships. There are four dogs and two bitches, the former larger than the latter; three are almost black, one black and white, and two brownish, and appear nearly of the same species as those of Kamschatka, with heads and tails resembling a fox; their skins exceedingly thick, with long hair; they are very docile, and represented as of great strength. Among the curiosities was an amazingly large skin of white bear, about 7 feet in length: a sledge of bone, about 5 feet long and 2 high, with the whip, &c. used by the newly-discovered inhabitants; specimens of mineralogy and botany, and some very remarkable star-fish. The whole of the productions were conveyed to the British Museum, for the inspection of the public."; See Eth Doc 1949 for the 1986 analysis at Kew of the 3 pieces of wood at Kew: two are of Salix or Populus sp; one is of Picea or Larix sp.

Description: Smoking pipe (with carved bird and figures) made of argillite.;
Curators Comments:
The earliest argillite pipes tend to incorporate fewer figures than the later ones, which are usually more complicated, with figures separated by spaces pierced through the pipe. While the early pipes are often oval or round, and rather heavy, the later ones are longer and tend to have rectangular rather than oval cross sections. As Haida artists developed their skill in carving argillite the pipe forms became less and less functional. In particular, the pipe bowls became smaller, although both the bowl and the flue are always present.
The way in which a traditional sculptural form was altered is illustrated by the use of the extended tounge. The tounge, extending from the mouth of one creature into that of another, is a common motif on traditional artefacts and signifies the transference of spiritual power. In use on elaborate panel pipes the extended tounge became a functional method of joining together figures . This enabled the artist to cut out figures from the surrounding material, Thus demonstrating his technical skill in carving a fragile stone.

Description: Smoking pipe, carved with a man and a bird, with eyes inlaid with haliotis, made of stone (argillite), blackshale, shell (abalone).;
Curators Comments:
The oldest argillite carvings are probably the pipes recovered archaeologically on Haida Gwaii (formerly the Queen Charlotte Islands). While these pipes are undated, others are known to have been accessioned into museums in New England before 1820.
Argillite, called Kwawhlahl in the Haida language, is mined from one quarry on Slatechuck Mountain on Graham Island. It was the people of nearby Skidegate who began carving argillite, and it was their chief who controlled access to the quarry. By the middle of the nineteenth century people from other villages, such as Masset and Kiusta, had also started to produce argillite carvings. After the beginning of the twentieth century the number of carvers declined until the 1950s. Since then there has been a great revivial with a wide variety of traditional and non-traditional forms being produced. Perhaps the most common of these are small pendants and model totem poles. In 1941 the quarry was set aside as a reserve for use by all Haida.
Bib references: King 1977 / Smoking pipes of the North American Indian

Description: Smoking pipe in the form of two figures, made of wood.;
Curators Comments: McEwan 2009, p.47 Wooden Pipe, Iroquois, New York, USA, AD 1700-1725 Just as the paccha opposite promotes the circulation of essential life energies in liquid form, so too this pipe channels spirit energies made visible in the curling wreaths of tobacco smoke. Like drinking, smoking was a collective ritual designed to cement alliances, and pipes themselves were sometimes gifted as a mark of respect. They are often carved with powerful images of ancestral clan leaders and probably became revered heirlooms. In this instance the smoker would face the embodied image of his ancestor and inhale smoke through the body of the figure. During communal smoking ceremonies the seated figure at the end of the pipe would face inward towards the circle of similarly seated participants.

Description: Wooden smoking-pipe with integral stem and bowl.;
Curators Comments:
Collected by Thomas Dobson who was midshipman and interpreter in Spanish on Captain George Vancouver's voyage (in HMS Discovery) of 1791-5 on the Northwest Coat of America. Dobson died in 1805, and his collection remained in his family until it was sold at Sotheby's. This pipe was purchased by the BM on 27/6/1983, lot 64, and was the only item purchased by the BM from the sale. 
See J C H King, ‘Vancouver’s Ethnography’, in the Journal of the History of Collections, V 1993, p.52, for a list of the items from his collection formed on the Vancouver voyage and sold at auction in 1983-4. 
ADD The Sotheby's sale on the 27th June 1983 was devoted solely to the sale of the Prince Sadruddin Aga Khan Collection of African Art and the catalogue makes no mention of any American items.There may, however, have been a second sale on that same day of other ethnographic objects.
Location: On display (Room 26 - North America, Display Case 4)
Exhibition history: 2011 2 Mar-Present, BM Room 26; Gallery of North America, Case: "The West Coast"
Acq notes (acq): Collected on George Vancouver's North Pacific voyage of 1791-1795.

Description: Zoomorphic, effigy smoking pipe carved, from light coloured pipestone, into the form of an aquatic mammal carrying a fish in its mouth. Substantially re-fabricated from excavated fragments, only the head and fore limbs remain from the original pipe. Facial features are detailed with light incisions.;
Curators Comments:
A small diamond shaped label reads: "673"
Davis in the inventory describes the effigy as "pipe comp(osite) S(and) stone manitou with fish in the mouth. Naturalists represent this animal as herbivorous but the Mound builders were doubtless close observers of its habits". Davis is referring to the manatee or sea cow (he also refers to it as a lamantin). These are herbivorous, as he mentions, and have broad flat tail fins rather the the long thin ones depicted in other effigy pipes also said to represent manitous (S.264 & S263). Therefore it is unlikely to be the species represented here. Stevens in Flint Chips accepts this identification.
Bib references: Stevens 1870 / Flint Chips: A Guide to Prehistoric Archaeology as Illustrated in the Blackmore Museum, Salisbury.

Description: Fragment of an effigy smoking pipe carved into the form of a man's head, heavily tattooed, with one ear surviving bearing a large piercing. Made from a light coloured pipe stone. Above the ear a green patina has developed.;
Curators Comments: McEwan 2009, p. 64 Stone pipe fragment in the shape of a tattooed warrior head. Hopewell culture, Mound City, Ohio, USA 2nd century BC-1st century AD Identifying tattooes are etched deeply into the facial features of this warrior. The one surviving elongated ear is pierced to receive an ear spool. The object was found among some two hundred pipe fragments in an offering cache deposited within one of the earthen platform mounds built by the Hopewell culture. Similar objects have been excavated from burial contexts, where they seem to have been deposited as trophies representing defeated rivals.
Bib references: McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail; Burland 1970 / The People of the Ancient Americas
Location: On display (Room 26 - North America, Display Case 17)

Description: Smoking pipe bowl, of a fine grained grey stone, polished to black, the bowl in clay pipe form of elbow pipe, with a pewter or lead ring inlaid near entrance for stem, the bowl surmounted with a large mammal, four paws on the bowl, head rising above the bowl looking at the smoker, long horizontal tail lying flat along bowl, the animal perhaps a wolf (dog ?).;
Curators Comments: Mohawk attribution suggested by Roland Vlau, Universite de Montreal, August 1995, the wolf clan being one of three. Description: Snow-spectacles or goggles made of wood.;
Curators Comments:
Sir Hans Sloane’s ‘Miscellanea’ catalogue is a bound volume in Central Archives containing seven separate catalogues: ‘Miscellanies’, ‘Antiquities’, ‘Seals’, ‘Pictures’, ‘Mathematical Instruments’, ‘Agate Handles’ and ‘Agate Cups, Bottles, Spoons’. Each contains numbered entries that list and describe objects collected by Sloane between the 1680s and 1750s. Each catalogue begins with object number one.

A piece of wood to cover the eyes & part of the nose to be fastened to the head by beaver straps wt. 2 chinks in the wood to see through. Id."; id referring to Miscellanea Miscellanies1838 "1838. Description: Spear made of wood, wicker, feathers, bone.;
Production place: Made in: Araguaia, River (Santa Isabel do Morro Hawaló community)
Find spot: Found/Acquired: Mato Grosso (state);  Found/Acquired: Araguaia, River
Materials: wood; stem; feather (macaw); bone (deer or jaguar); fibre; glass (light blue beads)
Curators Comments:
This spear was collected during Peter Fleming’s expedition in search for famous British explorer Colonel Percy Fawcett, who went missing in Central Amazonia in 1925 never to be found. The quest for El Dorado and ‘lost cities’ in Amazonia has shaped the region’s exoticism since colonial times and driven its exploration and occupation to this day.
The Iny-Karajá collection has been documented by traditional leaders from the Santa Isabel do Morro Hawaló community, Sokrowé and Ixysé Karajá, in collaboration with researchers Rafael Andrade (Brazil's National Museum), Manuel Lima Filho and 	Lucas Veloso Yabagata (Anthropological Museum of the Federal University of Goiás) as part of the SDCELAR project 'On the banks of the Araguaia:
Refounding Iny-Karajá collections in Brazilian Amazonia'.
Read more about the project here:  https://www.sdcelarbritishmuseum.org/projects/on-the-banks-of-the-araguaia/; Donor, R.G.Pettiward collected these objects on an expedition to find the missing explorer Col.Percy Fawcett who disappeared in 1925 while crossing a tributary of the Amazon. Eth Doc 1359 contains newspaper cuttings of the search expeditions
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 4, Relating to animals)

Description: Spindle shaft made of split palm, gum-resin.;
Curators Comments:
According to Wapichana historian and teacher Roseane Cadete Wapichana, from the Canauani territory (Brazilian Amazonia, 2024), this spindle was used to hand-spin raw cotton into thread for weaving textiles. Traditionally done by women, cotton spinning now survives among only a few knowledgeable weavers. The carved designs on each spindle served to identify its owner.; The Wapichana (Wapishana, Wapisiana, Wapixana) are an Arawak-speaking Amazonian people from souther Guyana and northern Brazil. The colony of British Guiana (1831-1966) separated Wapichana communities across English and Portuguese-speaking borderlines, leading to their displacement from the territory and waterways that once connected them. 
The Wapichana objects collected by Lionel Hector Tracy Ashburner and acquired by the British Museum are accompanied by a set of documents (Ethnographic Document 1399), including a letter from Ashburner to curator Elizabeth Carmichael (1984), an account of the ‘Wapisiana Indians’, an ‘English-Wapisiana Vocabulary’, and a description of slides from 1930 ‘from Georgetown via the Demerara, Essequibo and Rupununi rivers to Dadanawa Ranch’. 
As part of the British Museum’s Wapichana collection related to traditional weaving practices, this object featured in Ateliê-Lavrado: A Wapichana Residency with the British Museum, a collaborative project developed in 2023 with Wapichana artist Gustavo Caboco and historian and teacher Roseane Cadete from Brazil, the Santo Domingo Centre of Excellence for Latin American Research (SDCELAR) and Jamille Pinheiro Dias (CLACS/University of London). As the first Wapichana representatives to visit the collection at the British Museum, Caboco and Cadete conducted collection research and convened a series of community-based conversations and weaving workshops. 
These activities reflected on practices of spinning and threading cotton threads, and catalysed conversations between community members, curators and the wider public about how these materials from collection may open paths of connection and healing between Indigenous communities and museums. For more information on this project, see https://www.sdcelarbritishmuseum.org/projects/atelie-lavrado-a-wapichana-residency/; Hand written notes reads, '4 Wapisiana spinner for spinning cotton made from split palm and tortoise belly shell. Rupununi Savannah'
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 4, Relating to animals)

Acq name (excavator): Field Collection by: L H T Ashburner

The Wapichana objects collected by Lionel Hector Tracy Ashburner and acquired by the British Museum are accompanied by a set of documents (Ethnographic Document 1399), including a letter from Ashburner to curator Elizabeth Carmichael (1984), an account of the ‘Wapisiana Indians’, an ‘English-Wapisiana Vocabulary’, and a description of slides from 1930 ‘from Georgetown via the Demerara, Essequibo and Rupununi rivers to Dadanawa Ranch’. 
As part of the British Museum’s Wapichana collection related to traditional weaving practices, this object featured in Ateliê-Lavrado: A Wapichana Residency with the British Museum, a collaborative project developed in 2023 with Wapichana artist Gustavo Caboco and historian and teacher Roseane Cadete from Brazil, the Santo Domingo Centre of Excellence for Latin American Research (SDCELAR) and Jamille Pinheiro Dias (CLACS/University of London). As the first Wapichana representatives to visit the collection at the British Museum, Caboco and Cadete conducted collection research and convened a series of community-based conversations and weaving workshops. 
These activities reflected on practices of spinning and threading cotton threads, and catalysed conversations between community members, curators and the wider public about how these materials from collection may open paths of connection and healing between Indigenous communities and museums. Description: Staff (with rattle) made of wood, feather.;
Curators Comments:
The hollow tips at the end of these ceremonial spears contain small stones that rattle when the spear is shaken. At funerals, people would shake the spears to summon animal and human ancestors, asking the ancestors to help the deceased reach the next world. Alicia Sánchez and Oscar Román Jitdutjaaño, Murui-Muina elders, studied the stones left inside these rattles. They consider the stones to have come from the bodies of those who have the ability to communicate with spiritual worlds, and that this is how the spears became infused with the same power.
On display Room 24 (WTG) Relating to Animals (Amazonia) from July 2021
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 4, Relating to animals)

Description: Large globular storage jar made of clay, coiled, painted with orange and black slips, the design including two birds.;
Curators Comments:
For the published record see: F H Harlow: Two Hundred Years of Historic Pueblo Pottery: The Gallegos Collection. Santa Fe: Morning Star Gallery, 1990, cover and plate 53.
Harlow's description runs as follows: 'Zia Polychrome Storage Jar ca 1930, height: 16 in.  ///  The achievement of a most handsome effect is certainly possible with the newly-developed style in the 1920s of an overall orange-tan background slip. This superb jar illustrates the point perfectly.  ///  The jar itself is constructed in traditional fashion, with a high-shouldered form that is the standard after about 1900. The underbody is stone-polished red, and lacks any sign of red banding, which was consistently omitted after about 1930. Also omitted from this jar are any breaks in the encircling framing lines.  ///  Wide red bands form the dwelling places for the birds. They are neatly double-edged in black, with a precision that characterizes all the artwork on this fine, large jar. Around the walls of each dwelling place are feather symbols of two types, an older style that hugs the upper walls, and a newer style characteristic of the 1920s and 1930s, formed here of red-bodied feather clusters at the lower corners. The birds are typical Zia non-parrots, but what species they depict is uncertain. The older style of line breaks between tail feathers is here ignored.  ///  The grand sweep of design structure and the contrasting warm and intense black colors are the work of an extraordinary artist whose name is presently unknown. The overall effect demonstrates the consumate mastery of ceramics that continued to be achievable at Zia until recent times, and survives even to the present in a few exceptional potters at that village.  ///  Despite the handsome potential for orange-tan slip demonstrated by this jar, its use was not especially popular at Zia after about 1940. Perhaps the concern was the lack of striking contrast and consequently eye-catching appeal for the buyer.
Bib references: Harlow 1990 / 200 Years of Historic Pueblo Pottery: the Gallegos Collection

Description: Tankard; silver and oxidised silver, in the Japanese taste, decorated with applied silver motifs of figures, bamboo and other plants, the background oxidised to a deep grey-black colour; the handle with classical-style palmettes.;
Curators Comments:
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no. 351 (appendix)
The original design drawing for this tankard (Fig. 16) survives in the Tiffany archive in New York; shape number 2834 was put into production between 1870 and 1874, while the decoration was designed on 15 December 1874 (indicated by the decoration number '296' on the drawing). This is an early example of Tiffany silver in the Japanese taste, hence the combination of Japanese and classical-style motifs, and an exceptional survival of an original oxidised silver surface, intended to imitate Japanese shakudo grounds.
Additional text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A Catalogue of the British Museum Collection'. 2nd ed. 1994. Addenda.
For further discussion of oxidised silver by Tiffany and others, see J. Rudoe, 'Oxidised silver in the nineteenth century; the documentary evidence', in S. La Niece and P. Craddock (eds), 'Metal Plating and Patination. Cultural, technical & historical developments', Oxford 1993, 161-70. Col. pl. 14.2. Cultural, technical & historical developments',.  Oxford 1993, pp. 161-70. Col. pl. 14.2.

Title: Town and Country
Description: Teapot; from 'Town and Country' dinner service; earthenware, cast; of asymmetrical ovoid form with pale orange glaze; the upper surface slopes at an angle and the lid is placed off centre with rounded and shaped handle designed to fit the thumb.;
Producer name: Designed by: Dr Eva Zeisel;  Factory of: Red Wing Pottery
Curators Comments:
In set with 1989,0107.2-3; 1989,1104.1; 1990,1202.1-4.
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no. 333.
Zeisel was born in Budapest, where she studied briefly at the Royal Academy of Art and was apprenticed to the Hungarian potters' guild before setting up her own pottery in 1925. She then worked for the Kispester Pottery in Budapest, joining the Schramberger Majolika Fabrik in the Black Forest, Germany, in 1928. There she produced geometric designs of great inventiveness and individuality. From 1930 to 1931 she worked in Berlin, leaving in 1932 for the Soviet Union, where she worked initially for the Lomonossov factory in Leningrad and then for the Dulevo factory near Moscow.
     In 1939 Zeisel settled in New York, where she taught ceramic design at the Pratt Institute, Brooklyn, but it was her formal porcelain 'Museum Service' designed c. 1942-5 for the Museum of Modern Art and made by Castleton China that brought her widespread recognition. As a result she received the commission from Red Wing for an informal, inexpensive earthenware service named 'Town and Country' after the popular magazine. It was intended to be humorous, even eccentric: the shapes are more symmetrical than Russel Wright's 'American Modern' service (see Decorative Arts 1850-1950, Cat. 319-25), the lids could be in a different colour from the body of the vessel, while the salt and pepper shakers were photographed by the artist to suggest a mother and children or a whole family of friendly creatures (Montreal 1984, Musée des Arts Décoratifs, 'Eva Zeisel: Designer for Industry', exhibition catalogue, 39-40, fig. 58). The large and small forms were thus intended to be used together. The service was produced from 1947. For further discussion and bibliography, see the essay by Martin Eidelberg in  Montreal 1984.
Information supplementary to Rudoe 1991/1994:
For the most recent discussion of the 'Town and Country' service see Pat Kirkham (ed), 'Eva Zeisel: Life,  Design, and Beauty', San Francisco 2013, pp. 74-79.
Bib references: Rudoe 1991a / Decorative Arts 1850-1950: A Catalogue of the British Museum Collection.; Rudoe 1994 / Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A Catalogue of the British Museum Collection
Location: On display (Room 48 - Europe 1900 to the present, Display Case 6)

Title: Monumentos y Cartas - Miguel Grau
Description: Artist book; calico, cotton and metallic thread.;
Curators Comments:
This artwork is presented as a traditional Peruvian cuaderno escolar (school notebook). The material used is screen-printed fabric, fabric and thread sewn together in the shape of a notebook. The school notebook’s front cover shows a red map of Peru on a blue background surrounded by people performing particular jobs: they are working towards the construction of the country, symbolised by its map – the map is made of red bricks, a metaphorical message showing that the development and building of Peru depend on the work of its people.  
 
This idea of development is reinforced on the back cover, which presents a list of the Decálogo del Desarrollo – the 10 Commandments for Development. Words such as order, responsibility, honour, and respect are placed above the oración del decálogo (prayer of the commandments), a patriotic prayer that strengthens the message that Peru will only develop and prosper as a country if these commandments are followed. The white rectangular space on the notebook front cover reads “Monumentos – Miguel Grau Seminario”. 
 
Miguel Grau Seminario was a Peruvian naval officer who is considered the hero of the naval Battle of Angamos, during the War of the Pacific (1879-1884), where he died. He is a popular persona in Peru, known as Caballero de los Mares – Gentleman of the Seas, and received in 1999 the title “Peruvian of the Millennium”. The Peruvian Navy regards him as the greatest example of Peruvian dignity. 
 
In this artwork, Apaza embroiders excerpts of letters that Grau wrote to his wife, Dolores Cabero y Nuñez, from his ship Huáscar, just months before his death. These excerpts surround black and white images of photographs of sculptures and public monuments dedicated to Grau – they are photocopies of images that were screen-printed on the fabric. The content of these letters selected by the artist portrays the man behind the hero. They are caring letters that demonstrate his ways of thinking about life, the horrors of war, and his care for his children.
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 3, Living with sea and land)

Title: Shitikari
Description: Textile; made of cotton; white cotton textile printed with abstract representation of the night sky.;
Production place: Made in: Venezuela;  Made in: Orinoco, River (Pori Pori community, Upper Orinoco)
Curators Comments:
Yanomami artist Sheronawe Hakihiiwe maps the night sky, arranging star constellations within a celestial grid that may be seen through the clouds, here represented by white cotton. For the Yanomami, the sky embodies the cosmic balance between all beings (living and spiritual), which is continuously threatened by depredatory actions. Among the largest in Amazonia, the Yanomami people live across the Brazil-Venezuela border.
This artwork previously featured in the exhibition 'Le jour des esprits est notre nuit' at the Centre Rhénan d'Art Contemporain (CRAC) Alsace in France, in 2019.
This acquisition was made possible by the generosity of the Santo Domingo family, especially Alejandro and Charlotte Santo Domingo, together with Mrs Julio Mario Santo Domingo, Andrés Santo Domingo, and Lauren Santo Domingo.; ‘The sky is big and the stars are in order. At night, when there are clouds, only some stars can be seen through the open spaces left by the clouds. When there are no clouds, however, you can see all the stars. We do not have names for each and every one of the stars, we call all of them Shitikari.’ Sheroanawë Hakihiiwë
In this quote, Hakihiiwë refers to the cotton fabric he has used to create this artwork which, like the clouds, is ephemeral and diaphanous. The star constellations in this work are bounded by a series of squares emphasising Yanomami ancestral cosmological order. 
In Yanomami cosmology, a giant cosmic snake covered in spots encapsulates the universe: its past, present, and future. These designs, though abstract and schematic, are maps of lived and spiritual worlds, as well as an evocative expression of the individual experience of looking at the night sky and, in this way, renews traditional Yanomami designs. 
See:
Hakihiiwë, Sheroanawë. 2022. “Shitikari (Starscape)” in Mapping a New Museum: Politics and Practice of Latin American Research at the British Museum (ed. Osorio Sunnucks and Cooper), 343-345. London: Routledge.
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 4, Relating to animals)

Title: Quipus
Description: Artist book; calico, cotton thread.;
Curators Comments:
Quipus is an embroidered textile artwork that imitates a traditional Peruvian cuaderno escolar (school notebook). The material used is screen-printed fabric and thread sewn together in the shape of a notebook. The school notebook’s front cover shows a red map of Peru on a blue background surrounded by people performing particular jobs: they are working towards the construction of the country, symbolised by its map – the map is made of red bricks, a metaphorical message showing that the development and building of Peru depend on the work of its people. This idea of development is reinforced on the back cover, which presents a list of the Decálogo del Desarrollo – the 10 Commandments for Development. Words such as order, responsibility, honour, and respect are place above the oración del decálogo, a patriotic prayer that strengthens the message that Peru will only prosper as a country if these commandments are followed. The name Quipus is written on the white rectangular space of the notebook front cover, together with the artist’s name.  
 
The Quechua word quipus means “knots”, which is believed to be a register or story told in knots that the ancient Wari and Inca used. These are made with distinct codes of different shapes, types, and positioning of the knots.  
The pages of the notebooks are, therefore, filled with knots made of thread. These knots are arranged as the outline of the map of Peru, which is replicated on each page, from beginning to end. What changes throughout the book is the shape, colour, type, and position of the knots. Around the map and the knots, the artist embroiders her narrative that intertwines stories that are both personal and collective. 
 
If the ancient Wari and Inca used quipus to register information such as the censuses, taxes, calendar and historical events, Apaza Mamani makes a political use of the knots to present contemporary statistics about Peru. Her narrative interweaves her personal memory with that of the country: for instance, by including details about her father, who was a history teacher that died at age 67, while the average life expectation in Peru is of 76.5 years; and the fact that 429,000 children were born in 1979 in Arequipa, pointing out to the rapid birth-rate increase. Or that in 1985, they did not have drinkable water and 3.6 million families lived in extreme poverty. The artist embroiders the quipus on the maps in ways that resembles roads or rivers, evoking maps that presents social-political information about the country.
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 3, Living with sea and land)

Description: Spun thread (on spindle) made of cotton.;
Curators Comments: According to Wapichana historian and teacher Roseane Cadete Wapichana, from the Canauani territory (Brazilian Amazonia, 2024), this spindle was used to hand-spin raw cotton into thread for weaving textiles. Traditionally done by women, cotton spinning now survives among only a few knowledgeable weavers. The carved designs on each spindle served to identify its owner.; Hand written notes reads, '4 Wapisiana spinner for spinning cotton made from split palm and tortoise belly shell. Rupununi Savannah'

Description:
Gold minaudiere (vanity case) the lid engraved with flower sprays and set with ruby and diamond flower heads. Inside are boxes for powder and cigarettes, lipstick case, scent bottle, comb and mirror. Contained in original black silk carrying case lined with black suede and stamped inside in gilt letters: 
VAN CLEEF & ARPELS INC. 
744 FIFTH AVENUE
NEW YORK  PALM BEACH
The case has two handles of different lengths, but no fastening: the shorter handle loops over the longer one to secure the box inside.;
Producer name: Made by: Van Cleef & Arpels (and designed by)
Dimensions: 92.00 millimetres minaudiere ;  154.00 millimetres minaudiere ;  165.00 millimetres carrying case ;
Curators Comments: Large vanity cases such as this were used instead of a handbag in the 1930s. Van Cleef & Arpels called them minaudieres from the French minauder, to simper. For a similar minaudière of 1948 in the Van Cleef & Arpels collection, see F. Falluel et al, 'Van Cleef & Arpels', exhibition catalogue, Musée de la Mode et du Costume, Paris 1992, cat. 265, p. 156. For other similar examples with coloured stones on an engraved ground of the late 1940s and early 1950s, see S. Raulet, 'Van Cleef & Arpels', New York1986, pp. 286-7., and S. Coffin, 'Set in Style. The Jewelry of Van Cleef & Arpels', exhibition book, Cooper-Hewitt National Design Museum, New York, London 2011, p. 94

Description:
Register 1951:
Steel combined tomahawk and pipe with a wooden haft.;
Curators Comments:
This tomahawk is very likely photographed with Chief Waubuno of Delaware taken in the studio of Robert Richardson, 66 New Bridge Street, Newcastle-on-Tyne,  in the 1880s. Chief Waubuno, a descendent of Delaware Chief Waubuno who signed the Treaty of 1682 with Penn, is standing in the photo, wearing a buckskin shirt with two metals, a beaded satchel, and a tomahawk in his left hand. The younger Waubuno was a member of the Anglican Church and traveled to England several times, advocating termperance.; Register 1951:
Steel combined tomahawk & pipe, with a wooden haft, pewter work on mouthpiece.
Said to have been formerly the property of the Delaware chief Waubuno, who signed the treaty of 1682 with Wm Penn Given by Waubuno's grandson to Mr Milligan who gave it to the New England Co.

Description: Totem pole carved of wood. At the head of the pole is an eagle, followed by a human intertwined with a killer whale. Below them is the face and front limbs of a wolf. Description: Tumbler, 'Knickerbocker'; clear glass, free-blown bowl with mould-blown and cut square base.;
Producer name: Designed by: Arthur Douglas Nash;  Factory of: Libbey Glass Company
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: Libbey Inscription note: Acid-etched in a circle.;
Curators Comments:
In set with 1988,0608.5
In set with 1988,0608.7
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no.220b.
Arthur Douglas Nash was the son of Arthur J. Nash, an Englishman who worked in Stourbridge before emigrating in 1892 to the USA, where he ran L.C. Tiffany's glass studios until 1919.
    From 1919 A.D. Nash ran the Tiffany Furnaces until their closure in 1928, when he formed his own firm, the A. Douglas Nash Corporation, but this too closed in 1931. He was then hired by Libbey to create a new line of luxury glassware. The series was formally introduced in 1933, with a catalogue and exhibition at the Waldorf Astoria Hotel in New York. Advertisements appear in Arts and Decoration, New York 40, November and December 1933. The Libbey-Nash series comprised some eighty new patterns, but much of it proved too expensive; it was hit by the Depression and admitted a failure after two years. Nash left Libbey in 1935 and made no further designs for glass.
     The complete 'Knickerbocker' service comprised a 12-oz tumbler, 10-oz tumbler, 6-oz sherbet, 4-oz cocktail and 1-oz cordial glass. It was a custom-built line - i.e. not regularly carried in stock - and so could be decorated at the factory with the purchaser's personal monogram, at an extra charge. The service was still in production in 1942, with the addition of a sugar bowl, cream pitcher and salt dips, not designed by Nash (C.U. Fauster, Libbey Glass since 1918', Toledo 1979. 398, reprint of Libbey 1942 Modern American Glassware Catalogue). As the sales blurb rightly says, 'the heavy crystal block gives each item perfect balance and a delightful "feel"'.
     Glasses with solid square bases were produced by other American firms in the early 1930s, notably the ranges designed by W.D. Teague for Steuben (Brooklyn 1986, The Brooklyn Museum, 'The Machine Age in America', 329, pl. 8.81) and by G. Sakier for Fostoria (see The Studio Yearbook, 1931, 149). Both these ranges were stemmed. The idea of placing the bowl directly on to the square base was already current in France, since the Paris Exhibition of 1925, when Baccarat exhibited a service with flared bowls on square bases designed by G. Chevalier. Later on, Jean Luce designed a straight-sided, square-based, clear-crystal service very similar to the Nash glasses. For illustrations of the Luce service, see Mobilier et Decoration, Sèvres 1934, 179 and 196, and Design for Today, 111, London June 1935, 212. For a square-based decanter and glasses designed by S. Gate for Orrefors, Sweden, see Design for Today 11,1934, 371.
Bib references: Rudoe 1991a / Decorative Arts 1850-1950: A Catalogue of the British Museum Collection.; Rudoe 1994 / Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A Catalogue of the British Museum Collection; Carey 1991 / Collecting the 20th Century
Location: On display (Room 48 - Europe 1900 to the present, Display Case 6)

Description: 'Weed-holder' or vase; sheet copper, hand-wrought in the form of a tapering square-section shaft set on a pyramidal base; at the junction of the base and shaft is a small square section with an indented motif of an oval within a square on each side set at a 45-degree angle to shaft and base.;
Producer name: Designed by: Frank Lloyd Wright;  Workshop of: James A Miller & Bro
Curators Comments:
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no. 316.
Wright's tall copper vases were inspired by his love of long, spindly weeds or dried grasses; they were described as weed-holders by his son: 'Not satisfied with the bric-a-brac of the day, Father designed his own. The copper weed-holders ... are his early creations. Father liked weeds' J.L. Wright, 'My Father who is on Earth', New York 1946, 24). Frank Lloyd Wright himself, writing about his respect for sheet metal as a medium in 1928, described his first meeting with James Miller some thirty years before: 'At that time I designed some sheet copper bowls, slender flower holders and such things, for him' (Architectural Record 64,  October 1928, 334-5).
     It is generally accepted that the weed-holders date from after 1893, when Wright began to practise independently. Contemporary photographs show them to have been in use by 1899 in the house which Wright designed for himself at Oak Park, near Chicago. An early article on the architect's house and studio in The House Beautiful 7, no. 1, December 1899, includes a photograph of weed-holder of this form in the draughting-room. A description of the Oak Park studio in the Architectural Record for June 1900 observed 'some very interesting vessels and flower-holders of sheet copper of Mr Wright's design, always filled with masses of summer bloom or trophies of autumn fields and woods according to the season'. There are a number of other undated photographs of Wright's studio showing the weed-holders in situ, sometimes filled with grasses (see D.A. Hanks, 'The Decorative Designs of Frank Lloyd Wright', New York 1979, pl. 27) or on their own, as in a photograph in the Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York (Fig. 29), Department of Prints and Drawings, Henry Fuermann collection, 1985.1005.17, dating probably from after 1903.1 am grateful to David Kiehl for bringing this to my attention. A photograph included in the 1902 exhibition of the Chicago Architectural Club showed a pair of weed-holders with a cube chair and a statue of the architect's son; the photograph was captioned 'Individual Flower Holder. Made by James A. Miller' (Hanks 1979, pl. 141; the same illustration is erroneously dated 1889 in 'Frank Lloyd Wright, the Early Work', New York 1968).
     Weed-holders of this design were used in other Wright houses of the period; for example, in 1903 in the dining-room of the Susan Lawrence Dana House, Springfield, Illinois (Frank Lloyd Wright, the Early Work, 36). Weed-holders were also used at Taliesin in, Spring Green, Wisconsin, the house which Wright built for himself in 1911 (Hanks 1979, pl. 155).
     A number of examples survive in public and private collections, though the provenance is not always known, one of the problems being that it is not clear whether the weed-holders were custom-made for Wright's houses or whether they were made for stock. Two examples are said to come from Oak Park (Hanks 1979, 70; Sotheby's, New York, 1-2 December 1989, lot 667, from the collection of the architect's son). A third comes from Falling Water, Bear Run, Pennsylvania, built for Edgar Kauffmann senior in the 1930s (Princeton 1972, The Art Museum, Princeton University, 'The Arts & Crafts Movement in America', ed. R.J. Clark, no. 84). A fourth and fifth are in the Frank Lloyd Wright Foundation at Taliesin West, Scottsdale,  Arizona  (Hanks 1979,  70).  An unprovenanced pair was acquired by the Metropolitan Museum of Art in 1985 and a single example by the Museum of the Carnegie Institute, Pittsburgh, in the same year (Carnegie Institute, 'Museum of Art, Carnegie Institute, Collection Handbook', Pittsburgh  1985,  214).  Further  saleroom  examples  include Christie's, New York, 6 June 1988, lot 116; see also London 1985, Fischer Fine Art, 'Frank Lloyd Wright: Architectural Drawings and Decorative Art',.no. 1.
     The Miller workshops executed two other Wright designs in copper, an urn and a lamp, see Princeton 1972, cat. nos 86-7. Wright made further designs for copper objects, apparently not executed, including another tapering vase with pyramidal base and large circular knop (Hanks, pl. 55, drawing in Taliesin archives). For the history of J. Miller & Brothers, see Hanks 1979, 212-13.
Information supplementary to Rudoe 1994:
See also M. Collins, 'Towards Post-Modernism, Design since 1851', London, British Museum, revised ed. 1994, fig. 27.

Description: Vase (stirrup spout; with scene of ceremonial dance) made of pottery.;
Curators Comments: Eth.Doc.1492.; McEwan 2009, p.84 Ritual Runnes, fine-line painting on a ceramic vessel. Moche, Peru, 2nd-8th century Moche fine-line painted vessel feature selected themes that, with slight variations, appear repeatedly on many different pots. Well-known ones, named after the principle activity depicted, include the 'Burial Theme', in which a coffin is shown being placed in a deep shaft tomb, and the 'Presentation Theme', where goblets filled with the blood from a sacrificed captive are presented to a lord on a raised dais. This vessel portrays a line of Ritual Runners processing around the circumference. The runners wear a loincloth or short skirt and each carries a small cloth or bag with the ends pointing upwards. The Runners traverse an undulating desert landscape indicated by heat-tolerant species such as the Tilansia shrubs represented on this vessel. A large bean identifiable as Phaseolus vulgaris is shown prominently in the scene. This was one among a suite of productive crops that flourised in the irrigated valleys and has led scholars to propose that the Runners are engaged in a Ritual Race bearing sprouting beans in their bags as part of seasonal fertility rituals.
Bib references: McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail
Location: On display (Room 1 - Enlightenment, Art & Civilization, Wall Press 40)

Description: Vase (in form of seated man with basket; stirrup spout) made of pottery.;
Curators Comments: Eth.Doc.1492.; McEwan 2009, p.54 Ceramic portrait vessel of a Moche Lord 'Long Nose'. Moche, Peru, 2nd-8th century AD Moche portrait vessels often have distinctive facial traits. This one, nicknamed 'Long Nose', has an unusually long, pointed nose. He has a vertical, white stripe painted from his forehead to his chin, a narrow face and lens-shaped eyes, perhaps intended to emphasise his unusual nose. He wears a headband decorated with diagonal serrated motifs, large square ear ornaments and a multi-stringed necklace with beads of precious stones and shell below which hangs a pectoral ornament. He grasps in his left hand a small bag with a chequerboard design, probably used to hold coca leaves, and in his right hand holds a spatula.

Description: Vase (in form of seated figure) made of pottery. The solemn bearing of this individual and the plain cape knotted at the front suggest that he is a priest. The square plaque may represent a pectoral of shell mosaic set in wood. Attached to his headdress is a pair of crouching pumas or monkeys (the rear legs are visible towards the back of the headdress). His facial adornment may have been painted or tattooed and he wears a string of small shells across his chin.;
Curators Comments: Eth.Doc.1492.; McEwan 2009, p.52 Ceramic Vessel of a priest. Moche, Peru. 2nd-8th century AD Different offices are represented in the Moche social hierachy, ranging from priests and warriors up to ruling lords. Items of costume and ornament provide intriguing clues to their identity, roles and responsibilities. This figure wears a chest ornament with a four-part stepped geometric pattern. His headband is surmounted by a pair of attached monkeys with a chin strap to help keep the assemblage in place. Similar figures wear headbands adorned with birds or felines. A few actual examples have been excavated, which show that these depictions are realistic and accurate portrayals. Monkeys tend to be associated with the 'underworld' - a licentious and unruly realm that exists outside established cultural order with its codes of acceptable behaviour. Beneath a line traced across the chin hangs a row of flies emerging from their pupal cases, perhaps alluding to beliefs about that flies that mysteriously emerge from within corpses that have been left exposed to the elements following sacrifice. These associations help make the case for identifying this figure as a priest charged with mediating between the worlds of the living and the dead.
Bib references: Blurton 1997 / The Enduring Image: Treasures from the British Museum; McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail

Description: Vase, vessel made of pottery, with human head emerging from serpent jaws.;
Curators Comments:
McEwan 1994, p.32
'A human emerging from the jaws of a serpentine dragon is a recurring image in Mesoamerican art. Among their many other connotations, snakes were seen as a metaphor for the umbilical cord connecting new life on the earthly plane with the hidden, spiritual sources of creation. Such images, therefore, evoke ancestral connections and call attention to the threshold dividing the human domain from the invisible but ever-present ancestral spirits.'
Bib references: McEwan 1994 / Ancient Mexico in the British Museum; Fields, Pohl & Lyall 2012 / Children of the Plumed Serpent: the legacy of Quetzalcoatl in Ancient Mexico

Description: Vase; free-blown glass, deep blue, with iridescent surface, inlaid with coloured glass and pulled to form a wave-like pattern in gold, greenish gold, purple and turquoise blue; the rim is folded over at the top.;
Producer name: Designed by: Louis Comfort Tiffany;  Factory of: Louis Comfort Tiffany
Production place: Factory in: Corona;  Factory in: Long Island (New York State)
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: L.C.T. L.602 Inscription note: Etched.;
Curators Comments:
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no. 284.
Louis Comfort Tiffany, the son of Charles Lewis Tiffany (see Decorative Arts 1850-1950, Cat. 282-3), trained as a painter before turning to interior decoration in 1879. In 1892 he formed the Tiffany Glass and Decorating Company, renamed Tiffany Studios in 1902 and remaining in operation until 1938. It was this part of the enterprise that produced the bronze candlesticks and lamps (see Cat. 286-8). Tiffany's first glass factory at Corona, Long Island was formed in 1893 as the Stourbridge Glass Company with Arthur J. Nash (1849-1934), a former manager of a Webb subsidiary in Stourbridge, England, as manager. It was renamed Tiffany Furnaces in 1902 and continued to operate until 1928 (Cat. 284-5).
     Vases with similar decoration are illustrated in a booklet issued by the Tiffany Glass and Decorating Company entitled Tiffany Favrile Glass (a copy of the fifth edition, 1896 and 1899, is in the library of the Metropolitan Museum of Art). The trade name 'favrile' was derived from 'fabrile', a seventeenth-century English word meaning 'belonging to a craftsman or his craft'. Tiffany's lustre decoration was initially produced as an attempt to simulate the iridescence caused by natural decay in ancient glass (Brush and Pencil rx, no. 3, December 1902, 167- 76). The iridescent surface was obtained by applying to the hot glass metallic salts which were absorbed into it to create the lustre, in addition to the coloured glasses which produced the pattern (D. Klein and W. Lloyd eds, 'The History of Glass', London 1984, 211).
     Similar vases were acquired by the Musee des Arts Decoratifs, Paris (G.P. Weisberg, 'Art Nouveau Bing: Paris Stlye 1900', New York 1986, pl. 13) and by the Cincinnati Art Museum in 1897 (R. Koch, 'Louis C. Tiffany's Glass, Bronzes, Lamps', New York 1971, 46-9). A vase of almost identical shape and decoration (numbered G2469), but slightly more flared, was included in the collection of Elton John (sold Sotheby's, London, 7 September 1988, lot 617), mounted on a metal stand. The rim of the vase was also folded over at the top, suggesting that the British Museum vase may have been intended for mounting, perhaps as a lamp base (see The House Beautiful v, no. 4, March 1899,179 and no. 5, April 1899, 224).
     The complex numbering system of Tiffany glass is difficult to interpret; if the system worked out by Koch (1971, 56) is reliable, then this vase was made in 1899. However, Victor Arwas has noted that numbering is no real indication of the year of production as items were often kept in storage for several years before being numbered and sold (V. Arwas, 'Glass. Art Nouveau to Art Deco', London 1987, 324).
     For the role of Siegfried Bing in the promotion of Tiffany glass in Europe, see Weisberg 1986; for further discussion of the reactions to Tiffany glass in Europe, see H. Schaefer, 'Tiffany's Fame in Europe' The Art Bulletin 44, December 1962, 309-28. For further contemporary accounts, see The Studio 11, August 1897, 156-65; 14, June 1898,16-20; 17, June 1899, 39-44 (description of the exhibition of Tiffany glass and metalwork at the Grafton Galleries, London, organised by Bing. See also The House Beautiful VII, no. 5, April 1900, listing the different varieties of glass then in production.
Information supplementary to Rudoe 1994:
See also M. Collins, 'Towards Post-Modernism, Design since 1851', London,  British Museum, revised ed. 1994, colour pl. III (left)..

Description: Vase; earthenware; yellow body, heavy hand-thrown form with applied leaves and matt deep green glaze; the veins of the leaves are modelled in high relief and the yellow body clay shows through at the edges, where the leaf curls over; the interior of the neck is glazed matt green, the interior of the body buff.;
Producer name: Designed by: George Prentise Kendrick;  Factory of: Grueby Pottery Company
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: GRUEBY POTTERY BOSTON USA Inscription note: Impressed in a circular mark surrounding a lotus flower.; Inscription type: mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: 88 Inscription note: Factory number impressed.;
Curators Comments:
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no.120.
William Henry Grueby (1867-1925) trained at the Low Art Tile Works before setting up his own architectural faience company, which was incorporated as the Grueby Faience Company in 1897. From 1894 he had begun to develop his matt glazes, which he always called 'enamels' to distinguish them from the clear glazes popular at the time, but initially the matt glazes were applied to architectural decoration. The first display of Grueby art pottery was at the first exhibition of the Society of Arts & Crafts of Boston in 1897. Among the twelve pieces shown were four vases and lamps designed by George Prentise Kendrick (Evans, P., 'Art Pottery of the United States', New York 1987, 121).
     Kendrick was a Boston craftsman who had previously worked in brass and silver; he designed the forms of Grueby art pottery from 1897 to 1901. The modelling and decoration were executed largely by young women graduates from local art schools (Brush and Pencil IX, January 1902, op. cit, 241). Designing always remained the responsibility of a single person, initially Kendrick and later others, which gave the pottery a uniform appearance; the decoration is distinguished by its vertical emphasis. The technique used was peculiar to the Grueby Pottery: the clay was rolled out, applied in thin strips and modelled to shape while the vessel was still damp; it was then biscuit-fired and glazed.
     A closely similar vase, of the same form but with the addition of handles on the upper part, was illustrated in The House Beautiful V, December 1898, 5, suggesting that this model is likely to have been designed at about the same time. It may have been one of the four vases and lamps shown at the 1897 Boston exhibition. Both this and the handled model are known to have been used as both vases and lamps: for an illustration of the handled model with oil-lamp fitting and leaded-glass shade, see Brush and Pencil op. cit, 240. An example of the model without handles with its original bronze oil-lamp fitting and glass shade made by the Boston firm of Bigelow & Kennard was in the possession of Cathers & Dembrovsky Gallery, New York in 1988; in this case the base was pierced between the leaves. L.C. Tiffany & Co. also occasionally made shades for Grueby bases suitable for oil-lamps.
For an example of this vase in a bright yellow glaze, see New York 1987, American Ceramic Arts Society, 'From our Native Clay: Art Pottery from the collections of the American Ceramic Society' ed. M. Eidelberg, pl. 110; for discussion of the variation in quality found in this particular model, see Rago, D., 'Good-Better-Best', Arts & Crafts Quarterly 1, issue 2, January, 1987. 6-7.
     For the influence on Grueby of French potters such as Delaherche, see Eidelberg, M., 'American Ceramics and International Styles 1876-1916', Aspects of the Arts and Crafts Movement in America, Record of the Art Museum of Princeton University,  1975, 49. Grueby's agent in Europe was Siegfried Bing, through whom the Victoria and Albert Museum and the Museum of Decorative Art in Copenhagen purchased examples around 1900.
Additional text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A Catalogue of the British Museum Collection'. 2nd ed. 1994. Addenda.
For an example of this model fitted with a Tiffany leaded-glass shade, see Christie's, New York, 8 December 1990, lot 186.

Acq notes (acq):
In February 1882 Walter K Foster gave to the BM two groups of material from his own collection. The first was of British archaeology, which was registered as 1882,0206.1 to 36. The second was of Peruvian pottery. This was registered at the time on slips written by C H Read with a special 'Foster' numbering, running from 1 to 44; the descriptions are accompanied by excellent drawings. 
After some confusion with the archaeology series, this gift was reclassified (bizarrely) on the database as Am1930,Foster.1 to 44. There is no rationale whatsoever for the 1930 date, and there are no relevant entries in the register for 1930. Foster seems never to have visited Peru, and the collection was presumably purchased in England in or before 1882.

Description: Vase; mould-blown glass, amber flared rectangular shape with 'skyscraper' outline.;
Producer name: Designed by: George Sakier;  Factory of: Fostoria Glass Company
Curators Comments:
For further information, see also 1988,0107.1
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no. 268.
Sakier trained as an engineer at Columbia University and worked as a commercial illustrator, as art director for Harper's Bazaar and as an interior decorator. During the 1920s he directed the Bureau of Design for the American Radiator and Sanitation Company, specialising in luxury bathroom accessories and fittings; see The Studio 105, London February 1933, 120; see also vol. 123, March 1942, 66-9, for an account of Sakier's work as interior decorator.      Sakier was hired by Fostoria in the late 1920s to design inexpensive glassware for large-scale production. Among his early designs were stemwares with square-shaped ebony-glass bases (The Studio Yearbook, 1931, 149), and a square-shaped dinner-ware service of 1930 known as 'Mayfair' pattern (H.M. Weatherman, 'Fostoria: Its First Fifty Years', Springfield 1972,225).
     In his own article written in 1933 and entitled 'Primer of Modern Design' (Arts and Decoration 40, New York November 1933,36-7), Sakier illustrated his bathroom fittings as 'functional modern' and his glassware for Fostoria under the headings 'geometric modern', 'classic modern' and 'romantic modern'. The latter included a lobed bowl while 'classic modern' included a series of cylindrical fluted vases (see also S. & M.C. Cheney, 'Art and the Machine: an Account of Industrial Design in 20th Century America', New York 1936, 221; Newhaven 1983, Yale University Art Gallery, 'At Home in Manhatten. Modern Decorative Arts. 1925 to the Depression',  K. Davies, no. 81; Brooklyn 1986, The Brooklyn Museum, 'The Machine Age in America', pl. 8.80). For a hexafoil lobed vase similar to that catalogued here, but with straight instead of curved sides, see P. McGrain ed., 'Fostoria, The Popular Years', Frederick, Maryland 1982, 81. For the skyscraper vase, see M. Greif, 'Depression Modern: The Thirties Style in America', New York 1975, 181.
     Sakier's glassware for Fostoria was included in the Industrial Arts Exposition of 1934 held at the Rockefeller Plaza, New York, cat. nos 459-66. See also The Studio Yearbook, 1933, 104 and 110, for illustrations of candlesticks and bowls.
     The vases were produced in several colours including amber, rose, green, silver mist and ebony. Green and amber were both discontinued in 1941. Some of Sakier's vase designs remained in production after the war. For Fostoria, see L.D. Shelley, 'Modern Fine Glass', New York 1937, Weatherman 1972 and McGrain 1982.

Description: Vase, human-shaped vessel made of alabaster.;
Curators Comments:
From acquisition "tomb".; McEwan 2009, p.9 
Alabaster vessel with figure of a crouching warrior.  
Veracruz (Isla de Sacrificios), Mexico, AD 900-1521
This vessel was probably a revered personal possession invested with the power and prestige of its owner - perhaps the skilfully sculpted warrior himself. It was taken as an offering during a long-distance pilgrimage to the island sanctuary of Sacrificios, just south of Veracruz, where a cult to Quetzalcoatl was celebrated in pre-Columbian times.
Bib references: McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail

Description: Vase; earthenware, cast, yellow body with matt blue glaze over-sprayed with green, the base also glazed; the angular line of the handles is continued in relief down the body of the vase.;
Producer name: Designed by: Artus Van Briggle;  Factory of: Van Briggle Pottery Co
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: VAN BRIGGLE Inscription note: Impressed, beneath conjoined 'A's in a rectangle.; Inscription type: mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: 1902 Inscription note: Date made.; Inscription type: mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: 30 Inscription note: Design number, impressed.; Inscription type: mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: III Inscription note: Impressed.;
Curators Comments:
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. no. 289
The Van Briggle Pottery was established in 1901 by Artus Van Briggle (1869-1904), who had been forced by tuberculosis to settle in Colorado in 1899. He had previously worked as a decorator at the Rookwood Pottery in Cincinnati (see Decorative Arts 1850-1950, Cat. 260) since 1887. From 1893 to 1896 he studied drawing and painting at the Académie Julian in Paris, during which time he became influenced by the matt glazes on oriental ceramics as well as the sculpture of Rodin and the work of the French artist potters. Van Briggle's first matt-glazed wares were made at Rookwood in 1898 and were highly praised at the Paris Exhibition of 1900. After Van Briggle's death in 1904 the Pottery was run by his wife until 1912 and is still active.
     A vase of this shape was illustrated in The Craftsman iv, Eastwood, New York, September 1903, opp. p. 414, and exhibited at the St Louis Exhibition of 1904 (Gazette Telegraph, 1 January 1905, reproduced in S.H. Nelson et al., 'A Collector's Guide to Van Briggle Pottery', Indiana 1986, 28). The vase also appears in a photograph of Van Briggle in his pottery of c.1902 (D. Bogue, 'The Van Briggle Story', 16 and Nelson, 16) and was illustrated again in The House Beautiful xv, February 1904, 137.
     The monogram 'AA' was adopted in 1900 by Artus and his fiancee and assistant Anne Gregory, whom he married in 1902. The Roman numeral 'III' indicates the type of clay and the design number '30' was created in 1901. For further discussion of the marks on Van Briggle wares and a catalogue of Van Briggle designs, see Nelson 1986.
     Van Briggle's glazes were applied with an atomiser, a second colour often sprayed over the first, as on this vase. According to a contemporary account, the dead glaze was 'a "fat" solution of glass, which, when applied to the biscuit ware, undergoes a devitrification in the second firing, and the result is a finish which seems to possess the depth and softness of velvet ...' (Brush and Pencil ix, Chicago October 1901, 3-4). For further discussion of the methods of manufacture, see The Craftsman 1903, 415-25.
     For the influence of French artist potters on Van Briggle, see E. Eidelberg, 'American Ceramics and International Styles 1876-1916, Aspects of the Arts and Crafts Movement in America',  Record of the Art Museum of Princeton University, 1975. 34, 13-19.
Information supplementary to Rudoe 1994:
See also M. Collins, 'Towards Post-Modernism, Design since 1851', London, British Museum, revised ed. 1994, fig. 26.

Description: Vase; free-blown glass, in the form of a jack-in-the-pulpit flower; greenish-yellow and pink glass, with heavy base and hollow stem opening out into a flower of undulating glass with stretched edges; the surface is iridescent throughout giving a lustrous gold effect to the front, while the back is a more silver-green colour.;
Production place: Factory in: Corona;  Factory in: Long Island (New York State)
Dimensions: 11.90 centimetres base ;  49.30 centimetres;  26.60 centimetres of top ;
Inscription: Inscription type: maker's mark Inscription position: base Inscription quoted: L C Tiffany-Favrile 9257 G Inscription note: Etched; 'favrile' is a trade name derived from 'fabrile' meaning 'belonging to a craftsman or his craft'.;
Curators Comments:
For information about Tffany Glass see J. Rudoe, Decorative Arts 1850-1950, London 1991, cat. no. 284 (1980.1109.1).
Text from J. Rudoe, 'Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A catalogue of the British Museum collection'. 2nd ed. 1994, no. 285.
According to R. Koch, 'Tiffany's Glass, Bronzes, Lamps', New York 1971, 40, the jack-in-the-pulpit shape was introduced by Thomas Manderson, Tiffany's first master blower.
     For discussion of marks and dating, see Cat. 284. If Koch's dating system is reliable, this vase was made in 1912. Several jack-in-the-pulpit vases survive in a variety of colours and with a range of different numbers; see, for example, Christie's, New York, 10 June 1989, lot 244, numbered 5110K, with two variants, lots 248 and 250, numbered 9464A and 4045A.
     Jack-in-the-pulpit vases were also made by the Quezal Art Glass and Decorating Company (1901-25), set up as a rival concern by Martin Bach, a former employee of L. C. Tiffany. The Quezal shape is usually more squat and the bases are sometimes decorated
with feathering; see, for example, Sotheby's, New York, 16-17 March 1990, lots 523 and 525.
Information supplementary to Rudoe 1994:
Fo a closely similar vase in the Bayerisches Nationalmuseum with more recent bibliography, see M. Koch, 'Meisterwerke des Jugendstil im Bayerischen Nationalmuseum München', Stuttgart 2010, no. 109. 
See also A. Cooney Freylinghausen, 'Louis Comfort Tiffany at the Metropolitan Museum', New York 1998.
Bib references: Rudoe 1991a / Decorative Arts 1850-1950: A Catalogue of the British Museum Collection.; Tait 1991 / Five Thousand Years of Glass; Rudoe 1994 / Decorative Arts 1850-1950. A Catalogue of the British Museum Collection

Description: Flared bowl with fine line painting representation of a weaving scene comprised by female weavers with back strap looms.;
Curators Comments: McEwan 2009, p. 68 Weaving Scene, fine-line painting on a pottery vessel. Moche, Peru. 2nd-8th century This fine-line painting of a weaving scene arranged around the inner circumference of a large flared bowl shows female weavers with back strap looms surrounded by weaving implements such as spindles and spindle whorls. One end of each loom is suspended from the top of a vertical wall post. These posts support a thatched roof, suggesting that the artisans are gathered inside a special building housing a workshop or guild of weavers. Other elements in the background may be samples or finished products, and various forms of ceramic vessels are noted including the characteristic stirrup-spout and flared drinking beakers. Males in elaborate dress are engaged in other (unspecified) activities, perhaps in courtyards outside. This is one of very few scenes illustrating the actual production of objects.
Bib references: McEwan 2009 / Ancient American Art in Detail; Pardo and Cooper 2021 / Peru: A Journey In Time
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 6, Life's ordinary dangers)

Description: Pottery vessel with kené design;
Materials: pottery; resin (kaolin (maosh), ochre (mashinti), tree bark and copal resin were used in paints, slips and glazes)
Curators Comments:
The Shipibo-Konibo people have lived along the Ucayali River (modern-day Peru) for more than a millennium. Until the early 20th century, they were divided in two: the Shipibo occupied the middle Ucayali, and the Konibo its upper course. Increasing external pressures since the rubber boom led them to unite alongside the Xetebo people. Their recently created Indigenous Guard has been a turning point in the protection of their territory.
The geometric designs known as kené are an important expression of Shipibo-Konibo identity, representing elements of nature, their territory and worldview in pottery and textiles. Through mastering kené, women artists such as Sara Flores and Celia Vázquez Yui advocate for the protection of their territory and knowledge transmission. Made today by fewer and fewer Shipibo-Konibo women, traditional pottery is threatened by climate change and illegal logging, which limit access to essential forest materials. Yet this cultural practice endures, still relying on ancestral techniques: from the mixing of clay with grog (keng kesh) and ashes from tree bark (apacherama), to the seasonal procurement of kaolin (maosh), ochre (mashinti), tree bark and copal resin for use in paints, slips and glazes.
This object has been documented in collaboration with Shipibo-Konibo women potters* from the Ucayali Valley (Peruvian Amazonia) at the workshop ‘Ancestral Connections through Shipibo-Konibo pottery’ organised in November 2025 by the Shipibo Conibo Center and the Santo Domingo Centre of Excellence for Latin American Research at the Bakish Mai Multiversity (Pucallpa, Peru). *Shipibo-Konibo master ceramists and knowledge holders: Celia Vázquez Yui and daughter hija Diana Ruiz Vázquez, Judith Pereyra and daughter Rina Pereyra, Eusebia Picota Coribary and granddaughter Kimberly Pinedo, Elena González Baneo and daughter Janina Peso González, Lucía Vázquez and daughter Levania Puedo Romena Vázquez, and Lucilda Amacifuen Valera.; See "Hidden Peoples of the Amazon" for a general introduction to the area
: [Ed.] Carmichael, Elizabeth
The design on these pots, known as kené, represents and embodies a primordial anaconda. The anaconda is the life-force behind the waters, trees and animals that make up the Ucayali river system, the ancestral home of Shipibo-Konibo People. If this power were to die, all life in the forest would die with it. Only women produce kené. They dream about the designs and then bring them to life in artworks.
Location: On display (Room 24 - Living and Dying, Display Case 4, Relating to animals)
Acq name (excavator): Field Collection by: Frederick J Stevenson
Acq notes (acq): Am.8843 to 8947 is a collection formed by Fredrick J Stevenson among the Conibo and Cocama Indians on the river Ucayali, a tributary of the Upper Amazon in the area of Peru, given by him to the BM on 18 July 1873.

Description: Fruit-shaped vessel with lid (Am1910,1202.7.b) made in tumbaga by lost-wax casting with core. Its form depicts a flattened 'totuma', the fruit of the gourd tree (Crescentia cujete) employed in Central and South America to make vessels and other artefacts. Two rectangular holes in opposite sides near the edge might have been used to carry it from a cord.;
Curators Comments: It was probably used to carry coca leaves holding it from a cord tied to two opposite rectangular holes placed towards the edge. The flat form would have allowed the vessel to be carried comfortably.

Description: Vessel; made of pottery; vessel of red ware partially painted white in the form of head with open mouth showing large canine teeth, wearing head-dress with small face at centre.;
Location: On display (Room 1 - Enlightenment, Art & Civilization, Wall Press 40)
Acq notes (acq):
In February 1882 Walter K Foster gave to the BM two groups of material from his own collection. The first was of British archaeology, which was registered as 1882,0206.1 to 36. The second was of Peruvian pottery. This was registered at the time on slips written by C H Read with a special 'Foster' numbering, running from 1 to 44; the descriptions are accompanied by excellent drawings. 
After some confusion with the archaeology series, this gift was reclassified (bizarrely) on the database as Am1930,Foster.1 to 44. There is no rationale whatsoever for the 1930 date, and there are no relevant entries in the register for 1930. Foster seems never to have visited Peru, and the collection was presumably purchased in England in or before 1882.
