Description:
Watch.
3/4-plate; going barrel; gold train wheels, keyless winding and hand-setting.
Club-tooth lever escapement, gold escape wheel.
White enamel dial, hours I-12, minutes 5-60
Blued-steel hands.
Aluminium case specially made in 1939.
Rock-crystal back-plate and balance cock.
Front-plate punched 40.;
Producer name: Made by: American Waltham Watch Company;  Case made by: W Bensley;  Movement made by: William R Wills
Materials: aluminium (watch-case); brass (movement); quartz (movement plate); steel (pinions etc.)
Inscription: Inscription type: signature Inscription position: dial Inscription quoted: A.W.W.Co.Waltham No.27 (movement), A.W. Co. WALTHAM.; Inscription type: mark Inscription position: plate Inscription quoted: No.27;
Curators Comments:
For a discussion and description of this type of watch see National Association of Watch and Clock Collectors Inc.  Supplement 14 to Bulletin Spring 1984, M.C. Harrold, "American Watchmaking 1850-1930", p.103; Comment from Richard Good, Catalogue of Watches in the British Museum. Vol. V (Unpublished manuscript)
Made by American Waltham Watch Co.
Waltham, Massachusetts, USA, c. 1890
Watch with crystal back plate and balance cock and with club tooth lever escapement.
Signature: "A.W.W. Co WALTHAM" on the barrel "A.W. Co WALTHAM" on the dial. No 27 on the front plate on the inside, visible from the back.
Case: Aluminium replacement case glazed back and front. 
Dial and Hands: Dial with Arabic numerals in black, the minutes in red numbered every five.
Movement:
Ebauche Marks: 40
Frame: ¾ plate layout. The back plate and cock in rock crystal, the back plate supported by three turned pillars and retained by counter-sunk screws. The cock steady pins are in the front plate, the pallet cock is in gold. The front plate is damascened.
Barrel and Mainspring: Both the going barrel and its cover stamped 27. Barrel I. diam 12.0 mm, h. 1.95 mm.  
Mainspring: h.1.74 mm, t. 0.17 mm.  
Arbor: diam. 3.8 mm. snailed.  
Hooking: T piece.
Train: Wheels of gold with five crossings the escape wheel with four.
Jewelled: From the centre onwards in the crystal back plate, the jewels pressed in. In the balance cock the jewel with its endstone is pressed into a gold chaton and cannot be separated. Jewelled in the front plate from the third onwards, these jewels rubbed in. The balance jewels in the front plate in screwed settings.
Escapement: Club tooth lever, straight line layout, short lever, single roller, impulse pin D shaped, an escapement.  Pallet stones convex on the impulse faces but flat on the locking faces. Escape wheel of gold. Adjustable banking pins. Pallet frame of steel, but gold plated on its top surface. 
Number of teeth embraced 3½.
Balance and Spring: A split bimetallic balance with gold compensation and quarter screws. Balance diameter 12.0 mm., thickness 1.0 mm. Blued-steel flat spiral spring with 12½ turns and terminal curve.
Means of Regulation: Free sprung, hence the quarter screws on the balance have to be used.
Train Counts and Beat Rate:
Great wheel (barrel) 75
Centre wheel 75  pinion  10 (A safety pinion)
Third wheel 60  pinion   9
Fourth wheel 60  pinion   9
Escape wheel 15  pinion   7
Beat Rate: 17,142.856 
Motion work: cannon pinion 12
Minute wheel 36, minute pinion 10
hour wheel 40								
Winding System: Positive set.
Dimensions: Case diameter 38.5 mm., height 12.4 mm.
Movement: diameter 33.0 mm., height 7.1 mm.
Provenance: Formerly in the Ilbert collection. Purchased from Waltham 22/1/3939. Aluminium case made by Bensley.
Related works:  
Two of these crystal plate watches are in the collections of the Time Museum, Rockford, Illinois, USA. They are numbered 17 and 21. See Hoke 'Catalogue of American Watches' pp. 130, 131. It is interesting that there are two different sizes, cat No 52 is the same size as this example, the other, cat 51 is larger, the movement being 41.5 mm. in diameter.
Notes:
1. This must be the earliest example of pressed-in jewels, a tour-de-force in rock crystal plates.
2. There is another of these watches in the collections of the Smithsonian Museum Philadelphia USA. It is number 21.
Bib references: Watches Vol V / Catalogue of Watches in the British Museum, Vol. V The Lever Watch

Description:
GOLD CASED KEYLESS LEVER POCKET WATCH

CASE: 18ct gold open-face case with an inscription on the inner dome and a monogram on the back.

DIAL: White enamel dial with Roman hours I-XII and sunk subsidiary seconds dial. Blued-steel spade-pattern hands. The maker's name and serial number inscribed in the upper half.

MOVEMENT: 3/4 plate movement with gilt-brass plates and ringed pillars. The back plate has a raised are to provide for a large going barrel. Four wheel train. Ratchet-tooth lever escapement with split bimetallic compensation balance and free sprung spiral balance spring with terminal curve.

BOX: A red leather covered wooden box with silk lining and gold embossed inscriptions.;
Producer name: Made by: Barraud & Lunds;  Case made by: Joseph Walton
Dimensions: 49.00 millimetres watch ;  125.70 millimetres box ;  13.50 millimetres;  38.00 millimetres;  110.50 millimetres box ;
Inscription:
Inscription type: signature Inscription position: back-plate Inscription quoted: Barraud & Lunds
14 Bishopsgate Without
LONDON
No.3/4702; Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: on case dome Inscription quoted: Presented to

in recognition of the very exceptional

 3
 4702; Inscription type: monogram Inscription position: on case back Inscription quoted: ACS; Inscription type: inscription Inscription position: outside box lid and inside box lid Inscription quoted: BARRAUD & LUNDS
14 Bishopsgate St.
LONDON Inscription note: Around and within a gold embossed garter;
Curators Comments:
Text from 'Watches', by David Thompson, London, 2008, p. 134-135.
Barraud & Lunds
GOLD CASED LEVER WATCH 
LONDON, 1909
SIGNED: 'Barraud & Lunds, 14 Bishopsgate Within, LONDON. No. 3/4702' (on the movement)
In 1909 the Pelican, a supply ship owned by the Hudson's Bay Company, set sail from London bound for Labrador and Quebec in order to establish a new trading post at Wolstenholme at Eric Cove on the northwest point of the Ungava Peninsula. An account of the voyage and captivating photographs of the Inuit make up part of the personal diary and photo album of an officer on board the ship. Following the British Museum's acquisition of the journal and album, the account and photographs were published by Jonathan King in 'Imaging the Arctic'. The diary and album are thought to be those of either C.M. Cato, an officer on board the Pelican, or, perhaps more likely, the ship's commander, Captain Arthur Smith.
In 2000 the Museum purchased a gold watch that had been presented to Smith in recognition of his actions during difficulties encountered on the return voyage from the Arctic in the autumn of 1909. As Smith or Cato recorded on 8 August ofthat year, "our propellor got caught & had every blade but one stripped off, so we had to finish our journey with one blade". King further explains that: "the Pelican also lost its rudder on 24 August and a replacement was smashed on 26 September. The voyage was further complicated by the need to rescue the Paradox, a steam ketch from Ipswich, with a crew of walrus hunters, which had been badly nipped by the ice and was in a sinking condition."
The gold cased lever watch, given to Captain Smith by the underwriters rather than the Hudson's Bay Company itself, is signed on the movement 'Barraud & Lunds, 14 Bishopsgate Within LONDON No. 3/4702'.The standard white enamel dial has the hours I-XII and a sunk subsidiary seconds dial below the centre. The time is shown by blued-steel spade-pattern hands and the maker's name and serial number are inscribed in the upper half. On the underside of the dial are the letters 'O L', fired into it in blue. The 18-carat-gold case is hallmarked 1909 and bears the maker's mark - 'JW' in an oval cameo - of Joseph Walton & Company, which was trading in Upper Charles Street, Clerkenwell, when this case was made. Joseph Walton was born in Coventry around 1834. Following the death of his father he was sent to London to be looked after by his guardian, James Thickbroom, a flourishing Clerkenwell watch-case maker. From his guardian Walton learned the art of watch-case making, and later took over Thickbroom's business.
The insurers clearly did not feel the need to splash out on a fully jewelled free-sprung keyless fusee watch. However, it has a reasonable quality ¾-plate movement with gilt-brass plates and ringed pillars. There is a raised area on the back plate to provide space for a large going barrel, which drives a four-wheel gear train, and there are jewelled bearings for the fourth wheel, escape wheel and balance, the last two with end-stones. The ratchet-tooth lever escapement has jewelled pallet stones and is controlled by a free-sprung, split bimetallic balance with gold timing and compensation screws, and the balance spring has a terminal curve. The dial side of the front plate is punched with the serial number of the watch, along with the numbers '140 2' and the initials 'JP', showing it to be an ébauche supplied by Joseph Preston (see registration 1958,1201.1459). The watch is pendant-wound with rocking-bar hand-setting, operated by a push-piece in the case band at 1 o'clock.
In order to record the underwriters' gratitude, the watch is inscribed to Captain Smith on the inner case dome:
"Presented to Captain Arthur C. Smith by the Underwriters in recognition of the very exceptional seamanship displayed by him on board The Pelican(s) During his Autumn voyage from Hudson's Bay 1909"
The engraved monogram 'ACS' on the outside of the case back gives an additional indication of the owner. The watch is still contained in its original, now rather worn, red leather-covered wooden box, with silk lining and the gold embossed inscription 'BARRAUD & LUNDS, 14 Bishopsgate St. LONDON' around and within a gold embossed garter.
The underwriters must have felt highly indebted to Smith. His ability as a seaman enabled him to bring the Pelican safely to port with no rudder and a seriously damaged propeller, which must have almost shaken the ship to pieces as it rotated completely out of balance. His skill and bravery not only brought the ship home in one piece, but also saved its crew and cargo from the perils of the freezing Arctic seas.

Curators Comments:
Text from 'Watches', by David Thompson, London, 2008, p. 158-159.
Longines-Wittnauer
STAINLESS-STEEL WRISTWATCH 
USA, 1974
SIGNED: 'Wittnauer' (on the dial) and 'Polara' (on the bracelet clip)
The first models of quartz watch, such as the Omega Megaquartz, had analogue dials with conventional hour, minute and seconds hands advanced by miniature electric motors. At the same time, however, a new form of watch with no moving parts was being developed. Following hard on the heels of the launch of the first Japanese quartz-controlled wristwatch, which had a traditional analogue dial, the Hamilton Watch Company presented their new electronic watch, the Pulsar, to the press on 6 May 1970. At the time there were only three working examples of this watch, but just one year later a limited edition gold cased watch was made available in the shops, and major production began.
This watch displayed the time in an illuminated 24-hour digital form. The new technology was based around the use of a light-emitting diode, a semi-conductor that emits light on passage of an electric current. The phenomenon was first recognized by Captain Henry Joseph Round, who worked for Marconi in New York. Round published his findings in the journal 'Electrical World' in February 1907, recording in a letter to the editor that: "During an investigation of the unsymmetrical passage of current through a contact of carborundum and other substances a curious phenomenon was noted. On applying a potential of 10 volts between two points on a crystal of carborundum, the crystal gave out a yellowish light." Another pioneer in the field was Oleg Vladimirovich Losev, who published his research in 1927. 
Although work continued, it was not until 1962 that the first practical LED was produced by Nick Holonyak Jr, working for the General Electric Company in America. In watches, an LED display consists of four miniature panels, each usually with seven separate sections, each section containing a number of tiny light-emitting diodes. The electronics then switch electrical currents through different patterns of the sections to produce the numerical display. The major drawback of the LED system was its need for relatively large amounts of electrical power to illuminate the diodes. Many watches using the technology had to be provided with two batteries, which needed relatively frequent renewal.
As a result of this shortcoming, the LED display was soon replaced by watches with liquid crystal displays, a technology first documented in 1904 by the German scientist Otto Lehmann and developed to a unit patented in 1936 by Marconi. Despite this early date, a practical liquid crystal display was not made until the late 1960s. This complex combination of molecular science, electronics and optics has now been developed to a point where production is cheap and carried out on a massive global scale, with huge numbers of applications. In watches it very quickly replaced the light-emitting diode, as it needed far less electrical power. The introduction of this new technology and improvements in the longevity of batteries means that watches now run for a number of years before requiring a battery replacement.
The firm Wittnauer was founded by Albert Wittnauer, who emigrated from Switzerland to America in 1872. With his brothers Louis and Emile he established a business that imported Swiss-made watches. In later years, following a merger, the company became Longines-Wittnauer. That Wittnauer was keen to move into the new market is well demonstrated by its early marketing of this LED quartz watch. It uses a unit made by the Hughes Aircraft Corporation, responsible for very large production in America of quartz watch 'movements'. The smoked glass is dark until buttons in the case band are pushed in, either singly or in combination, to illuminate the dial to show either the time in hours and minutes or seconds, or the date.
